title: La Morte D'Arthur
author: Rob McArthur
description: An elegiac retelling of the Arthurian age — from Camelot's golden morning through the Grail's terrible light to the ruin of the Round Table.

entries.body:
The age of Arthur is new, and the world holds its breath.

From the white towers of Camelot the knights ride out like spokes from a wheel's bright hub. The king has drawn Excalibur from its stone and proved his right before all of Britain, yet the proving was only the beginning — there are warlords still unbroken in the north, enchantments old as the standing stones, and a fellowship only now learning what it means to be worthy of itself. Merlin walks the corridors yet, his counsel given in riddles the young king must learn to unravel.

Go forth, then. The roads of Britain lie open, the summer is long, and glory, like a quarry just sighted, is always a little ahead of you.

A vision passed through the hall of Camelot like a wind through an open door.

The Grail — that vessel of impossible light — appeared at the high table and vanished before any knight could move, and in its wake the air tasted different, charged with a longing no tournament prize could satisfy. The vows were made before the king could counsel otherwise: one hundred and fifty knights sworn to ride until they had seen the Grail face to face. Arthur wept. He knew what the going meant.

The roads of Britain are stranger now. Every crossroads holds a hermit with hard words; every forest conceals a test the blade cannot pass. Ride carefully. What you seek may be closer than you know, or further than you can travel.

The wound that was always there has opened at last.

Mordred sits in Arthur's seat and calls it regency; his messengers ride through every hall in Britain demanding oaths. Lancelot fights his war across the water, his grief a continent between himself and his king. The Round Table is not broken — it is dispersed, its fellowship stretched until the threads snap one by one. Those who ride now do so through a land that has begun to forget it was ever whole.

This is the age of last things. Ride with what honour you can keep, spend it where it matters, and remember: even in ruin, a knight may do some deed worth the telling.

The game is ended, and the age has passed.

Arthur is gone to Avalon — or so the stories say, for no man saw him laid in earth and the barge was out of sight before the mist closed behind it. The Round Table stands empty in a hall that fewer and fewer remember. The knights who remain have scattered: some to monasteries, some to exile, some to quiet lives in corners of Britain that the wars did not reach. They do not speak much of what they saw.

But the stories are already changing in the telling. What happened becomes what should have happened, then what must have happened, and at last what is remembered as truth. You have been part of those stories. Whatever you did in the three ages — the glory and the failure, the kept oaths and the broken ones — it is woven into the legend now.

Let it be worthy of the weaving.

Upon the old road through the oak-wood you come upon a king walking beside his horse, leading the beast by the bridle as though he has forgotten how to ride with purpose. His tabard is faded to the colour of old rust, and his beard has grown past any lord's keeping, but his spine is straight and his eyes are clear. He gives his name when you ask — a border lord whose hall lies seven days north-west — and his tale is quickly told: a treacherous cousin, a garrison turned, a gate closed against him in the night.

He is riding south to petition Arthur. He has been riding three weeks and has another seven days yet before him. He does not ask for company, or pity, or a sword. He is simply on the road, as a man must be when there is nothing else left.

responses.label: You may ride with him to Camelot and speak on his behalf before the king.
You may offer what counsel you have and send him forward better-armed in thought.

entries.body: Three days to Camelot, and then the waiting, which is its own test. When at last the lord's petition is heard, it is you who must frame it — the king's patience is not inexhaustible, and a border grievance is small beside the business of Britain. The displaced lord stands beside you, and something in his bearing asks to be believed.

resolutions.label: argue his cause with dignity before the court

success.body:
The king listens, and then he acts. A writ is given; knights are dispatched. The border lord rides back north with the law behind him, and as he goes he looks at you once over his shoulder — a look that is neither thanks nor relief but acknowledgment, man to man.

That look will be remembered.

failure.body:
Arthur hears you out, but his councillors have other matters pressing, and the writ is delayed. The border lord understands — he is too old to pretend otherwise — but the understanding sits heavily on him. You have done what you could, and it was not enough.

There is a kind of teaching in that.

entries.body: You sit beside the road with the dispossessed lord and listen — that is the greater part of counsel, as the wise know. His grievance is real, his plan is rough, his hope is thin. When you speak, you must be honest without being cruel, for there is a line between clarity and the boot on a man's neck.

resolutions.label: counsel him with honesty on his best course

success.body: The words come clear. You advise him to arrive at Camelot not as a petitioner but as a vassal reporting treason against the realm — for that is what it is — and to name the cousin's men rather than his own loss. He nods, and when he rides on there is purpose in his back that was not there before.

failure.body:
Your counsel, though honestly meant, comes out too plain or too vague, and the lord thanks you with the politeness of a man who has made up his own mind. Perhaps it was the right mind. You do not know how the road ends for him.

You ride on the wiser for the attempt.

entries.body:
He sits his horse in the centre of the road, and the horse is patient with him in a way that animals are patient with grief. He is past the age when sorrow passes quickly, and his eyes have the flatness of one who has wept past tears. His escort rides at a distance, uncertain whether to approach.

His son, a page tells you quietly, fell at a river ford three weeks past. A skirmish of no great account. The king had not yet sent for the body.

responses.label: You may ride closer and sit with him in silence a while.
You may speak to him of his son's honour — what you have heard, or what you believe.

entries.body: You walk your horse slowly forward until you are beside him, and you say nothing. There is a ministry in presence alone — the acknowledgment that grief requires witness before it requires remedy. The king does not look at you, but he does not ride away either.

resolutions.label: remain with him in quiet witness

success.body:
After a long time — it is hard to say how long — the king draws a slow breath and straightens. He looks at you at last, and whatever he sees seems to satisfy him. He says: 'He was a good boy. Not a great one. But good.' Then he rides on.

You did nothing, which was the everything required.

failure.body:
The silence holds too long, and against your will you grope for words, some small offering, and in the reaching you lose the quality that drew you forward. The king glances at you with something that is not quite reproach and rides away.

You have learned something about stillness that you did not know before.

entries.body: To speak to a father of a fallen son is to walk on ground that may hold your weight or may not. You know some scraps of the boy's reputation — a tourney well-ridden, a moment of courage at a lord's gate — and you must weave these into something true enough to bear the king's grief, for he will know flattery from fact.

resolutions.label: speak of his son's deeds with care and plain honesty

success.body:
You find the right story — a small one, but real — and you tell it as one knight might remember another. The king listens with his whole body, the way a starving man listens to the sound of food. When you are done, he asks you to tell it again. You do.

His hand grips your arm once before he rides on. That is enough.

failure.body:
Your words are well-meant but they do not quite reach him — there is a specificity of truth required that you cannot fake, and he knows it. He listens to the end, then thanks you, and the thanks is the kind that closes a conversation.

Honour requires honesty, and honesty requires knowing what you do not know.

entries.body:
He stops you on the road with a raised hand and a smile of such openness that your own hand does not go to your sword. He is a king of the middle lands, neither great nor small, and he has just come from three days with a hermit in the high hills — a man of great age whose counsel, he believes, has settled something long restless in him.

He asks if you are on pilgrimage. Few knights are, in this age, but the question does not seem idle. He looks at you as though the answer will tell him something about the state of the world.

responses.label: You may speak with him of matters of faith and receive his blessing.
You may ask him the hermit's name and seek out the man yourself.

entries.body: He does not speak as a scholar of theology, but there is a plain quality to his words about his time with the hermit that is harder to dismiss than learned argument. He asks you a question, then another — the kind that have no easy answer and are better for it.

resolutions.label: engage him honestly in matters of faith and purpose

success.body:
The conversation ends — as good ones do — only when there is no more to say. The king places his hand on your head and speaks a blessing in old words, and you ride away not converted to anything in particular but carrying a clarity you did not have before.

The road seems lighter for an hour.

failure.body:
You reach for words that will not come, and those that come are too small for what he is asking. The conversation turns to pleasantries rather than truth. The king does not seem disappointed — he has had many such conversations — but the blessing he offers is brief and dutiful.

Some doors open only once.

entries.body: He gives you the direction — not the hermit's name, which the man does not tell, but the name of the hill and a description of seven white stones set at intervals in the turf. The seeking will take you a day off your road through trackless terrain. Finding a man who does not advertise himself is its own kind of test.

resolutions.label: find your way through the wilderness to the hermit's place

success.body:
The markers are where the king described, and you find the hermit — a man so old he seems part of the hill itself, his eyes still sharp as winter glass. He speaks with you for three hours and says nothing you can repeat, and nothing you can forget.

The road back is the same road. You are not the same traveller.

failure.body:
You find the first three markers and then a mist comes down and the fourth is not where it should be. You search until the light fails, then make camp on the hillside. The hermit, if he is there, does not reveal himself.

There are things that cannot be found by wanting them hard enough.

entries.body:
He wanders in the forest without memory of his name. His arms are fine — a lord's arms, well-kept — but his eyes have the quality of a man dreaming while awake, and he asks who you are with a child's directness, as though your answer might restore something he has lost. His horse follows him like a dog.

Something binds him here. You can feel it as you feel a change in weather before the clouds arrive — an enchantment of forgetting, laid by a hand with power enough to unravel a man's self. The question is whether it can be undone, and by what means.

responses.label: You may attempt to break the enchantment through prayer and force of will.
You may speak to him of himself — his hall, his name, his people — and try to restore him through memory.

entries.body: You dismount and face the king, and you speak the old words of exorcism — whatever you know of them — while holding his gaze. The enchantment resists. You can feel it as a cold intelligence that does not wish to be undone. Your faith must be stronger than the will that laid this working.

resolutions.label: drive out the enchantment through prayer and conviction

success.body:
The working cracks. Not all at once — there is a moment of violent confusion, the king crying out and clutching your arm — but then the light comes back into his eyes, and with it his name, his family, the long thread of himself. He looks at you with an expression no language has a word for: the face of a man restored.

He will owe you a debt he cannot repay. That is the heaviest kind.

failure.body:
The enchantment holds. Your prayer beats against it like water against stone, and you pull back before the working begins to test you in turn — for you can feel its edges reaching. The king watches you with his blank dreaming eyes.

You cannot break this alone. You can only mark the place and carry the knowledge forward.

entries.body: You sit beside him on the forest floor and begin to speak: the name of his hall, the colour of his banner, the name of his eldest son — details gathered from the saddle-cloth and the rings on his fingers, a man assembled from evidence. Whether the pieces fit the mind that once carried them, you cannot know until you try.

resolutions.label: reconstruct his identity from all you can observe and deduce

success.body:
Something in the list of names catches. He repeats one back to you — his wife's name — and the enchantment stutters. You press forward, naming more, and gradually the dreaming recedes. He is weeping before he is fully himself, which is perhaps the most human thing about the process.

When he speaks again it is with his own voice, and the first thing he asks is how long he has been gone.

failure.body:
You guess wrong at a crucial name — the wrong child, or the wrong keep — and the enchantment seems to thicken in the confusion. He looks at you with sad bewilderment, as though you have confirmed something about the world he had hoped was untrue.

You cannot rebuild a man from the outside if the inside does not meet you halfway.

entries.body:
He is younger than you expected — a king can be young, though we forget it — and he has the distracted air of a man whose mind is not where his body stands. His court is visible a quarter-mile behind him; they have learned to give him distance when the mood is on him. The mood being, as his steward will tell you quietly, a woman.

She is Irish, the steward says. Sent as hostage from a western chieftain, though she rides and feasts and speaks her mind freely. The king has failed to notice that a woman who smiles for political reasons is not a woman smiling for joy.

responses.label: You may counsel the king on how a lord ought to conduct himself with a guest in his protection.
* You may seek out the Irish lady and discover her true disposition.

entries.body: The king is not a villain — he is simply young and infatuated, which amounts to much the same mischief if left unchecked. He listens to you with the half-attention of a man who would rather be elsewhere. You must decide whether to be gentle or direct, for one may slide off him like water and the other may strike somewhere useful.

resolutions.label: make plain to him the nature of his obligation to a guest

success.body:
He goes quiet, which is better than argument. You see the moment it reaches him — one thing you said that landed differently from the rest — and he looks away, embarrassed in the way young men are when they recognise their own appetite reflected back at them.

He will not be grateful. But he will behave better.

failure.body:
He thanks you for your concern and walks back toward his court, and you suspect nothing has changed except that he now knows you have noticed. The infatuation will run its course — for good or ill — without your help.

There are men you cannot counsel, only outlast.

entries.body: She is easy enough to find — she keeps her own company in the eastern garden, and her escort steps back when you approach with courtesy. She is sharp-eyed and direct, and she will say what she thinks if she believes the asking is honest. The question is whether you are the kind of knight she trusts on sight, or the kind she does not.

resolutions.label: win her confidence and hear her true mind on her situation

success.body:
She speaks plainly once she has decided you are worth the plainness. She does not hate the king, she says, but she does not wish to marry him, and she would prefer to return to her father in spring. She asks if you will carry that message. You say you will.

Carrying it will cost you something. Keeping the promise will cost more. But it was given.

failure.body:
She studies you and decides — not unfairly — that you are not quite trustworthy enough for honest speech. She answers in the language of the court, smooth and uninformative. You ride away knowing nothing you did not know before.

Trust cannot be demanded. It can only be earned, and you have not yet paid the price.

entries.body:
He stands in the road with his sword drawn, though there is no enemy, and he challenges you in the name of a kingdom that has not existed for twenty years. He was the king of that country — that much you can piece together from the device on his sword, the badge on his servant's shoulder. What made him as he is, no one in the small group following at a careful distance will say.

He does not seem violent, only lost. But a drawn sword in the hand of a man who does not know where he is can be its own kind of danger.

responses.label: You may stand your ground and answer his challenge in the old forms, as a true knight would.
You may try to calm him with patience rather than ceremony, speaking of the morning itself.

entries.body: You dismount and acknowledge the challenge as custom requires — name, lineage, the cause for which you travel — and you wait. The ritual seems to reach some layer of the king that the madness has not fully claimed. His sword wavers.

resolutions.label: meet his challenge with the full dignity of knightly form

success.body:
The ceremony steadies him. He sheathes his sword, not because he understands but because the forms are older than reason and something in him answers them still. He speaks then of his kingdom — as it was, not as it is — and you listen, and in listening you give him back, briefly, a country.

He will not remember this tomorrow. Today it was real.

failure.body:
The forms are not enough. He stares through the words as though you speak a different language, and his sword remains drawn, and at last you must simply stand aside as his attendants ease him off the road.

Some things no ceremony can reach. But the reaching was not wasted.

entries.body: You speak to him quietly, the way you would speak to a startled horse — not about the challenge he issued, but about the morning itself: the light through the trees, the sound of the river nearby. The sword stays drawn, but you are not afraid of it. You have the calm of a man who has decided this is not his time today.

resolutions.label: settle him with gentleness and patient speech

success.body:
He blinks, and then again. The sword lowers by degrees, as a man sets down a burden he had forgotten he was carrying. His servants are weeping quietly at the road's edge, whether from relief or sorrow you cannot tell.

You stay with him until he sleeps, sitting against a tree, and his face in sleep is the face of a man at peace.

failure.body:
He tires of your calm before you tire of his storm, and he walks away into the wood with his sword still drawn, his servants hurrying after. You let him go — there was no choice in it — and you stand in the road feeling the absence of the thing you could not do.

Patience is learned, not given. You have grown a little.

entries.body:
He does not pretend to be anything other than what he is. His army is camped a half-mile away, he tells you, and he is riding to burn a particular hall to its foundations — because the lord of that hall murdered his brother at a feast three years past, stabbed him under the table in the middle of a toast, which is as dishonourable a method as the world offers.

He is not asking your permission. But he stops when he sees you, because something in your bearing makes him stop, and now he waits — in his cold way — to hear what you have to say.

responses.label: You may attempt to turn him from his vengeance toward justice under Arthur's law.
You may ask to hear the full account before you speak, and then give your honest judgment.

entries.body: He does not want to be turned. But something in him is listening — the part that knows vengeance and justice are not the same thing, that has known it for three years and chosen not to examine the knowledge too closely. You must reach that part before the army breaks camp.

resolutions.label: invoke the king's law and make the case for Arthur's justice

success.body:
He hears you to the end, and then there is a long silence. When he speaks again, he says that he wants a trial under Arthur's law — the murderer in chains, the verdict public. It is not what he wanted an hour ago. But it is something a man can live with.

You have not made a friend. But you have made a man act better than his grief.

failure.body:
He has heard this argument before — from priests, from his own counsellors — and your version finds no new angle of entry. He thanks you for the speech and rides back to his army.

Some wounds are too fresh for law. You hope the hall is empty of innocents.

entries.body: He tells it plainly, as a man recounts a story rehearsed a thousand times: the feast, the toast, the knife, his brother dead before he hit the floor, and the murderer fled north before morning. That man still lives. Still holds his hall. That is the wound that festers — not the death, but the continuing impunity.

resolutions.label: deliver your honest judgment after hearing the full account

success.body:
Your judgment is clear: the killing was dishonour, the flight was cowardice, and the continued impunity is an affront to the realm. Arthur should know it. You will carry the petition yourself if he will wait.

He looks at you for a long time. Then he turns his horse back toward his camp. You have not stopped the war — only delayed it. But delay, in the right circumstances, is everything.

failure.body:
Your judgment is honest but incomplete — you miss some element he would have granted weight, and he knows it. He listens, nods, and rides on. Whether your words have any effect on what comes next, you cannot know.

The truth is rarely welcome on first hearing. Keep saying it.

entries.body:
He has the eyes of a man who has seen everything twice. He is old — older than Arthur, older than the current age of Britain's quarrels — and he rules a small country in the west that no one has bothered to conquer, whether from oversight or wisdom. He sits in the shade of a great tree beside a well, and he is alone, and he watches you approach with the unhurried attention of someone who decided long ago that patience was the only virtue worth practising.

He has a question, he says. Not a challenge — simply a question. He is curious whether you will try to answer it truly or say what you think he wishes to hear.

responses.label: You may sit with him and answer his question as truly as you can.
You may ask him a question of your own in return.

entries.body: His question, when it comes, is about you — not your deeds or your lineage, but what you believe you are for. What purpose you move toward. He asks it without ceremony, as a man asks the time of day, and then he waits with the patience of stone.

resolutions.label: answer with your truest understanding of your purpose

success.body:
The answer comes out better than you expected — not perfect, but honest, including the parts you are uncertain of. The king listens to all of it. Then he says simply: 'Good.' In a man of his particular gravity, that word carries more than it would from another.

He gives you a word of counsel before you ride on — so plain and so correct that you will not understand its full weight for another year.

failure.body:
The answer comes out shaped for approval rather than truth, and he knows it before you finish, and you know he knows it. He does not call you on it. He simply thanks you and looks at the well.

There is a particular shame in failing honesty before a man of wisdom. You carry it a while.

entries.body: He smiles — the first real expression you have seen on him — and says fair enough. You may ask him one question and he will answer it as truly as he is able, which is very truly indeed. The question must be your own: not borrowed, not clever, just the thing you most want to know.

resolutions.label: ask the thing you most truly want to know

success.body:
You find the question — it is harder to find than you expected, the genuine one beneath all the useful ones — and you ask it. He answers at length, carefully, as a man who has given the problem thought. The answer is not quite what you hoped for, but it is what you needed.

You will be thinking about it for weeks.

failure.body:
The question you ask is a useful one, but not your own — borrowed from something you heard or read, dressed in your voice. He knows the difference and answers it anyway, but the answer lands flat because the question was not true.

The asking was still worth something. The next question will be truer.

entries.body:
He seems to stumble upon you by accident, though you doubt it. His reputation precedes him — a king who has kept his hall through three reigns of Britain's greater lords by being always slightly more useful than he is threatening — and his opening pleasantries are the pleasantries of a man who wants something and is deciding how to ask without asking.

He needs a knight of reputation to carry a sealed letter to a lord to the east. A debt, an old agreement — a matter of no great moment. The lord is expecting it. The road is easy. He will pay you well for the service.

responses.label: You may take the letter and ride east as he asks.
You may decline, but ask him plainly what the letter truly contains.

entries.body: The lord in the east receives you with more ceremony than a letter-carrier deserves, and more anxiety than a letter about an old debt ought to produce. Something is happening that you were not told, and you are in the middle of it. Your task is to navigate from the middle back to clear ground without becoming the instrument of something you did not choose.

resolutions.label: read the situation and protect yourself from being used as a pawn

success.body:
You read the room correctly — the lord's reception, the sealed letter placed too quickly in the fire, the guards appearing at the courtyard gate — and you find the words and the timing to make yourself too valuable to harm and too aware to use. You leave with your reputation intact and a clear sense of what the letter contained, though you say nothing of it.

A man who knows the game and says nothing is still playing.

failure.body:
You miss the signs until too late. The lord is polite — always polite — but you have been used, and you understand it afterward, as cover for some arrangement that had nothing to do with a debt. You are out of it before real harm is done, but you do not know everything you touched.

Cunning is learned through error. This was an expensive lesson.

entries.body: He pauses. A man of his particular craft is rarely asked plainly, and the asking momentarily displaces the social machinery he relies on. He weighs you — whether you are a fool or a threat — and decides. He may answer honestly, or he may find a way around it. It depends on what he thinks you'll do with the truth.

resolutions.label: press him with directness and hold your ground

success.body:
He tells you, because he has decided that keeping secrets from the kind of knight you appear to be will cause more trouble than revealing them. The letter was a threat dressed as a courtesy, and he wanted a knight's name on it to give it weight.

You decline. He nods, almost satisfied. There is a kind of respect between men who do not play games with each other.

failure.body:
He smiles and sidesteps your directness with a grace you cannot match, and you find yourself agreeing to carry the letter after all, though you could not say quite when or how you agreed.

Honour is necessary but not always sufficient. You will remember this when you open the letter on the road.

entries.body:
He is lost in the ordinary sense — his horse stepped wrong in a ditch two days past and is lame, his retinue is somewhere he is not — but there is something else lost in him too, a greater disorientation that the physical circumstance only makes visible. He is a king of a country that belongs to Arthur now, which means he is a king in name and not much else, and he has been walking these hills thinking about what a man is when the thing that defined him is taken away — not violently, but by treaty, by the slow arithmetic of power.

He is not asking for rescue. He is asking — if anything — for company.

responses.label: You may walk with him and talk as you go, until his retinue is found.
You may help him find his bearings through the country and send him on his way.

entries.body: The talking is not hard to begin — he has things to say, and you can listen, and listening is more useful than speaking for the first hour. He speaks of governance, of loyalty, of the question that has no clean answer when every oath has a counterweight. He is not complaining. He is thinking aloud, and he needs someone to think against.

resolutions.label: be the companion his thought requires for the day

success.body:
You ask the right questions at the right moments — not many, but placed well — and by the time his retinue is found (you hear them calling half a mile off), he has arrived somewhere in himself that he was looking for.

He clasps your hand and calls you by name, though you told him your name only once, and four hours ago at that.

failure.body:
You try to answer his questions when you should listen, and the thinking shifts from his to yours, and somewhere in that shift you lose him. He is polite about it. His retinue finds him as dusk comes down, and he leaves with a word of thanks.

Listening is harder than it looks. You have learned a little of its difficulty.

entries.body: He has been in these hills two days — you can work the direction from what he describes: the rivers crossed, the sun's angle, the pattern of the wood. Navigation is a matter of knowledge and memory, and you have both enough to work with.

resolutions.label: read the country and lead him to his people

success.body:
It takes three hours and some careful reading of the stream-flow, but you find the road he was looking for, and his retinue comes into sight around a long bend in the afternoon light. He looks back at you with something that is not quite gratitude — that would be too small a word for it — and nods.

You ride on knowing the country better than you did.

failure.body:
Your reading goes wrong at the junction of two streams — both running west, which ought not to be possible — and you spend an extra day in the hills before a shepherd sets you right. The king bears it with patience that shames you.

The land has its own logic, and you have learned a little of it the hard way.

entries.body:
He rides toward you from the wrong direction — from the east when all roads from the east are closed — and his horse is thinner than a king's horse has any right to be, and his face has the hollow quality of a man who has stopped eating in solidarity with his people. A pestilence has come to his lands: something in the water, or the air, or the mercy of God withdrawn. Three villages emptied. The abbey closed its doors.

He is riding to find help, and he has found you, and the question in his eyes is not whether you are willing but whether anyone can help at all.

responses.label: You may ride back with him and face what awaits there.
You may offer him what practical aid you carry and send him on to those with greater healing arts.

entries.body: What you find is worse than he described, but not the worst possible — there is still life here, people who have not fled and a few who have not fallen. What is needed is someone who can work without flinching, who can hold what must be held, who does not add fear to a place that already has too much of it.

resolutions.label: work alongside the survivors and endure what must be endured

success.body:
You are there for a week, and the week is the worst week of your life, and at the end of it something has turned — not the pestilence, not yet, but the despair, which is its own contagion. Three men who were giving up begin again. The king watches you and understands, in the way kings understand, what it cost.

You carry a mark of what you touched. You also carry the memory of doing something real.

failure.body:
The week is long and your strength is not equal to it. You fall sick — not mortally, but enough to be of no use and some concern. The king nurses you with a courtesy that shames you, and you ride away having added to his burdens rather than lessened them.

You survived. That, at least, is something to build from.

entries.body: You give what you have — a purse of silver, what herbs you carry, and a letter bearing your name and rank to give him some small authority at the monastery three days to the east. Then you pray with him, because it is what you have left to give and he needs something he cannot buy.

resolutions.label: pray with him and speak words that give his burden meaning

success.body:
He is steadied. Not healed — nothing heals the hollow of what he carries — but steadied, the way a man steadied at a crossroads can still find the right road. He takes the letter and the silver and the herbs, and when he rides away something in his posture is different.

You gave a small thing and it was received as a great one. That is not nothing.

failure.body:
Your prayer is sincere but your words are ordinary, and the king is past the reach of ordinary comfort. He thanks you for the practical gifts, pockets the letter, and rides on. Your prayer sits in the empty air behind you.

God does not always answer in the terms we choose. The attempt was still right.

entries.body:
She travels with only two attendants, and her dress is plain enough to pass for a merchant's wife — but the quality of the horse, the set of her hands on the reins, and the way her attendants move around her like planets around a sun make plain that she is no merchant's wife. She nods when you ask if she has need of company, and she does not give her name.

She is travelling east on a road where several unpleasant men have been seen of late, and she seems neither afraid nor oblivious — more as though she has calculated the risk and decided it is worth it, which is more alarming than either of the alternatives.

responses.label: You may ride with her as escort without asking her business.
You may ask who she is before you offer your service.

entries.body: The road east is three days, and the unpleasant men appear on the second afternoon — four of them, blocked across the road with the studied ease of men who have done this before. The queen's face does not change. She looks at you as though the situation is yours to resolve, which it is.

resolutions.label: clear the road by force or the credible threat of it

success.body:
It does not come to blood — the sight of a knight with a clear purpose and no hesitation persuades them there are easier roads. They depart. The queen watches them go, then looks at you with an expression that might be satisfaction.

'You did not ask who I am,' she says. 'That was the right choice.' She does not explain further.

failure.body:
The ruffians are more numerous than they first appeared, and the confrontation becomes costly — you take a cut across the arm, and one of the attendants takes a blow before they scatter. The queen is safe, but she is watching you with an expression of careful recalibration.

You got her through. That must be enough.

entries.body: She regards your question with neither surprise nor displeasure, as a woman who has decided on her own terms before you asked. She will tell you her name or she will not, and the telling will depend on what she sees in you when you wait for the answer.

resolutions.label: wait for her answer with patience and without pressing

success.body:
She tells you. The name is significant — a queen of one of Britain's greater houses, travelling on a matter she will not describe but which clearly has weight. She accepts your escort with grace, and when you reach her destination she gives you a token of thanks that is more than courtesy.

A queen's regard is not lightly earned. You have begun to earn it.

failure.body:
She declines to say, pleasantly but finally, and you must decide whether that is disqualifying. You escort her anyway, not knowing who she is, which puts a particular kind of pressure on the road that might have been avoided by not asking at all.

Honour sometimes complicates what courtesy would have simplified.

entries.body:
She sits alone in a great hall's garden — her attendants withdrawn to a respectful distance — and she has the stillness of a woman who has trained herself not to show what she feels, though the training is under strain today. The garden is her own; she is queen here. But a queen's garden can be its own kind of prison.

You have been brought to her by her chamberlain, who said only that she wished to speak with a knight of reputation. What she wishes to speak of, you do not yet know.

responses.label: You may listen to whatever she wishes to tell you and offer what service you can.
You may ask her directly what weighs on her before she is ready to say it.

entries.body: When she speaks, it is of her husband — a king who is absent more than he is present, whose absences have a particular quality she does not name. She is not asking you to do anything. She is asking you to understand something, and to be a witness who will not repeat what they have understood.

resolutions.label: hear her with full attention and give nothing away

success.body:
You listen without interruption, without judgment, without the small adjustments that men make when they are uncomfortable with a woman's candour. When she finishes, the silence is clean. She looks at you with something like relief — not because you have helped, but because you have not made it worse.

She gives you a ring as you leave. Not a pledge — a thanks. There is a difference.

failure.body:
You listen, but at some point your expression gives you away — an opinion formed too quickly, a look that says you are thinking about what to say rather than hearing what is said. She sees it. The conversation closes, smoothly, the way a door closes on a room whose contents are no longer shared.

To be trusted is harder than to be brave. The work is different.

entries.body: She looks at you for a moment — measuring whether the directness is useful or intrusive. A woman who has been surrounded by diplomacy all her life has a complicated relationship with plain speech. She might welcome it. She might not.

resolutions.label: ask what troubles her with plain and honest concern

success.body:
You find the right words — not blunt, but direct; not presumptuous, but present — and the question opens something that courtesy alone would not have unlocked. She tells you what she has told no one, and the telling seems to ease something in her, the way bleeding eases a wound.

You leave knowing more than you can use and carrying it carefully.

failure.body:
The question comes out wrong — too early, or too pointed — and she withdraws into the courtesy that queens carry like armour. She is perfectly gracious for the rest of the meeting, and you learn nothing.

Directness requires timing. This was an education in the cost of missing it.

entries.body:
You find her kneeling in the small chapel attached to a wayside manor, and she does not rise when you enter, which is either deep prayer or a test of your manners — possibly both. When she does rise, she has the quality of a woman recently returned from somewhere very far away, though she has not left the room.

She is making a pilgrimage, she says, of the soul rather than the body — working through a vow she made in extremity three years past, when her husband lay dying and she promised God everything in exchange for his life. He lived. Now she is paying.

responses.label: You may walk with her and share in the pilgrimage for a day.
You may ask what form the vow took and whether you can help fulfil it.

entries.body: The pilgrimage is not a physical journey — it is a series of prayers spoken at certain hours, a fast observed, and a silence kept between them. Walking with her means sharing the silence, which is harder than it sounds for a man accustomed to filling his days with motion.

resolutions.label: keep the silence of the pilgrimage without breaking it

success.body:
The day passes in a particular quality of quiet that you have not experienced since childhood. You do not break the silence. By evening she has forgotten you are not part of her own household, which means you have become, briefly, part of her story.

She blesses you at parting with words you do not understand, in a language older than Latin.

failure.body:
You break the silence — not deliberately, but a sound escapes you at the wrong moment, a word said without thinking. She does not reproach you. She simply returns to her prayer as though the moment has passed, and the quality of the afternoon is slightly different from what it was.

Faith is not always a natural condition. Sometimes it is a practice.

entries.body: The vow was specific: she promised to send aid to seven poor houses in God's name, to have a Mass said for seven souls in purgatory, and to go without silver on her person for a year. The last is the difficult one, because a queen without silver is dependent on the goodwill of others in a way that queens are not accustomed to being.

resolutions.label: offer to carry her gifts to the poor houses and ensure the vow is fulfilled

success.body:
You take the gifts — goods rather than coin, which satisfies the vow's terms — and you ride to the seven houses, and you do it properly, not as an errand but as a deed in its own right. When you return, she receives your account with a gravity that suggests she understands the difference.

The vow is kept. A thing promised to God and then kept is a weight lifted from the world.

failure.body:
You reach six of the seven, but the seventh house has moved or does not exist or was described incorrectly, and you cannot find it after three attempts. You return to tell her this, and she nods, unsurprised. There will be another way.

The attempting was itself a form of the vow. You have done something worth doing, even if incomplete.

entries.body:
She stands at the edge of the forest as though she cannot leave it and cannot go further in — frozen at the boundary, one hand against a birch tree, her face turned toward you but her eyes not quite focused on this world. Her attendants are gone, or were never there.

The enchantment is visible to anyone who looks: a shimmer at the edge of her, as if she is a reflection and the original is somewhere else. Morgan's work, or work like Morgan's — a binding that fixes a person at a threshold, caught between the going and the staying.

responses.label: You may attempt to break the binding through your knowledge of such workings.
You may speak to her through the enchantment and find the thread she can pull to free herself.

entries.body: The binding is of a particular kind — not a cage but a contradiction, two equal compulsions pulling in opposite directions, neither strong enough to break the other. The only way through is to dissolve one. You must find which compulsion is false and unmake it, using what power you have of the spiritual arts.

resolutions.label: identify the false compulsion and break it through force of spirit

success.body:
The false compulsion is the older one — a fear, not a truth — and you unmake it cleanly. The shimmer departs. She steps away from the tree with the careful movements of a woman testing that the ground will hold her weight, and then she stands straight and draws a full breath.

'I have been there a long time,' she says. She does not mean the forest.

failure.body:
You pull at the wrong thread. The shimmer intensifies briefly, and she flinches, and you stop before you make it worse. The binding holds.

You cannot undo this alone, or with what you know. But knowing what does not work is the beginning of finding what does.

entries.body: Her eyes are in this world, even if part of her is not. You can talk to her. The question is what to say — what word or memory or truth might be the thread she can follow back, if the enchantment has a weakness on her side that it does not have on yours.

resolutions.label: find the word that reaches her through the enchantment

success.body:
It takes time, and patience, and several wrong tries before the right question gives her something to hold. She answers it, speaking haltingly, and in the answering she moves her weight forward, and the enchantment — which required her stillness — breaks at that first step.

She does not thank you with words. She looks at you for a moment with a clarity that feels like payment enough.

failure.body:
The questions you ask do not reach her — they slide off the surface of the enchantment like water off stone, and after a long time you accept that you do not have the right words. You leave her as you found her, which is its own kind of failure.

Some languages must be learned before they can be spoken.

entries.body:
She is looking for a man she cannot name aloud, in a place she cannot say plainly, for reasons that she will not state — but all three are visible in the way she asks you whether you have seen 'a certain knight, dark-haired, on a grey horse.' The certainty with which she describes him tells you everything about the nature of the search.

She is a queen, not a free woman; the distinction matters, though she is trying to behave as though it does not. The man she seeks has not been seen on this road for a month.

responses.label: * You may offer to find and carry a message to the man she seeks.
You may counsel her gently on the dangers of what she is attempting.

entries.body: She gives you a sealed letter and a token, and the name — quietly, watching your face for judgment. You give her none. The knight in question is known to you by reputation, and his whereabouts require three days' search and a careful reading of certain roads that are not much used.

resolutions.label: find the knight and deliver her message without drawing attention

success.body:
You find him where such men go when they wish not to be found — a particular kind of solitude that leaves just enough trace for someone who knows how to read it. You deliver the letter and the token. He reads the letter twice and asks no questions about who sent you.

You have carried a secret that is not yours, which is the trust she placed in you. Keep it well.

failure.body:
The search takes longer than it should, and by the time you find the knight the letter has lost the moment for which it was written. He reads it anyway, but with the quietness of a man receiving news after it can be acted on.

Timing is everything in the matters that matter most.

entries.body: She listens to your counsel with the patience of a woman who has heard caution from every direction, and she looks at you as though she is trying to decide whether you are the kind of knight whose caution comes from wisdom or from timidity. The distinction matters to her.

resolutions.label: speak your concern plainly and without softening it

success.body:
You tell her, plainly, what the risks are — not in terms of her reputation, which she has already weighed, but in terms of what it will cost the man she seeks if he is found. That consideration stops her. She stands very still.

'I had not thought of it in those terms,' she says. Coming from a woman of her intelligence, that is the highest form of thanks.

failure.body:
Your counsel comes out sounding like judgment, or like fear on your own behalf, and she hears the difference. She thanks you politely and continues on her way, and you watch her go feeling that you said the right things in the wrong order.

The truth requires the right moment as well as the right words.

entries.body:
The road has been cleared for half a mile in either direction and her guard stands at the edges looking inward, not outward, which is how you know the danger is within. She rides in a circle on the empty road, and the circle is exact, and she has been riding it for some time. Her horse is patient in the way horses are patient with grief.

Something has broken in her — not enchantment, though it has the look of it — something that happened in the world that the world had no right to do. She calls out a name in the circling that no one answers.

responses.label: You may ride into the circle and break it by riding alongside her.
You may learn what name she is calling and bring her word of the person.

entries.body: You ride into the circle and take a position beside her, matching your horse to hers, and you begin to ride with her rather than against her. She does not look at you. The name continues. The circle continues. You are betting that presence, rather than intervention, is what is needed.

resolutions.label: ride with her in patient companionship until the circle breaks itself

success.body:
It is two hours before the circle slows, and another hour before it stops. She has not spoken to you, but you are there when she finally halts and lets her horse's head drop and sits in the sudden stillness. She does not weep. She simply is, for a moment, present.

Her guard looks at you with an expression that is wholly gratitude.

failure.body:
The circle is patient and so are you, but your patience runs out first — you need water, your horse needs rest — and you are forced to leave the circle before she does. She continues as you go.

Endurance, like all virtues, has a limit. You have found yours, which is worth knowing.

entries.body: Her guard tells you the name when you ask: her son, who died three months past of a fever. A simple death, a terrible one. She had been told by a travelling soothsayer that if she called his name enough he might answer from whatever place he had gone to. The guard does not believe it will work. He does not say so to her.

resolutions.label: speak to her gently of what you know of the soul's passage and bring her some comfort

success.body:
You ride into the circle and speak his name once, clearly, as though you knew him, and then you say what you believe: that he is heard, wherever he is, and that the calling has reached him, and that he would not wish her to ride in circles.

The circling slows. Perhaps it is only that she is tired. It does not matter.

failure.body:
Your words are not wrong, but they are not right either — they are the words a priest says, not the words a mother hears. She does not stop circling. She does not seem to hear you at all.

There are griefs that are their own language, and you have not yet learned it.

entries.body:
She has ridden three days to find you, which means someone told her where you were, and that someone wished this meeting to happen. She gives her name and her hall and her cause without preamble: a lord of the south has dishonoured one of her ladies — taken her, then discarded her, and made a joke of the discarding. She has petitioned Arthur. She has been told to wait for the hearing. She has waited four months.

She is not waiting any longer. She wants a knight to ride south and make the situation plain.

responses.label: You may ride south on her behalf and confront the lord.
You may offer to carry her petition directly to Arthur and force the hearing.

entries.body: The lord of the south is not a great man, but he is a connected one, and he receives you with the blend of courtesy and contempt that minor men use when they believe themselves to be untouchable. He does not deny what was done. He simply believes it to be unremarkable.

resolutions.label: make plain to him the nature of his wrongdoing and demand a public reckoning

success.body:
You say the thing that needs to be said, clearly and in front of witnesses, and you do not leave until you have his word — publicly given — that the lady will be compensated and the jest never repeated. He is angry. He will be angry for a long time. But the public word is given.

When you tell the queen, she says nothing for a moment. Then: 'Good.'

failure.body:
He outmanoeuvres you socially — turns your accusation into a grievance against himself, gathers his people around him before you can isolate the question of right and wrong. You leave having said your piece but having achieved nothing concrete.

Justice requires strategy, not only will. You will need both next time.

entries.body: Carrying a petition to Arthur is not difficult. Being heard is. The court is large, the schedule of matters is long, and four months of waiting suggests that the petition has been shelved rather than lost. You will need to find the right moment and the right ear.

resolutions.label: navigate the court and bring the petition before the king

success.body:
You find the angle — a moment in the great hall when the king is in good temper and the matter can be framed as what it is: an insult to the realm's honour rather than a private complaint. The hearing is granted. The queen is sent for.

Justice delayed is not justice denied, if the delay can be ended. You have ended it.

failure.body:
The court deflects you with its usual machinery — the matter is being reviewed, the appropriate parties have been notified, someone will be in contact. You leave no better than you arrived.

Patience is a weapon that some courts understand and others use against you. You have learned which kind this is.

entries.body:
She has ruled her county alone since her husband's death seven years past, and she has ruled it well — her people do not go hungry, her roads are safe, her hall is known for honest dealing. She sends for you not because she needs a sword but because she needs a second opinion, and she is the kind of person who knows the difference.

She has received two offers of marriage in the last year: one from a lord who would double her lands and halve her authority, one from a younger man of modest means who would change neither and might improve both. She wants a knight's perspective, not because she will necessarily take it, but because she wants to hear herself think aloud.

responses.label: You may give her your honest assessment of the two offers.
You may ask her which she already prefers, and work from her own wisdom.

entries.body: She lays the two cases out with the precision of a woman who has already thought them through completely and wants to hear them said back to her. The rich lord's offer is political, practical, and carries risks she has already catalogued. The younger man's offer is personal, uncertain, and carries a kind of promise that cannot be guaranteed.

resolutions.label: give your honest assessment of the two offers without favour

success.body:
You see the thing she already knows but wants confirmed: that the practical choice will cost her the thing she has built, and the personal choice may strengthen it. You say so without telling her what to do, which is the difference between counsel and command.

She smiles — not warmly, but genuinely. 'You are more useful than most,' she says. You take it as the high praise it is.

failure.body:
You give her a knight's answer rather than a wise one, and she hears the difference before you do. She thanks you and closes the subject, and you ride away suspecting you told her what you thought she wanted to hear rather than what you actually saw.

A wise person can usually teach the people who try to counsel them, if they let themselves.

entries.body: She pauses when you ask this, because it is not the kind of question she is usually asked. She is usually told. The pause has quality in it — it is not the pause of someone who does not know, but of someone who is deciding whether to say what they know.

resolutions.label: wait for her true preference with patience and complete attention

success.body:
She tells you. It is the younger man, for reasons she states clearly once she begins — his steadiness, his lack of ambition, the way he asks questions rather than making pronouncements. You listen, and when she finishes you simply say: 'Then you have already decided.' She laughs, which is a good sound in a room that has been full of serious things.

You have helped her by not helping her at all.

failure.body:
She begins to tell you, then stops, and the moment closes — not from your fault exactly, but from some quality in the room, in the timing, in the weight of the question she is no longer ready to answer aloud. She thanks you for your time.

Some conversations can only happen once, and only when the conditions are exactly right.

entries.body:
She sends for you through a third party — a merchant, of all people, who carries a sealed note and does not know what is in it. The note says only that a lady of consequence wishes to speak with you at a particular inn, at a particular hour, on a matter that will be worth your time.

The lady of consequence, when you arrive, is a queen in the old sense: sharp, poised, accustomed to getting what she wants by making people believe they arrived at her conclusion themselves. She wants something from you, and she is deciding how to make you want to give it.

responses.label: You may let her make her case and hear it out before you decide anything.
You may ask her directly what she wants before she has the chance to frame it.

entries.body:
She makes her case with the skill of a woman who has made many cases, and it is — as these things go — a good one: she needs a knight to ride with a document to a lord in the north, a document that will settle a dispute before it becomes a war. The document exists. The dispute is real. The war would be costly. Everything she says is true.

The question is what she is not saying.

resolutions.label: listen for what she is not saying as much as what she is

success.body:
What she is not saying is that the document benefits her house as much as it settles the dispute — that the settlement, fairly read, tilts the balance of influence northward in a particular direction. Not wrongly. But deliberately. You see it, and you let her see that you see it, and she nods, and now you negotiate the actual terms.

Things done clearly are cleaner than things done in the dark.

failure.body:
You hear what she says and agree to it without seeing what she left out. Only three days later, on the road, does the omission become visible — and by then you are committed to a task whose edges are not what they appeared.

A keen ear is often more useful than a sharp sword. You will keep yours sharper next time.

entries.body: She pauses. She was not expecting directness — she rarely is, because directness is not the custom in the places she moves through. The pause is brief. She adjusts. She is the kind of woman who adjusts quickly.

resolutions.label: hold your direct approach and do not let her reframe the exchange

success.body:
She tells you plainly, which is what she would have done eventually anyway, and the plainness is almost refreshing to her — you can see it in the slight release of tension at her shoulders. The ask is what it is: real, useful, and carrying a political benefit she would have hidden if you had let her. You accept or decline from a position of full knowledge.

The cleanest agreements are made in clear air.

failure.body:
Your directness arrives as accusation rather than clarity, and she retreats into formality, and the conversation becomes a series of courtesies that reach nowhere. You leave without what you came for.

Directness requires a careful aim. You fired wide.

entries.body:
She is on the wrong road and she knows it, and she is not going to turn back because turning back would mean admitting that the right road was the one she refused to take. This is a common kind of being lost: not geographical but principled, the lost that happens when a woman has made a vow and the vow has led her somewhere she did not expect.

She vowed to bring a petition to a holy place three days east, but the eastern road was blocked by a landslide, and now she is two days west of where she was, on a road that may or may not connect to any road that goes east.

responses.label: You may guide her to the holy place by a different route you know.
You may help her understand whether the vow permits a delay and offers another way.

entries.body: You know the country well enough to see an alternative: a longer route, two days more, but clear and passable. The question is whether she has the time and the provisions for two extra days, and whether the longer road is still the road she vowed to walk.

resolutions.label: lead her by the longer way to the holy place

success.body:
The longer road is harder but beautiful — you pass through country that the blocked road never offered, and she remarks on it twice, the third time with something like wonder. You arrive at the holy place two days later than planned, and she makes her petition with a clarity she might not have had on the easier road.

'The detour was itself the journey,' she says. You think she may be right.

failure.body:
Your knowledge of the country proves less reliable than you thought — a bridge is out that you expected to stand, and you lose a full day finding the ford. She bears it without complaint, but the extra day puts the holy place beyond her supplies. You reach it, but barely.

Confidence in the country should be tested before it is offered.

entries.body: The vow was made to a specific place by a specific route, and the spirit of a vow and the letter of it are different things, and the theology of one versus the other is not a simple matter. She needs someone who understands not just the law but the intention behind it.

resolutions.label: reason through the vow's intention and offer your understanding of what God requires

success.body:
Your reasoning is this: the vow was made to reach a holy place, not to travel a specific road, and the obstruction was not of her making. God, who set the landslide there, presumably knows about it. She listens to this with the careful attention of a woman checking it for holes, and when she finds none she nods.

'That is good theology,' she says. 'I think.'

failure.body:
Your reasoning is well-meant but you reach past your knowledge of these matters, and she catches it, politely. She will find a priest and ask again. You have not helped her and you have been slightly embarrassed in the attempting.

The humility to know the edge of your knowledge is itself a kind of wisdom.

entries.body:
She met you on the road because she had no choice. She is three days from her hall and she has lost half her escort to an ambush — they are alive, most of them, but scattered and unable to reconvene in the time she needs. She is carrying something of great importance — you can see it in the way she holds herself, the way one hand stays near the saddlebag at all times.

She does not say what it is. She asks only whether you will ride with her to her hall, and she says it as a woman says a thing when she has no other option and knows it.

responses.label: You may ride with her without asking what she carries.
You may ask what she carries before you commit to the road.

entries.body: The road to her hall takes three days, and the men who ambushed her escort have not given up — they appear again on the second afternoon, better-organised this time, and with a clear purpose that is about the saddlebag rather than the knight accompanying it.

resolutions.label: defend her and what she carries against the ambush

success.body:
The fight is serious, and it costs you — a cut here, a bruise there — but you hold the road and the attackers break. She is unhurt. The saddlebag is unhurt. When you reach her hall that evening and she opens it to confirm the contents are safe, you see a brief glimpse of something that glows with its own light before she covers it again.

She thanks you. She says: 'You did not ask.' You say: 'No.' That is the whole conversation.

failure.body:
You hold most of the road, but one attacker gets past you and seizes the saddlebag before you can recover it. He is gone into the wood before you can give chase. The queen is safe. She looks at the empty road where the saddlebag went and says nothing for a long time.

You failed to protect what was most important. That knowledge will ride with you a while.

entries.body: She looks at you for a moment, deciding. She needs you — badly enough that refusing to tell you is a worse risk than telling you. She opens the saddlebag a fraction and you see enough to understand: a relic of some kind, small and wrapped in cloth, but charged with a quality that makes the air around it feel different.

resolutions.label: accept what you have seen and commit to the road with full knowledge

success.body:
Knowing what you carry changes nothing about how you carry it — if anything, it clarifies your purpose. The three-day road has its dangers, but you ride with the particular attention of a knight who knows what is at stake. You deliver her to her hall and the relic with her.

She says, as you leave: 'Most knights ask out of curiosity. You asked out of duty. There is a difference.'

failure.body:
Knowing what you carry unsteadies you — the weight of what it might mean, the fear of what happens if you fail — and that unsteadiness shows on the road. You make a decision at the ambush that the queen would not have made, and it costs you precious ground.

Knowledge is a weight as well as a tool. You are learning how to carry it.

entries.body:
He rides without a device on his shield — it is covered in black cloth, the way a device is covered when a knight has disavowed a name or a cause. He stops when he sees you and his hand does not go to his sword, but the readiness is there. He is not aggressive. He is simply a man who has been alone on the road for long enough that other people have become something to be assessed rather than assumed.

He is wandering, he says, on a self-imposed term of service — he swore to ride without a name until he had done a deed that deserved one. He does not say what the old name was or why it needed to be earned back.

responses.label: You may ride with him for a time and share the road.
You may challenge him to a friendly pass of arms, as knights who meet on the road may do.

entries.body: Two days on the road with a man who is choosing silence makes a particular kind of company. He is not unfriendly, but he holds himself at a distance that is not distance from you specifically but distance from everything, and you begin to understand that the wandering itself is the penance. He is waiting for a moment that will tell him his time is done.

resolutions.label: share the road and give him something worth riding toward

success.body:
You tell him of an injustice you passed on the road three days back — a small one, but real, and unaddressed because you had other business. He listens. He asks two questions. He nods. The next morning he is gone when you wake, riding east.

You gave him a deed to ride toward. Whether he found it worthy, you do not know. But his horse had a different quality of purpose to its gait.

failure.body:
Whatever you offer — counsel, company, a cause — does not reach the particular quality of readiness in him. He listens to all of it and remains at his distance. In the morning he turns north; you turn south.

Not all wanderers are waiting for the same thing. You have learned to look more closely at the waiting.

entries.body: He accepts the challenge with a gravity that tells you this is not mere exercise to him — every joust in his current state is a measure of whether the name, when he earns it back, is worth having. He is very good. The question is whether you are better.

resolutions.label: ride against him with your full skill and prove yourself

success.body:
You unseat him — cleanly, no foul in it, a good pass — and he lies in the road for a moment looking at the sky. Then he gets up and laughs, which is the first real sound you have heard from him. He retrieves his horse and bows.

'You are better,' he says. 'That is useful to know.' He rides on lighter than he arrived.

failure.body:
He does not unseat you, but he does not need to — the pass is close enough, his technique precise enough, that you know you were beaten in everything that mattered. You both rein in and regard each other in the road.

'You are good,' he says, without irony. Coming from a man who fights like that, it is worth something.

entries.body:
He sits beside the road with his helm off and his sword across his knees, and you know by the way he holds himself that someone is dead. Not an enemy — a friend. He looks up when you approach with the eyes of a man who has been sitting with something heavy for too long and has not yet decided whether to put it down.

He tells you the name, when you ask: a brother-knight, fallen in a skirmish three weeks past. A good man. Better than most. The kind of man the world does not have enough of, and has one fewer now.

responses.label: You may sit with him and be present with his grief.
You may speak of the dead knight's deeds and give the grief somewhere to go.

entries.body: You sit beside him on the verge of the road and take the weight of the silence. This is what friendship asks sometimes: not words, not remedy, only the evidence that you are still in the world together, that the world is still worth being in.

resolutions.label: remain present with him until the silence has done its work

success.body:
After a time — it is the kind of time that feels longer than it is — he puts his helm back on. Not because he is done grieving, but because the grief has found its shape and can be carried now. He clasps your arm.

'Thank you for not saying anything useful,' he says. You take it as intended.

failure.body:
The silence works against you — you find yourself shifting, looking for something to offer, some word that would give the moment a direction. He notices. He stands and says he must ride on.

Not every vigil can be kept by every person. You have learned something about the particular hardness of stillness.

entries.body: You knew the dead knight by reputation, or perhaps by meeting, and the stories are there to be told. Grief needs somewhere to go — it can go into silence, but it can also go into memory, into the act of speaking the deeds aloud and making them permanent in the telling.

resolutions.label: speak of the dead knight's deeds with honesty and care

success.body:
You find the right story — not the famous one, but a smaller one that only a few people saw, which makes it more real for the telling. He listens with his whole attention, and when you are done his eyes are wet but his jaw is set, the way a man looks when grief has turned into something he can use.

'He would have liked that one told,' he says.

failure.body:
You reach for a story and pull out something too large or too formal — a famous deed, a public honour — and it is the wrong shape for the moment, too much armour for what is needed. He listens politely.

The right memorial is specific, not grand. You will know this next time.

entries.body:
He is returning from a quest completed, and the completion shows in him the way morning shows in a room where the shutters are opened — not a single change but the quality of everything at once. He completed a task he had undertaken for reasons of honour and against poor odds, and the world had allowed it, which is not something that can be assumed.

He rides slowly, as though the road is something to be tasted rather than covered, and he stops when he sees you with genuine pleasure, as a man with good news who has not yet had the chance to tell it.

responses.label: You may ride with him and hear what he accomplished.
You may ask him what the quest taught him rather than what it achieved.

entries.body: The story is a good one, and he tells it well — neither inflating it nor diminishing it, which is the mark of a knight who knows what he actually did. At a certain point in the telling he pauses and looks at you with a directness that suggests the story has become, in some way, about you.

resolutions.label: receive his account with full attention and offer your honest response

success.body:
Your honest response is that the deed was genuinely worthy, and you say so without the social inflation that would make it a compliment. He understands the difference and values it. The conversation continues longer than roads usually allow for, and when you part it is with the feeling of something real exchanged.

Good company is its own kind of quest completed.

failure.body:
You try too hard to match the moment — to say something worthy of his story — and the effort shows. He looks at you kindly but the connection you were reaching for does not form. He rides on satisfied with his own company, which was always going to be good enough.

The desire to be equal to a moment can prevent you from simply being in it.

entries.body: He considers your question with more seriousness than the journey had prepared him for. The question of what a quest teaches is different from the question of what it accomplished, and he has not yet sorted the two — he has been telling the accomplished version and has not yet spoken the taught version aloud.

resolutions.label: draw out what the quest truly taught him

success.body:
He thinks for a time, and then he says a thing that he did not know he understood until he said it — something about patience, or about the particular quality of fear that tells you the right direction, or about what it means to complete a thing that did not need to be done. He looks surprised by his own answer.

'I shall have to write that down,' he says, 'before I lose it.'

failure.body:
Your question is good but your timing is wrong — he is still in the glow of the completing, and the deeper lesson has not cooled enough to be examined. He gives you the surface answer, which is fine but not the thing you were reaching for.

Good questions deserve the right moment. Perhaps when he has ridden another day.

entries.body:
He rides in armour of black and silver, and there is nothing wrong with the armour or the horse or the road he travels — except that he cannot stop. Not physically: he can rein in when he chooses. But when he tries to rest, his horse begins again of its own will, and he has been riding for six days without meaningful sleep and the enchantment shows in his eyes.

Something was laid on him at a crossing three days back — a gift from a lady who was not what she appeared, which is the beginning of half the troubles in Britain. The enchantment is not of the killing kind. But it will exhaust him to death if it is not lifted.

responses.label: You may attempt to lift the enchantment through your knowledge of the spiritual arts.
You may ride alongside him and use your own will to hold him still long enough to sleep.

entries.body: The enchantment is of the riding kind — not a compulsion of the will but a compulsion of the body, placed in the horse as much as in the rider. You must address both, and the working that placed it is old and clever and will not yield to anything less than a full counter.

resolutions.label: unravel the enchantment from rider and horse together

success.body:
It takes the better part of an hour, and your own hands are shaking at the end of it, but the horse stops. The knight almost falls from the saddle. You catch him and lower him to the ground, and he is asleep before you have removed his helm.

He sleeps for twelve hours. You keep watch. When he wakes, the enchantment is entirely gone, and he looks at you with the expression of a man who cannot fully comprehend what he owes you.

failure.body:
The enchantment resists you — it is too old and too well-made for what you can bring against it alone. The knight looks at you with exhausted gratitude for the attempt, and rides on.

Some workings require more than one person, or more than one skill. The attempt was not wasted — you have learned the shape of what is needed.

entries.body: There is a simpler approach than breaking the enchantment: if two people ride together, the compulsion has less purchase — it was made for a lone rider. If you can ride close enough, hold his horse's bridle, and will the stillness with enough force, there may be a window in which he can sleep.

resolutions.label: hold him still through sheer will and force of presence

success.body:
It works, though only in intervals — you can hold the horse for an hour at a time before the compulsion reasserts itself. Over the course of a day you give him three hours of sleep in pieces, and three hours of sleep in pieces is enough to bring a man back from the edge.

He is still under the enchantment when you part, but he is no longer dying of it.

failure.body:
The compulsion is stronger than your will, or the timing is wrong, and you cannot hold him still for long enough to matter. He thanks you anyway, and rides on.

Some problems cannot be solved by effort alone. But effort is often the only tool you have.

entries.body:
He challenges you to a joust before you have exchanged names, which is his right on the open road, and his bearing says the challenge is not hostile — it is the challenge of a man who has so much energy that it must go somewhere, and jousting is the most socially acceptable somewhere available. He is in love, you can see it, and love in a young knight is a kind of intoxication that makes everything seem possible.

The object of his devotion is a lady of another lord's house, and the joust is, in his mind, the opening formality of a much longer campaign.

responses.label: * You may joust with him and in winning or losing discover the quality of the man.
You may decline the joust and speak with him about the wisdom of his pursuit.

entries.body: He is very good — the particular quality of good that comes from excessive practice in service of a fixation. He rides with a joy that is almost reckless, and the joy is contagious enough that you find yourself matching it rather than countering it. This is the kind of joust that both parties can win.

resolutions.label: ride against him with skill and spirit

success.body:
The pass is one of those that is remembered: two knights well-matched, both riding with something real behind the effort, and the outcome a matter of inches. You unseat him — or he unseat you — and it does not matter, because both of you know it was a good pass.
