He laughs and pulls you to your feet or receives your hand himself. 'She will hear about this,' he says, meaning his lady, meaning the story is already being shaped for her.

failure.body:
The joy he rides with tips into recklessness and takes you with it, and the pass ends badly — a fall that rattles both of you, a moment of confused sorting-out in the road. You are both unhurt, but the grace of it is gone.

Joy and skill are different things, and the one does not guarantee the other.

entries.body: He is not especially interested in wisdom, but he is a knight and he has the manners to listen. You may find the angle that reaches him — something about the lady herself, her situation, what an excess of hopeful ardour does to a woman who did not ask for it — or you may not.

resolutions.label: counsel him with honesty about the nature of his pursuit

success.body:
You find the right angle — not a warning about his chances, but a question about hers. What does she want? Has he asked? He is quiet for a moment, which is remarkable in a man in his current condition.

'I have not asked,' he says. He looks like a man who has just noticed a very obvious thing. You hope he does something useful with the noticing.

failure.body:
He listens with the patience of a man who is already thinking about what he will do after the conversation ends. He thanks you and rides on at the same speed, toward the same destination.

Love, in its early phase, is not easily counselled. You have learned the particular stubbornness of it.

entries.body:
He is fighting a battle that ended a year ago. The road is his field, and the trees are his enemies, and he is riding in the patterns of the engagement — forward, wheel, charge again — calling out to men who are not there. His arms are real and his horse is real and the danger to any traveller on this road is real.

Something broke in him at that battle — not enchantment, but the kind of breaking that looks like enchantment from the outside. He was present for something that the mind refuses to contain, and the refusing has gone sideways.

responses.label: You may ride into his field of battle and find a way to stop the fighting.
You may wait for a pause in the riding and try to reach him in that moment.

entries.body: You ride into the pattern and become part of it — not as an enemy, but as a fellow knight on his side, calling to him in the language of the engagement. The risk is that the battle-mind will not distinguish friend from foe. The possibility is that it will, if you are convincing enough.

resolutions.label: take your place in his battle and ride it to its end

success.body:
You ride the pattern with him for half an hour, calling the correct responses, moving as the battle required, and when you reach the moment of the original victory — the turn that won the field — you call the command to stand down and hold your ground.

He stops. He looks at you. He is exhausted and bewildered and entirely present. 'Did we win?' he asks. You say yes. He says: 'Good.' Then he begins to weep.

failure.body:
You ride into the pattern and it does not know you — or it does, and takes you for an enemy. You are forced to break off before you are hurt, and the battle continues without you.

This is not a problem that force can solve. You need a different language.

entries.body: There is a pause in the riding — a moment when he stops at the edge of the road and looks at the trees as though they have suddenly become trees again. That moment is your window. It is small and it may close quickly, but it is there.

resolutions.label: reach him in the pause with something that returns him to himself

success.body:
You call his name — his real name, not his title — and something in the register of it works, the way a particular bell-tone can carry further than a shout. He turns to look at you. The battle retreats.

You pray with him then, briefly, in the old form, and the old form is a place he recognises. When he looks up the road is ordinary again.

failure.body:
The window closes before you find the right word. He rides back into the pattern, and it is a thing you cannot follow him into today.

Patience and the right moment must align. You have the patience. The moment will come again.

entries.body:
He tells you what was done to him before you ask: a fellow knight unseated him at tournament through foul means — a move illegal under the rules of the lists, witnessed by half the gallery and unpunished because the offending knight stands high in a powerful lord's favour. He reported it. He was advised to let the matter rest. He is not letting the matter rest.

He is not asking for your help. He is telling you, because a thing said aloud is more real than a thing only thought, and he wants it to be real.

responses.label: You may offer to stand as his witness and help him bring the matter before Arthur.
You may counsel him on the wiser course between public grievance and private redress.

entries.body: Standing as his witness means you will be drawing the displeasure of a powerful lord — a man with connections to Arthur's court who does not enjoy having his knights' conduct questioned. The case is righteous. The path is not simple.

resolutions.label: stand as his witness before the court and hold your ground

success.body:
You stand and give your account, clearly and without softening, and the weight of your name and your clarity together is enough. The offending knight is censured. The lord is displeased. You have made an enemy of consequence.

Some enemies are worth having. This one confirms you are doing something right.

failure.body:
The lord's influence reaches further into the court than you knew, and your witness is received with doubt and procedural delay. The case is not dismissed — it is still there — but it is buried under the weight of a man who does not wish to be accused.

Justice delayed is not justice abandoned. But it tests the will of the just.

entries.body: You have seen this kind of case before: a genuine wrong, a compromised court, and a man whose righteousness may cost him more than the original injury. The question is not whether his cause is just — it plainly is — but which course serves justice best.

resolutions.label: help him think through the wiser path to genuine redress

success.body:
You lay out the two courses honestly, and you ask which of them he can live with — not in terms of outcome, but in terms of self. He is quiet for a long time, and then he says the one thing that matters most to him is not the punishment but the public acknowledgment that what was done was wrong.

With that clarity, the path becomes visible. It is not the loudest path, but it is the right one.

failure.body:
Your counsel is too cautious for where he is emotionally, and he hears it as discouragement rather than strategy. He thanks you and rides on his original course.

The right counsel at the wrong moment can do more harm than none at all.

entries.body:
He is old enough to have fought beside men your grandfather knew, and he carries himself with the particular ease of a man who has nothing left to prove. He does not stop you for a quest or a challenge — he stops you because he recognises something in your bearing that interests him, and because the road is long and the day is fine and a good conversation is its own reward.

He has seen three kings and outlasted most of his generation, and the things he knows are not in any chronicle. He offers to ride with you for an afternoon.

responses.label: You may ride with him and learn what he is willing to teach.
You may ask him the one question that, in all his years, he most wishes someone had asked him.

entries.body: He teaches by example more than instruction — a comment here, a silence there, a way of approaching something that you see and then understand you have been doing wrong. The lesson is not about swords. It is about how to read a day, a man, a moment.

resolutions.label: learn from him with your full attention

success.body:
By the end of the afternoon you are a different kind of attentive than you were in the morning. He has not said much — five or six things, perhaps, of the instructive kind — but each one has landed squarely, and they will come back to you over the next months in different situations.

'You have good ears,' he says when you part. It is the highest praise he gives.

failure.body:
You try to take in everything and in the trying miss some of the things worth keeping. He does not remark on this. He rides on when the afternoon is done.

The habit of reaching for everything is the enemy of the habit of receiving what is given. You have identified your obstacle.

entries.body: He stops when you ask this, and you can see him going through the years, deciding which of many possible answers is the true one. He is not a man who rushes an answer. He is a man who gave up rushing things a long time ago.

resolutions.label: wait for his answer with full patience and receive it completely

success.body:
He tells you. It is not the question you would have guessed, and the answer he gives is not a lesson but a sorrow — something he would have done differently, and the reason it could not be done differently at the time, and why the two things together are what they are. He says it without self-pity.

You carry it with you. It makes you more careful with something you were careless with before.

failure.body:
You ask well, but something in the moment — a sound on the road, your own slight restlessness — shifts the quality, and he gives you a public answer rather than the true one. It is good, but it is not what you asked for.

The oldest wisdom is offered only when the asking is completely clean.

entries.body:
He wants something, and everything about him is in service of the wanting — the pleasantness of his manner, the timing of his appearance, the careful compliment he opens with. He is a knight of the Round Table or close enough to it that the difference hardly matters, and he is good at the particular game of obtaining assistance that looks like companionship.

The assistance he wants involves riding with him to a particular hall and being seen there. Not to fight. Not even to speak. Simply to be seen, which will tell a particular lord something he wants that lord to believe.

responses.label: You may agree to accompany him and see what the hall reveals.
You may name what he is doing before he has finished offering it.

entries.body: The hall is three hours away, and on the road there he tells you more than he intended — the shape of the politics, the name of the lord to be impressed, and incidentally what is actually at stake. By the time you arrive you know more than was offered, which is a position of some power.

resolutions.label: use what you have learned to navigate the hall on your own terms

success.body:
You are seen, as requested, but not only as requested — you take one additional action in the hall that shifts the calculation slightly in your own direction, nothing dramatic, nothing that the knight beside you can complain about, but enough to make it known that you were there for reasons of your own as much as his.

He knows what you did. He does not say anything, which is its own kind of respect.

failure.body:
The hall is more complicated than either of you planned, and the lord in question is not where he was supposed to be, and the political message you were meant to carry has nowhere to land. You ride home having been used as an instrument in a message that was never sent.

Complexity favours the better-prepared. Next time, ask more questions on the road.

entries.body: He pauses, recalibrates, and then — because there is no reasonable alternative — tells you plainly what he wants. He is not embarrassed. He expected this possibility. He continues the offer anyway, on honest terms.

resolutions.label: engage with him honestly about whether his cause is one you will serve

success.body:
He tells you the truth of it, and the truth of it is actually reasonable — a lord is being misled about your knight's reputation, and a brief appearance with a knight of known good standing would correct the impression without any deception on anyone's part. You agree, because it is simply true.

Transparency is not always the enemy of persuasion. Sometimes it is its best instrument.

failure.body:
The truth of his situation, on examination, is more entangled than he presented even honestly, and you decline on terms that leave him with no useful alternatives. He departs with a politeness that covers irritation.

Honesty, brought to bear at the right moment, protects more than it costs.

entries.body:
He stopped at this crossroads three hours past, and he is still there. Not because his horse is lame or his road is unclear — all four roads are clear, and his horse is fine. He is stopped because he does not know which road leads to the thing that would make continuing worthwhile, and until he knows that, all four roads are equal.

He was a good knight once — still is, probably, in the technical sense — but somewhere the purpose came loose from the doing, and without the purpose the doing is just motion.

responses.label: You may tell him what you are riding toward and offer him a direction worth following.
You may ask him what road he most fears to take, and whether that fear is the answer.

entries.body: Your cause is real, and you can describe it plainly, and sometimes a real cause simply offered is enough to interrupt the circling that paralysis produces. He listens to you with the attention of a starving man watching bread being cut.

resolutions.label: offer him your road and your cause with honest clarity

success.body:
Something in the way you describe it — not the cause itself, but the plainness with which you hold it — answers something in him. He asks one question, then another, and by the second he is already deciding. He turns his horse to the same road as yours.

You have given someone something to ride toward. That is one of the few truly useful things one knight can give another.

failure.body:
Your cause is genuine but it is yours, and it does not speak to whatever is specifically missing in him. He listens carefully and thanks you and sits back at the crossroads.

The right cause finds the right person. Yours was not his. That is not failure — it is simply information.

entries.body: He looks at you with the expression of a man who was not expecting to be asked the difficult question. He knows which road he fears. He has been not looking at it for three hours.

resolutions.label: draw out what he fears and help him see what that fear means

success.body:
He says it. The feared road is north. The reason it is feared is that north is where the thing is that he failed before, and failing it once made him uncertain he could face it again.

You say: the fear is the direction. He looks at the north road. He looks at you. He nods, once, and turns his horse north. You have not solved anything. You have only made clear what was already there.

failure.body:
He will not say what he fears, not quite — he approaches it and then deflects, and after several attempts you both understand that today is not the day for that particular truth.

Some doors open from the inside only. You can knock. You cannot open them.

entries.body:
He has one horse, no squire, and armour that has been repaired so many times it is more repairs than original metal. He has ridden from the south to find a man who owes him a debt of honour — not money, but the public acknowledgment of a deed done in that man's service, a deed whose credit was claimed by another. He has been pursuing this acknowledgment for two years.

He is not bitter — something in the long pursuit has purified him past bitterness into something simpler and more dangerous. He simply will not stop until the account is settled.

responses.label: You may offer to ride with him to find the man and stand as witness to the claim.
You may offer what you can provide immediately — supplies, silver, your name in a letter — and send him on his way better-equipped.

entries.body: The man who owes the debt is two days north, in a hall that is comfortable and well-connected and knows how to delay what it does not wish to receive. Finding him is simple. Making him face the claim is not.

resolutions.label: stand with the desperate knight and force the acknowledgment

success.body:
With two knights present and one of them unknown to the hall's calculations, the delay becomes impossible. The acknowledgment is given — public, explicit, and witnessed. It is not a reversal of fortune. It is simply the truth, said at last in the right room.

The desperate knight sits very still for a long time after. Then he says: 'That is all I needed.'

failure.body:
The hall's machinery of evasion is older and more practiced than your combined directness, and you cannot pin the moment down. You leave having made yourself known as advocates but without the acknowledgment you came for.

Two years of waiting deserves better than this. So does your effort. The case is not closed.

entries.body: He has nothing he needs materially that he will accept — he is past accepting charity, and the desperate quality in him is specific to the one thing he lacks. But your name in a letter, certifying the facts as you understand them, is another matter. A knight's word in writing is currency in certain courts.

resolutions.label: write him a letter of witness to strengthen his claim

success.body:
You write the letter carefully, in the form a court will receive — not an accusation but a statement of fact, signed with your name and your device. He takes it with both hands, which tells you something about what the two years have cost him.

'This is more than you needed to do,' he says. You say it is no more than the truth requires. He nods and rides north.

failure.body:
You write the letter, but something in the phrasing is wrong — too hedged, or too assertive, or in the wrong form for the courts he will approach — and he reads it and thanks you but you can both see it will not serve.

The right words in the right form are a skill that takes longer to build than a good sword arm. Keep building.

entries.body:
He rides alone on a road that a squire should not be riding alone, carrying a message for a lord two days north and the particular air of a young man who accepted this errand because it would prove something and is now discovering that proving things has a cost. He is not lost. He is not in danger, exactly. But he is further from anyone who knows his name than he has ever been, and his horse has thrown a shoe.

He asks you, with the dignity of someone who does not wish to show need, whether you know the nearest smith.

responses.label: You may ride with him to the smith and on toward his destination.
You may give him what knowledge you have and let him complete the errand himself.

entries.body: The smith is two miles back and takes an hour, and by the time the horse is shod it is late enough that the two-day journey north is a longer proposition than it was this morning. He accepts your company with more relief than he shows.

resolutions.label: see him through to his destination safely

success.body:
Three days of road, ordinary enough, and the message is delivered. The lord receives it and dismisses the squire with the boredom of a man for whom such errands are daily business — but the squire carries himself differently on the way back. He has done the thing he set out to do.

That is not nothing, as first accomplishments go.

failure.body:
The road produces difficulties — nothing dramatic, but enough delays that the message arrives late and the lord is displeased. The squire takes the displeasure with more equanimity than you expected. He has learned something about the gap between intention and outcome.

First errands are rarely clean. That is part of what they teach.

entries.body: He needs the knowledge more than the company — the ability to solve the problem himself, rather than being solved. You can give him directions, and the name of the smith, and a piece of advice about the road north that may save him half a day.

resolutions.label: equip him with exactly what he needs and no more

success.body:
You give him three things: the smith's location, the road-advice, and one observation about the lord he is riding to that will help him read the room when he arrives. He receives each piece with the focused attention of someone who knows it may matter.

He rides away better-equipped than he arrived. That is exactly the right amount of help.

failure.body:
You give him more than he can use — too much advice, some of it contradicting the rest — and he rides away with a heavier head than he arrived with. The right amount of help is harder to calibrate than the most help.

You have learned something about the cost of excess counsel.

entries.body:
His knight is dead — three weeks past, in a tournament where a lance went wrong — and he has not yet decided what to do about it. His knight was not a famous man, but he was his, and now he is neither knight nor free man nor sworn to anyone, and the roads all look the same from that position.

He is not weeping. He is past that. He is in the particular numbness that comes after grief has done its first work, and he is sitting at a waypoint doing a very good impression of waiting for something.

responses.label: You may offer him a place in your service for a time.
You may sit with him and help him think through what comes next.

entries.body: He accepts. Not with enthusiasm — enthusiasm is some distance off — but with the relief of a man who has been given a direction. He is competent and quiet and he rides well, and after two days you begin to understand why his knight kept him.

resolutions.label: give him something to do that is genuinely worth doing

success.body:
You assign him a task — real enough that it requires his best effort, simple enough that success is possible — and he brings it back done, and done properly. The numbness has thinned a little. He has found the old habit of competence, and competence is a kind of comfort.

A good squire is his own kind of gift. You have helped restore one.

failure.body:
The task you give is too large for where he is, or too small, and either way it does not do what you hoped. He completes it anyway, without complaint.

Restoring a person is not the same as restoring a sword. The timescale is different.

entries.body: He is not yet ready to decide anything, but he may be ready to speak aloud the things that need to be spoken. His knight trained him, believed in him, argued for his advancement on two occasions he knows of. That debt cannot be repaid now. Something must be done with the owing.

resolutions.label: help him find what he owes and what that means now

success.body:
You ask him what his knight believed he was for — not the abstract, but specifically, what was said. He tells you. The answer arrives in his own words and lands heavily, because it is the first time since the death that he has let himself hear it.

He does not know yet what comes next. But he knows it comes next for a reason. That is enough to get back on a horse.

failure.body:
The conversation reaches toward the wound and then stops — he is not yet ready for it, and pressing would do harm. You sit with him a while and say nothing useful, which is something.

Some griefs have a season, and this one has not yet opened.

entries.body:
He received a sign yesterday — he insists it was a sign, and describes it with the earnest specificity of someone who has been turning it over for twenty-four hours: a white deer crossing his path at the exact moment he made a vow aloud to himself, which he interprets as confirmation. Whether it is confirmation or coincidence is a question he has not seriously entertained.

He wants to know what it means. He is asking you because you are the first knight he has met since the deer, and in his cosmology that is probably also significant.

responses.label: You may take his sign seriously and help him interpret it.
You may ask him what vow he made and engage with the vow rather than the sign.

entries.body: The white deer is a creature of the old meanings, and it appears in the old stories at moments of change and calling. Whether God or nature or chance sent it is a theological question beyond the road's resolution. What matters is what meaning the young man will build from it, and whether that meaning will serve him.

resolutions.label: help him build a meaning worthy of the sign he received

success.body:
You speak to him of the old meanings carefully, not imposing a reading but offering a frame. He finds the interpretation himself, and it is a good one — specific to his vow, clear in its direction, neither too grand nor too modest.

He rides on with the quality of purpose that young men carry when they believe the world is speaking to them. Whether it was or wasn't, the purpose is real.

failure.body:
Your interpretation is too cautious — you offer possibilities rather than meanings, and he needed something to hold. He thanks you and rides on still searching, which is perhaps the honest state to be in after a sign that may or may not have been one.

Faith is not always comforting. Sometimes it is simply alive.

entries.body: The vow he made was this: to ride until he found a cause worth fighting for, which is both very specific and entirely open-ended. He made it at the moment the deer appeared, which he has interpreted as the world endorsing the vow. The question of whether the cause he is looking for exists, and whether he will recognise it, is the practical matter.

resolutions.label: tell him what a cause worth fighting for actually looks like

success.body:
You tell him what you have seen: that the cause worth fighting for is usually small when you find it, that it does not announce itself, and that the primary evidence is that it requires your specific skills and no one else is doing it. He listens carefully, and then nods, and you can see him filing the description somewhere he will use it.

'I think I may have already passed one,' he says. He turns his horse.

failure.body:
Your answer is too abstract, or too specific to your own experience, and it does not translate to his situation. He thanks you and continues his search unchanged.

Wisdom acquired in one life does not always convert directly to another.

entries.body:
He follows you. Not aggressively — just follows, at a fixed distance, as a shadow follows in strong light. When you stop, he stops. When you turn and ask why he follows, he looks at you with genuine bewilderment, as though he does not know why either, but cannot make himself do otherwise.

He is under a following-enchantment, clearly — the kind of working that attaches a person to another until a condition is met. The condition is not obvious. The enchantment is not painful. But if you ride into a town with a bewitched squire following you at forty paces, questions will be asked.

responses.label: You may attempt to determine the condition that will release him and fulfil it.
You may try to break the enchantment directly through your knowledge of such workings.

entries.body: The enchantment has a logic, as all enchantments do — it was placed for a reason, and the reason contains its own release. You must discover what was done to him and by whom, and from that derive the condition. He can tell you the circumstances; you must read the working from the circumstances.

resolutions.label: read the circumstances and deduce the condition

success.body:
The enchantment was placed by a passing sorceress who was annoyed at being stared at, and the condition is that the squire must ask forgiveness from the next three people he wronged. An idiosyncratic punishment, as these things go, but clear enough.

You ride with him to the three people — two nearby, one a day back — and by the third apology the enchantment departs. He is embarrassed and free.

failure.body:
The enchantment's logic does not yield to what you know. You can see the working but not read its condition. He continues following at forty paces.

Some workings require a scholar's knowledge, not a knight's. You have identified the limits of your craft.

entries.body: The enchantment is not deep — it is more inconvenient than wicked, and workings of that kind are often easier to simply brush aside than to unravel methodically. A clean act of will, performed with enough conviction, may dissolve it.

resolutions.label: push the enchantment away through force of spirit

success.body:
You turn to face him and speak a brief authority over the working — not an elaborate ceremony, just the assertion of your will in the name of something larger — and it dissolves like morning frost. He blinks, and looks around, and does not understand why he stopped following.

'I have been following you,' he says, with the confused dignity of someone just waking up. 'I apologise.' You tell him there is no need.

failure.body:
The working resists your will — it is more stubborn than it appeared, or your conviction is not sufficient today. He continues following at forty paces, apologetically.

Faith is not always a blunt instrument. Sometimes it requires the specific key for the specific lock.

entries.body:
He is writing a poem. Or trying to. He has written six lines on a piece of vellum and crossed out four of them, and he is sitting at a stream's edge in the middle of a road looking at the remaining two lines with a despair that is entirely out of proportion to the literary challenge. The despair is about the poem's subject, who is a lady of a nearby hall who does not know he exists.

He is sixteen, possibly seventeen. The lady is probably twenty. The poem will not be good. He needs to write it anyway.

responses.label: * You may help him finish the poem.
You may counsel him that a deed speaks louder than a poem.

entries.body: The two lines he has kept are actually not bad. The problem is everything that needs to follow them, which must be specific enough to be about her and general enough not to be embarrassing. Poetry is a kind of honesty that requires more craft than simple truth.

resolutions.label: help him find the words that are both honest and worthy

success.body:
Together you finish it — mostly his words, but shaped better, the four bad lines replaced with four that actually say the thing. He reads it back slowly and his expression goes from anxious to something approaching wonder.

'That is what I meant,' he says. Whether the lady agrees is another question entirely, and it is the right question for him to go and discover.

failure.body:
Your contributions do not improve things, and may make them worse — the poem now sounds like two people rather than one, which is never what a love poem should sound like. He thanks you politely and returns to the original two lines.

Poetry, like love, resists the well-meaning intervention of a third party.

entries.body: He listens to this counsel with the expression of someone who knows it to be true and also entirely irrelevant to his current situation. He knows a deed speaks louder. He also knows he cannot do a deed for a woman who does not know he exists, whereas a poem can be left at a gate.

resolutions.label: help him find a deed small enough to be visible and large enough to matter

success.body:
You think for a moment, and then you think of something: her father's hall has a problem that requires exactly the skills a squire would have, and solving it would bring the squire to the father's attention, which is the necessary intermediate step. You describe it.

He abandons the poem. His horse is already moving.

failure.body:
The deeds you suggest are either too large for a squire or too small to register, and you end up back where you started. He looks at his poem.

Love that cannot find its avenue is one of the world's most common afflictions. You have helped him no more than the poem has.

entries.body:
He is standing in a field off the road, fully armed, fighting an invisible opponent — and losing, by the look of it. His movements are defensive, retreating, as though something has him cornered. There is no opponent. But there is a quality of genuine fear in him that is harder to dismiss than the absence of a visible cause.

This is the aftermath of an enchantment, or perhaps the enchantment itself. Something showed him something that he cannot unsee, and it is still there in the field with him.

responses.label: You may ride into the field and make yourself real to him, something solid to hold to.
You may call to him from the road and use your voice to anchor him.

entries.body: You ride into the field and place yourself between him and whatever direction he is retreating from. You are real. Your horse is real. Your shadow falls across him. If anything in the world can interrupt the vision, a solid presence in the way of it might.

resolutions.label: anchor him in the real world through your physical presence

success.body:
He collides with your horse's flank and stops. He looks at you with the blank confusion of waking, and you speak his name — or the title squire, because you do not know his name — and the present moment reasserts itself.

He is shaking. He accepts the waterskin you offer. He does not say what he saw.

failure.body:
He moves around you, not seeing you, and the retreat continues. The vision is stronger than your presence today.

Some retreats cannot be intercepted. You can only stay close and wait for the vision to exhaust itself.

entries.body: Your voice carries differently than your presence — it is familiar without being part of the vision, which may give it purchase. You call to him from the road, clearly, using the ordinary language of the everyday: the name of the road, the weather, an observation about his horse.

resolutions.label: call him back with the plain language of the real world

success.body:
He stops. The battle pauses. You continue talking — nothing important, just the ongoing texture of the actual world — and gradually the defensive stance relaxes into confusion, and the confusion into presence. He turns toward you.

He does not know how long he was there. You tell him: not long. That is not exactly true, but it is what he needs to hear.

failure.body:
He does not hear you, or the sound is absorbed into the vision without registering. You call until your voice is strained.

Some places require more than a voice to bridge.

entries.body:
He was robbed — not by bandits but by a senior squire who stole the horse that was his training gift from his knight, sold it, and disappeared into the city. He knows the man's name, he knows the district, and he is going to get the horse back or die of it, which is the kind of formulation that seventeen-year-olds arrive at easily and must be gently redirected from.

His knight does not know yet. He plans to return with the horse before anyone notices it is gone. The plan contains several obvious flaws.

responses.label: You may help him find the horse and recover it from whoever has it now.
You may counsel him to tell his knight first and solve the problem with support.

entries.body: The horse was sold to a horse-dealer in the western quarter of the nearest city, who may or may not still have it — horses move fast in markets. You will need to move faster, ask the right questions of the right people, and be persuasive or resourceful enough to get it back without creating a worse situation.

resolutions.label: track down the horse and arrange its recovery

success.body:
The trail is cold but not dead — the dealer sold it to a merchant going north, and the merchant is two hours out of the city, and you catch him at the first ford. He is not unreasonable; the price is returnable with your name as surety. The squire has his horse.

He looks at it for a long moment. Then: 'Thank you.' Which is not enough but is everything he has.

failure.body:
The horse is two markets ahead of you and each market takes it further. You reach the third and it has gone again, and the squire faces the choice of pursuing until he is three days from his knight or returning and confessing.

Sometimes the recovery is not possible, and what is required instead is the harder courage of admission.

entries.body: The squire does not want to tell his knight, because the telling will require explaining how the horse was stolen — a situation he was told not to put himself in. But a problem faced alone by a seventeen-year-old is generally larger than a problem faced with an experienced knight's resources.

resolutions.label: persuade him that the honest path is better than the secret one

success.body:
You tell him, plainly: a knight's honour is not in never making mistakes, but in how he faces them. And he is not yet a knight, which means he is allowed to learn from this rather than be destroyed by it. His knight will be angry. He will also help.

The squire straightens. He says he will tell. He means it.

failure.body:
He is not ready to hear it. The shame of the telling is larger than the practical argument for it, and he rides off to find the horse himself, carrying both problems now instead of one.

Youth has its own logic. Sometimes the best you can do is stay close enough to help when it fails.

entries.body:
He is reading. Stopped at the roadside with a volume in his lap, a volume that is too large for a squire to be carrying and too densely annotated to be anything other than a serious study. He looks up when you approach with the slightly guilty expression of a young man who knows that scholars and knights are considered incompatible and has not yet resolved which he intends to be.

The book is a collection of accounts of the old kings — the kings before Arthur, the half-legendary period — and he is annotating it for a knight who asked him to summarise the parts relevant to a current territorial dispute.

responses.label: You may ask him what he has found and discuss it with him as an equal.
You may help him find the specific passage his knight needs.

entries.body: He was not expecting to be treated as an equal, and the adjustment takes a moment. He has opinions about what he has read — careful, well-sourced opinions — and they have been accumulating without anywhere to go.

resolutions.label: engage with him on the material as a genuine intellectual equal

success.body:
The conversation is better than either of you expected. His observations correct two assumptions you carried from an older reading, and your experience in the field gives the historical material a practical dimension he had not considered. You part an hour later both slightly smarter.

'Come back when you've read the third volume,' he says. You take it as an invitation.

failure.body:
The conversation begins well and then you reach a point where your knowledge runs out before his does, and you find yourself floundering. He is polite about it, which makes it worse.

There are many kinds of wisdom. Book-learning is one of them, and you have let yours grow thin.

entries.body: The territorial dispute concerns a boundary marked in the reign of a king two generations before Arthur, and the relevant passage, if it exists, is in the older section of the volume. He has been through it twice without finding it, which suggests either it is not there or he has been looking for the wrong words.

resolutions.label: search the volume methodically and find what is needed

success.body:
You find it — not in the section he was looking, but cross-referenced in a footnote that refers to a grant made not by the king but by his regent. The squire stares at it.

'I read past that three times,' he says. You say that is the nature of what you are looking for: it hides in the margins. He writes that down.

failure.body:
If the passage exists, it is hidden well enough to defeat your combined search. Perhaps it is in a volume neither of you has.

The boundary may be older than the records. That, too, is a form of finding.

entries.body:
He has a plan. He explains it to you unsolicited because he needs someone to find the flaw before he executes it, and you are the first person he has met who looks like they would find a flaw rather than just agree with him. The plan involves three separate deceptions, a horse he has borrowed without asking, and the assumption that a particular merchant will act exactly as expected when surprised in his counting house.

The objective is to recover a debt owed to his knight that the merchant has been evading for two years. The debt is real. The plan has problems.

responses.label: You may help him find the flaws and improve the plan.
You may suggest a simpler, more direct approach to the same debt.

entries.body: The plan's primary flaw is the assumption about the merchant's behaviour — the plan only works if the merchant panics, and the merchant did not get to his position by panicking easily. The secondary flaw is the borrowed horse, which is a debt riding on top of a debt.

resolutions.label: identify the flaws and rebuild the plan around them

success.body:
With the two central problems addressed — and the horse returned before it is needed — the revised plan is leaner and depends on the merchant's actual character rather than an imagined one. It works. The squire returns to his knight with the debt, in documents, and an expression of elaborate innocence.

'I simply asked him clearly,' he reports. The truth is more complicated than that, and both of you know it.

failure.body:
The revision does not reach all the flaws, and the remaining ones become visible at the worst possible moment — in the merchant's counting house, with the merchant looking at you both with the expression of someone who has seen this attempted before.

Even a well-planned complexity fails if the plan outruns the planner. Simpler is usually better.

entries.body: The simpler approach: present the debt in writing, in company, with your name as a witness. Merchants settle debts when the cost of continued evasion exceeds the cost of payment, and a knight's name in the room raises that cost considerably.

resolutions.label: accompany him to the merchant and settle the debt simply

success.body:
The merchant calculates. The debt is real; you are a known knight; the squire's demeanour is calm rather than desperate. Payment is made, not cheerfully, but completely.

The squire looks at the documents as though they are a treasure. He says: 'That was not what I expected.' You say: the direct path usually isn't.

failure.body:
The merchant has a lawyer present, which you did not anticipate, and the lawyer finds a technical dispute that pushes the resolution into a process that will take weeks. The debt is real. The resolution has been complicated.

Direct paths can be blocked. Even so, they are usually worth attempting first.

entries.body:
He is lost — genuinely, thoroughly, cartographically lost — and he has the quality of a person who has been lost long enough that the lostness itself has become familiar. He knows which direction the sun sets, and he knows he has been going in circles for two days, and he cannot explain why a man with those two pieces of information continues to go in circles.

He was sent to deliver a message to a village two hours east and has somehow arrived, twice, at the same clearing.

responses.label: You may guide him out of the confusion personally.
You may help him understand why he keeps arriving at the same place.

entries.body: The clearing he described is circled by a mild confusion-working — not strong enough to trap a determined adult, but persistent enough to catch a young man who does not know to look for it. The way out is to navigate by sound rather than sight: the village has a mill, and a mill has a wheel, and running water carries further than forest sight-lines.

resolutions.label: navigate him out by sound and lead him to the village

success.body:
You listen for the wheel, find it, and follow it through the trees. The clearing does not reappear. The village is a quarter-mile further than it should have been — the enchantment was shifting distance, not direction — but the message is delivered.

He looks back at the wood with the expression of someone who has discovered that the world contains more unreliability than advertised.

failure.body:
The mill is harder to hear than expected — the wind is wrong today — and you end up in the clearing a third time. There is a longer way out, by stream rather than sound, but it will take the rest of the day.

Nature does not always cooperate with the reading of it.

entries.body: Two days in circles means the clearing is working on him somehow — not an aggressive enchantment, but a passive one, the kind that exploits an existing tendency. The question is what in him the enchantment is feeding on: fear, perhaps, or grief, or an unconscious reluctance to arrive.

resolutions.label: find what the enchantment is feeding on and help him resolve it

success.body:
The message he carries is to his father's house, and it is bad news — news he was given to carry rather than choose to carry. The reluctance is real, and the enchantment found it.

You speak to him about the nature of messages that must be delivered regardless of their content, and he is quiet for a long time, and then he looks at the east and walks toward it without hesitation. The clearing does not reappear.

failure.body:
You probe at several possibilities and find nothing that unlocks the circling. Either you have missed the real cause or the enchantment is simply stronger than the working you have proposed.

Not all circles are fed by something findable. Some require a different kind of exit.

entries.body:
He has been riding for four days without stopping more than to water the horse, and both he and the horse are at the end of what riding without sleep produces. He is carrying a message that must reach a lord sixty miles north before dawn tomorrow or a man will be executed for a crime he did not commit. He is, he says, going to make it. He does not sound certain.

He does not ask for your help. He barely sees you. He is in the tunnel of a desperate ride, and the tunnel has room for one thing only.

responses.label: You may take the message and ride it yourself, fresher and faster.
You may give him your fresh horse and everything else he needs to finish the ride.

entries.body: He gives you the message only after you have explained yourself twice — he is too tired for quick trust — and then he watches you ride away with something that is either relief or the beginning of collapse. The sixty miles is possible. The roads must cooperate, and you must not rest.

resolutions.label: ride sixty miles without stopping and deliver the message before dawn

success.body:
You make it. Not comfortably — the last ten miles are a matter of pure intention over a body that has run out of willing — but the message is in the lord's hand before the horizon greys.

An hour later, a man who did not know you existed receives a reprieve. You are not there to hear it. You are asleep in a stable.

failure.body:
A bridge is out twelve miles from the end. The detour costs two hours. You arrive in the grey dawn, forty minutes after the execution, and you give the message to a lord who reads it in silence and then puts it in a drawer.

You did everything right. The world was not cooperating. These two things can both be true.

entries.body: Your horse is fresh. You give him your horse, your waterskin, your remaining provisions, and your name — so that when he arrives he has something to show the lord beyond a travel-worn squire on a strange horse. He takes each thing with the hands of a man who cannot waste time on thanks.

resolutions.label: equip him for the final ride and trust him to complete it

success.body:
He makes it. You hear about it three days later from a rider going south who mentions the case: a convicted man reprieved at the last moment, a desperate squire on a fresh horse, a lord who honoured the message because it came in a named knight's name.

You gave a stranger your horse and your name. Both came back well-used.

failure.body:
He does not make it — or he makes it but the lord does not honour the message without the knight present to vouch for it. You hear about it only in pieces, and the pieces do not fit neatly.

You gave what you had. That is the limit of what can be required.

entries.body:
She walks rather than rides, which is unusual for a lady on the open road, and she carries a plain travelling pack and no escort. She is going somewhere specific — you can see it in the purposefulness of her step — but she declines to say where, or why, or who she is beyond a name that is common enough to be a false one.

She does not seem afraid of the road or of you, which in combination with everything else suggests she has made journeys of this kind before. She simply walks past unless you give her a reason to stop.

responses.label: You may offer to walk with her as escort, asking nothing in return.
You may ask her plainly where she is going before you decide whether to offer escort.

entries.body: She accepts, briefly. She sets a pace that is not slow, and she does not make conversation unless you begin it, and she watches the wood with a competence that suggests she knows what watching the wood is for. After two miles, something appears on the road that was not there a moment ago.

resolutions.label: deal with whatever appears on the road

success.body:
Whatever appeared — a man, a working, a beast — you deal with it cleanly, and when you look back she is standing as she was, watching with an expression that is neither impressed nor unimpressed but simply noting. She continues walking.

At the fork in the road she takes the left path and says one thing before she goes: 'You are better than you know.' Then she is gone.

failure.body:
The thing on the road requires more than you have available, and you are forced to retreat with her into the wood. It passes. When you come back out, she is looking at you with the expression of someone recalibrating expectations.

She continues walking. She does not speak of it.

entries.body: She stops and looks at you with the expression of a woman deciding how much truth the situation requires. She is going to a place she will not name to do a thing she will not describe, and the only honest part she is prepared to share is that it matters.

resolutions.label: accept what she will tell and escort her on those terms

success.body:
You accept the partial truth and offer escort on those terms, which is the right response — she was testing whether you would try to know more than she offered. She accepts your company.

The road she walks leads somewhere significant. You can feel it in the quality of the journey, though not its shape.

failure.body:
You press past what she offers, and she looks at you with a patience that is not quite patience, and says she will continue alone. She does.

The things people carry in trust are not yours to demand. There is a lesson in being shown this.

entries.body:
She sits at the edge of a ford with her feet in the water and her shoes beside her, and she is looking at the far bank with the concentrated grief of someone watching a place they cannot go back to. She has been there long enough that her feet are cold, which she does not notice.

Her brother, she says, if you speak to her — he went across that ford six months past and did not come back, and the lord on the other side insists he never arrived, and she cannot determine what is true from the lie.

responses.label: You may cross the ford and investigate the lord's claim.
You may sit with her and help her determine what she knows from what she fears.

entries.body: The lord on the other side is not immediately accessible — his hall is half a day's ride from the ford — and he receives you with the courtesy that has something hiding behind it. He did not have you killed, which means he is not certain of his position. His steward has a quality of careful blankness when the brother's name is mentioned.

resolutions.label: press the lord's household until the truth is visible

success.body:
The steward is the weak point. Not dishonest by nature, simply loyal to a lord who has put him in an impossible position. An hour alone with the steward, pressing gently at the places where the blankness is thinnest, produces the truth: the brother arrived, argued with the lord about a debt, and was imprisoned in the lower tower, alive and well and furious.

You bring that news back to the ford.

failure.body:
The lord's household is more professionally sealed than you can open in a day's visit. You return to the ford with nothing more than you crossed with.

Some walls require more than one approach. But you have seen the shape of the resistance, which is itself a kind of knowledge.

entries.body: She knows more than she thinks she knows — the question is separating the fact from the catastrophising that grief produces. She has evidence, if it is named clearly: the last letter he sent, the character of the lord, the road's known dangers, the timeline.

resolutions.label: help her lay out what she knows clearly and find the true shape of it

success.body:
Working through it carefully, the evidence points in one direction more than another: the lord is the more likely party than the road. She has known it, she admits, but not wanted to know it, because a road's danger is impersonal while a lord's deliberate act is a different kind of weight.

Knowing does not solve it. But it gives her the right enemy to name.

failure.body:
The evidence is genuinely ambiguous — the road was dangerous that season, the lord is known to have debts, and both possibilities remain equally possible. You cannot reduce it for her.

Some uncertainties cannot be resolved by thinking. They require a different kind of action.

entries.body:
She carries a small carved reliquary at her belt, and it has the quality of a thing that has been in prayer for a very long time — the wood worn smooth, the silver fittings dark with use. She is travelling to a convent three days west to offer the reliquary as a gift, because she made a vow three years past and has only now gathered the courage to fulfil it.

She does not say what the vow was given for, or what it cost her. She says only that she is going, and that the road is long, and that she would be glad of company if it is offered without condition.

responses.label: You may ride with her to the convent and share in the journey.
You may ask about the vow and what it meant to give it.

entries.body: Three days is long enough to understand something about a person without requiring them to explain themselves. She prays at intervals with a simplicity that has nothing to do with performance, and the journey has the quality of something that has been a long time approaching.

resolutions.label: walk this pilgrimage with her in the right spirit

success.body:
You arrive at the convent on the third afternoon, and she gives the reliquary to the abbess with a quietness that is complete. Whatever the vow was, it is kept.

She looks at you as you ride back and says: 'I could not have come alone.' You are not certain that is true, but you are glad she did not have to find out.

failure.body:
The journey is not smooth — your company proves a distraction rather than a support at certain moments, and there are hours when she clearly wishes to be alone. You give her what distance you can.

Not every pilgrimage is improved by company. You have learned something about the weight of good intentions.

entries.body: She is quiet for a moment, and then she says: it was given for a life. Not her own. She does not say more, and the not-saying is not secretiveness but privacy, the kind that belongs to things that cannot be reduced to a road conversation without losing their shape.

resolutions.label: receive what she has shared with the full respect it deserves

success.body:
You do not press further, and you do not treat what she said as the opening to a story. You simply nod. She looks at you and her shoulders release slightly, the way they release when a person discovers the world contains more understanding than they expected.

She rides. You ride. The road is companionable.

failure.body:
You say something — well-meant, probably, but something — and she closes the subject with a politeness that does not hide the closing.

Some things require no response except silence. You have learned where that line is.

entries.body:
She stands at the edge of a clearing in the old manner of one who cannot move freely — not bound, not imprisoned, but constrained by something you cannot see. She has been there, her look suggests, for some time. The grass where she stands is flattened in a circle around her feet.

Morgan's work, or work like it: an enchantment that freezes a person at the moment of a choice they were unable to make. She is caught between two roads, in all senses, and she cannot take either until the impossibility is resolved.

responses.label: You may attempt to resolve the choice for her and break the binding.
You may speak to her through the enchantment and help her resolve the choice herself.

entries.body: The enchantment binds a choice, and you cannot make the choice on her behalf — but you may be able to dissolve one of the options, leaving only one road, which is not a choice at all. The question is which option to dissolve, and whether dissolving it is an act of help or an act of taking the choice from her.

resolutions.label: dissolve one road and release her through the other

success.body:
You dissolve the false road — the one that is offered by the enchantment rather than by the world — and the constraint lifts. She steps forward, blinks, and looks around with the expression of someone who has just noticed that a pressure they were used to is gone.

She does not thank you. She looks at the remaining road for a long time, and then she takes it, and you understand that what you did was harder than she makes it look.

failure.body:
You dissolve the wrong road, and the enchantment — which requires two options to hold — becomes something worse: a single unbearable compulsion. You restore it quickly, before harm is done, but she is back where she was and you are further from the answer.

The right approach was not this one. That is useful to know.

entries.body: She can speak and hear — the enchantment binds movement, not thought. You may be able to reach the source of the paralysis through conversation, by finding the thing that makes the choice impossible and making it possible.

resolutions.label: find what makes the choice impossible and name it clearly

success.body:
The impossibility is a false equivalence — she believes both roads will lead to the same loss, and you can show her, through careful questioning, that they will not. One road costs more but is more honest. Once she can see the difference, the choice is not a choice but a recognition.

She steps forward. The grass unflattens. She does not look back.

failure.body:
The impossibility runs deeper than conversation can reach — it is not a misunderstanding but a genuine grief, and no clever formulation can resolve a genuine grief quickly.

Some bonds require time more than intelligence. You will need to find another way, or return.

entries.body:
She is sending a message and hoping someone of good reputation will carry it. The message is to a knight of Camelot — a man she has seen at tournament twice, whose name she knows but who does not know hers — and the message is an invitation to call on her at her father's house, which is three days east.

She is not asking for your opinion. She is asking for a messenger. But she is also clearly hoping that you will say something that indicates whether the enterprise is hopeless or merely difficult.

responses.label: * You may carry her message and give the knight her invitation.
* You may speak with her about the knight before you decide whether to carry the message.

entries.body: The knight in question is known to you — a man of decent reputation, currently at Camelot, with no declared attachment. The message is brief and well-written. Carrying it requires two days out of your way and a conversation that the knight will receive with either interest or polite discomfort.

resolutions.label: deliver the message and read the knight's reaction honestly

success.body:
The knight receives the message, reads it, and asks one question about the lady. You answer it honestly. He says he will ride east before the week is out, which you take to mean interest rather than courtesy.

You have done a small good thing that may become a larger one. That is the nature of messengers.

failure.body:
The knight receives the message and thanks you, and his thanks has the quality of a closure rather than a beginning. He will not ride east. You return to tell her this, which is the harder part of the errand.

Not all messages deserve the answer the sender hopes for. Carrying the return honestly is also a service.

entries.body: She tells you about him with the particular detail that indicates she has been thinking about him for some time: a small act of courtesy at a tournament she witnessed, the way he carried himself in the lists, the fact that he did not boast about his victories. These are not nothing, but they are not knowledge either.

resolutions.label: give her an honest account of what you know of the knight

success.body:
What you know of him is good, and you say so plainly, and you also say what you do not know — which is more than what you do. She listens to both with equal care.

'I will send the message,' she says. 'Knowing it is not hopeless is enough.' You hope she is right, and you carry the message. It is, after all, not hopeless.

failure.body:
You do not know the knight well enough to give her what she needs, and your honest admission of this is not what she was hoping for. She thanks you and decides to send the message anyway, without the reassurance.

Honesty is not always comforting. But it is always cleaner.

entries.body:
She is singing to herself in a field beside the road, and the singing is beautiful and entirely disconnected from the world in the way that grief's extremity sometimes produces beauty. She has woven flowers into her hair — very carefully, with a jeweler's precision — and she is walking in a slow deliberate circle, and you think at first she has simply wandered from a celebration.

Her maid tells you otherwise. She loved a knight who did not love her back, and the loving has gone somewhere it should not go, and they have been watching her for three days and do not know what to do.

responses.label: You may approach her gently and try to bring her back to the present.
You may speak to the maid and learn what might reach her before you approach.

entries.body: She does not stop singing when you approach, but she watches you with a clarity that tells you the disconnection is not as complete as it appears. She is there. She is simply choosing, for the moment, to be somewhere else as well.

resolutions.label: reach her in the place she has half-gone to

success.body:
You speak to her as though the world is still real and worth returning to, without insisting she return on any schedule. After a time she stops singing. She looks at you with an expression that is sad and entirely present.

'He did not want me,' she says. You say: 'No.' She nods. The honest acknowledgment is the thing that was needed.

failure.body:
She sings through your presence, around your words, and you cannot find the right entry. After a time she simply walks away, still singing, and her maid follows.

Some doors require the right knock, and you do not have it today.

entries.body: The maid is practical and frightened and has been watching her lady deteriorate for three days. She tells you: the lady's name, the knight's name, the shape of the loving and the refusal. The lady has always responded to music — she sings herself, when she is well. The maid thinks a particular song might reach her, a song she learned as a child.

resolutions.label: use the song the maid names to reach her

success.body:
You do not sing it well. But you sing it recognisably, and the recognition is what matters — something in her childhood self answers before the grief can stop it. She stops circling. She looks at you with an expression of confused return.

The maid begins to cry with relief. You excuse yourself quietly.

failure.body:
You cannot recall the song well enough for it to register — too many verses mixed, or the tune incorrect — and it does not reach her. The maid takes your hand briefly, then follows her lady.

The right word, half-remembered, is not always the right word.

entries.body:
She is going to the hall of a man who wronged her sister, and she is going armed — a dagger at her hip that she is not wearing for ceremony — and she has a particular set to her jaw that tells you she has thought about this for long enough that thinking is done and she is now in the doing.

The man in question humiliated her sister publicly and has refused all private reparation. The law has failed. Her lord has declined to intervene. She is the last recourse, and the dagger is the last argument.

responses.label: You may try to talk her out of it, offering to carry her grievance further.
You may accompany her and help her achieve the reparation she deserves by less dangerous means.

entries.body: She has heard the argument for restraint before and dismissed it, which means the argument must be made differently — not as a call to patience, which she does not have, but as a call to effectiveness, which she cares about. The dagger is effective only in one way, and that way costs her everything.

resolutions.label: make the case for a more effective form of justice

success.body:
You find the argument that reaches her — not the law's argument or the patience argument, but the argument of consequences: what she will lose, what her sister will lose, if the dagger is used. She stands still for a long time.

'You will carry it further,' she says. Not a question. You say yes. She gives you the dagger to hold, which you understand is a statement of intent as much as a practical step.

failure.body:
She hears you out and thanks you and continues on her way. Your argument was good but it was not good enough for where she is.

Some things are past argument. You can only hope to be close enough to help when the moment comes.

entries.body: Going with her rather than against her is the wiser choice — she will get to the hall whether you approve or not, and your presence gives the encounter a different character. You are a witness, a restraint, and an alternative avenue for justice, all at once.

resolutions.label: stand with her before the man and force a reparation

success.body:
With a knight present, the man's calculations change. He is not afraid of the maiden's dagger — though he should be — but he is afraid of a knight's public witness. You obtain the reparation without the dagger being drawn.

She walks away with what her sister deserved. She looks back at you once. The look says she knows the dagger was not the right tool. She also knows she would have used it.

failure.body:
The man is more difficult than either of you expected, and the encounter ends without the reparation — with the man more convinced of his own position than before, having faced you both and not yielded.

Some injustices outlast any single attempt at justice. But attempting is not nothing.

entries.body:
She is copying documents at a travelling desk set up at the roadside — actual documents, legal instruments, with the careful hand of someone who does this well. She has set up because the light is better outside than in the wayside inn, and she regards your surprise at the sight with mild amusement.

She is a legal secretary to a lord of some consequence, and she has in her possession documents that will resolve a territorial dispute that has cost three villages their peace for eight years. She is going to the arbitration hearing. She would like to arrive safely.

responses.label: You may escort her to the hearing.
You may ask what the dispute is and whether the documents will actually resolve it.

entries.body: The hearing is two days away, and the road between here and there passes through territory controlled by one party to the dispute — a party that has its own documents, less favourable, and a clear interest in these not arriving.

resolutions.label: get her and her documents safely to the hearing

success.body:
The interference comes on the second day — not a direct attack, but riders who shadow you and block the road at a ford. You manage the ford. The documents arrive. The hearing proceeds.

She packs her desk at the end of the first day of arbitration and looks at you with the satisfaction of a woman who has done a thing that needed doing. 'Eight years,' she says. 'Eight years.'

failure.body:
The interference is better-organised than you anticipated, and you cannot force the ford without risking the documents. You find an alternate crossing that costs a day, and you arrive at the hearing after it has begun — not too late, but without the advantage of the opening.

Late is not absent. You have done what was possible.

entries.body:
She explains the dispute with the precision of a woman who has been living inside it for three years: land rights, an old grant, a river that changed course thirty years ago and moved the boundary with it. The documents prove the original grant. They will resolve it, she says, if the arbitrators are honest.

The if is doing a great deal of work in that sentence.

resolutions.label: help her think through the arbitrators and where the risk lies

success.body:
You know one of the arbitrators by reputation, and your knowledge of him lets you identify the specific argument that will be most persuasive — not the legal one, which he will already have read, but the one that speaks to what he values.

She listens, and makes a note. 'That,' she says, 'is why I wanted a knight's perspective.' You are slightly flattered and mostly just glad to be useful.

failure.body:
Your knowledge of the arbitrators is insufficient to be useful, and she knows more about the political landscape than you do in any case. She thanks you for the conversation.

It is a humbling thing to offer expertise and find someone more expert than you.

entries.body:
She has a proposition. She would like you to attend a particular feast — three days hence, at a lord's hall — and represent yourself as her cousin, a knight of minor consequence, in from the south. The cousin does not exist. The purpose is to allow her to attend the feast without being paired with a man she has already decided she does not wish to be paired with, which will happen automatically if she attends without a male relative.

The deception is small. The lord hosting the feast is not the target of it. She is clearly intelligent and self-possessed and has thought this through.

responses.label: You may agree to play the cousin and see where it leads.
You may decline the deception and offer to attend as yourself instead.

entries.body: The feast is as she described: pleasant, well-attended, and containing one man who looks at her with the proprietary expression of someone who has been told he can expect her. The man sees you and recalculates. The recalculation is the point.

resolutions.label: sustain the role of cousin convincingly through the evening

success.body:
You play the cousin well enough — minor knight, familiar with her family's situation, appropriately protective without being aggressive. The man recedes. She enjoys the feast. At the end of the evening she thanks you in the corridor with a directness that suggests she is not accustomed to depending on anyone and found it less unpleasant than expected.

'You are a good cousin,' she says. 'A shame you don't exist.'

failure.body:
Someone at the feast knows her family well enough to ask specific questions that a real cousin would answer easily. You navigate it, but not quite well enough — the man she wished to avoid is now watching you both with the interest of someone who smells a contradiction.

A lie requires more preparation than the truth. You have learned exactly how much more.

entries.body: She considers this alternative. A knight attending as himself, escorting a lady as a matter of courtesy, is not unusual and does not require the cousin fiction. The man she wishes to avoid will still recede — perhaps further, given that you are a real knight rather than an invented relative.

resolutions.label: attend as yourself and make your presence useful to her

success.body:
It works better than the plan she had. You are yourself, which requires no maintenance, and the unwanted suitor departs from the field early when it becomes clear that you are known to several people present and your character is established.

She looks at you with a reassessment that is not unflattering. 'Less complicated,' she admits. 'I should have thought of that.'

failure.body:
The simpler approach has its own complications: without the cousin fiction, your reason for attending together is open to interpretation, and the interpretation that spreads through the feast is not entirely to her benefit.

Simplicity is not always safe. Sometimes the complicated plan was complicated for a reason.

entries.body:
She does not know where she is, and more importantly she has lost something — not a physical object, but a memory, which is more alarming. She can tell you her name, her family, the name of the hall she lives in. She cannot tell you how she came to be on this road, or what happened in the last three days, or why her dress is not the dress she put on this morning.

This is the aftermath of something — enchantment, most likely, or a dream that bled into waking. She is calm about it in the way that people are calm about things they have not yet understood the significance of.

responses.label: You may try to help her recover the missing memory.
You may take her home first and worry about the memory second.

entries.body: The gap in her memory has edges — she knows exactly when it begins and has a quality of sense-impression at the boundary, a smell or a sound that belongs to the lost time without being part of it. Working from the edges is how you approach a gap that will not open directly.

resolutions.label: work carefully at the memory's edges until it yields

success.body:
The impression at the boundary is the smell of a particular flower — one that grows near a specific spring two miles from the road. You take her there, and the memory returns, not all at once but in pieces: a woman she met at the spring, a cup she was offered, a sleep that was not a sleep.

Not all of it is good. But all of it is hers, and she takes it back with the deliberate calm of a woman deciding what to do with what she knows.

failure.body:
The edges hold, and the gap remains a gap. Whatever was done was thorough enough to resist this approach.

Some memories are taken rather than lost, and taking leaves a cleaner wound than losing. This one was taken.

entries.body: Her home is a day's ride, and riding there is the practical thing — she is safe, her family will know more than she does, and the memory may return on its own with the passage of time and the safety of familiar surroundings.

resolutions.label: ride with her and see her home safely

success.body:
The day's ride is uneventful. Her family receives her with the particular relief of people who did not know she was missing but knew something was wrong. An aunt takes you aside and tells you what she knows about the missing days, which is more than the maiden does.

You have returned someone to a place that can help her. That is the most useful kind of return.

failure.body:
The road back is longer than expected — a detour, a delay, a night in uncomfortable circumstances. She bears it without complaint. You arrive a day later than hoped, and her family's relief is shaded with concern about the extra day.

The practical path is not always the swift one.

entries.body:
She is carrying a child — not yet born, but soon — and she is alone on a road that a woman in her state should not be on alone, and her face tells you that the alone was not chosen. She is travelling to her sister's house because her father's house is no longer open to her.

The reason for the closing is the kind that closes a great many doors in this world: she loved the wrong person, and the wrong person did not stay. She is past the stage of grief for the person. She is in the stage of practical necessity, and the necessity requires getting to her sister's house, which is two days away.

responses.label: You may ride with her and see her safely to her sister's.
You may arrange better travel for her — a cart, an escort — from the nearest village.

entries.body: Two days on the road with her is two days of learning how much determination is required to be in her situation and keep moving. She does not ask for pity and does not accept it, but she accepts company, and the company changes the quality of the road.

resolutions.label: see her through to her sister's house without fail

success.body:
You arrive at her sister's house on the second evening, and the sister opens the door and the look that passes between them is the look of two women who have held each other through difficult things before and are prepared to do so again.

She thanks you at the door with a brevity that is the opposite of dismissal. 'You did not have to,' she says. You say you know. She nods and goes inside.

failure.body:
The road presents difficulties — weather, a wrong turn, your horse going lame — and the two-day journey becomes three, and the third day is hard for her in ways that are nothing to do with the road. You arrive eventually. She is safe. The extra day was a cost she should not have had to pay.

You gave what you had. Not everything that is given is enough.

entries.body: The nearest village is an hour back, and a cart and a proper escort would make the two-day journey safer and more comfortable than riding would be. The cost of the arrangement is silver, and time, and the willingness to return rather than continue forward.

resolutions.label: arrange better travel for her at the cost of your own time

success.body:
You find a carter willing to make the journey and two men-at-arms from the inn who are riding the same direction. The arrangement costs you half a day and most of your remaining silver, and she looks at the cart and the escort and at you with an expression that has given up trying to find the right word.

'God go with you,' she says finally. It is the right word after all.

failure.body:
The village cannot provide what is needed — the carter is not going that direction, the men-at-arms are not available, and the negotiation takes time she does not have to spare. You return to her having arranged nothing.

Practical help requires the right resources in the right place. You have learned where the gaps are.

entries.body:
He has been tracking something for three days, and the something is not any ordinary quarry. The prints he shows you are larger than a deer's and shaped differently — closer to a stag, but the gait is wrong, the stride too long and too regular, as though the animal never hurries.
