resolutions.label: play the exchange on his terms and defend your interests

success.body:
You are outplayed but not defeated — he sells you something, and it is not what you needed, but it turns out to be useful in ways neither of you anticipated, and the price was less than the market value because you held your ground at the right moment.

He regards you with the satisfaction of a craftsman who met a worthy audience. 'Come back,' he says. 'I have things worth your while.'

failure.body:
He sells you something you do not want at a price you did not mean to agree to, and you realise it only three exchanges later. He is gone before you have fully understood what happened.

A master at work is worth studying. This was a masterclass.

entries.body: He stops. His expression shifts from the performance to something underneath it, which is the face of a man who enjoys being seen. He does not defend the method. He seems, if anything, pleased.

resolutions.label: engage with the real man rather than the performance

success.body:
What follows is an entirely different conversation: he tells you what he actually has, what it is actually worth, and why he might actually be useful to you. He is honest in the way that people who spend most of their time not being honest become honest when they finally encounter someone who cuts through the rest.

You buy something. It is worth buying.

failure.body:
Seeing through the performance was the right move, but engaging with the real man requires a quality of candour that goes further than you are prepared to go today. The conversation settles into a middle register — more honest than the performance, less real than it could have been.

Halfway is better than nothing.

entries.body:
He cannot do the thing he is supposed to be able to do. This is a very specific kind of crisis: he is a man whose reputation rests on a particular skill, and the skill has left him. He can still do the ordinary versions of it. The extraordinary version — the thing that made him worth hiring — is gone, and he does not know where.

He is not injured. He is not ill. He is in the position of a swordsman whose sword arm still works but whose edge is gone, and no one can see it from outside, which makes it worse.

responses.label: You may try to help him find what is missing.
You may be honest with him about what losing the edge means and what to do with the loss.

entries.body: Something took the edge. Usually the thing that takes an edge is not dramatic — it is a sequence of small things, or a gradual deadening, or the accumulated weight of doing the same thing too many times without refilling what the doing uses up.

resolutions.label: find what took the edge and help him address it

success.body:
The thing that took the edge was fear — not of the work itself, but of the consequences of success, which had become too high-stakes over years of increasing reputation. He had stopped trusting himself to be ordinary, which is the foundation of the extraordinary.

You tell him this. He is quiet for a long time. 'That's it,' he says. 'That is exactly it.'

failure.body:
The cause does not yield to your examination — it is something that requires longer knowledge of the person than you have. You find candidates but not the answer.

Diagnosis requires time as well as insight. You have the insight. The time was not there.

entries.body: The honest answer to losing the edge might be: the edge was always borrowed from somewhere, and what it borrowed from is now empty, and the question is whether and how that something can be refilled — or whether a different edge is available.

resolutions.label: speak to him honestly about the loss and what can be built from it

success.body:
You tell him plainly: the extraordinary version may be gone for now, but ordinary done consistently is its own kind of extraordinary, and the people who hired for the exceptional often end up grateful for the reliable. He has been undervaluing what he still has.

He looks at this from a new angle. It is not the answer he wanted, but it is a better one than emptiness.

failure.body:
The honesty arrives too bluntly and lands as dismissal rather than counsel. He takes it as a confirmation of the worst interpretation.

Truth that is delivered wrong can do more harm than a useful lie.

entries.body:
He is in significant trouble of the kind that money and connections cannot immediately solve: three men are looking for him, he owes a debt he cannot pay, and the debt is of the kind that the men collecting it do not deal in extensions. He has twenty-four hours.

He is not asking you to fight his debt. He is asking you to help him think, because the men looking for him are not stupid, and the obvious solutions are the ones they will be waiting for.

responses.label: You may help him find a solution that the men will not anticipate.
You may confront the debt directly — find the creditor and negotiate.

entries.body: The men are expecting him to run, hide, or try to raise the money quickly. All three have been accounted for. What they are not expecting is something that does not look like a solution.

resolutions.label: find the unexpected approach that gets him through the twenty-four hours

success.body:
The unexpected approach: he goes to the creditor himself, in daylight, and presents not the money but a proposal — an information trade that is worth more than the debt to someone who operates in the creditor's world. You help him frame it.

The creditor accepts. Whether this is better or worse in the long run depends on what the information costs. That is a problem for another day.

failure.body:
The unexpected approach has an unexpected flaw — the creditor has already been given better information by someone else, and the trade falls flat. The twenty-four hours run out in a particular direction.

Creativity is not always sufficient. Sometimes the problem is simply too large.

entries.body: The creditor is not a monster — he is a businessman, and businessmen can sometimes be reasoned with if you arrive before they have committed to the outcome. The question is whether the negotiation is possible and who is best placed to have it.

resolutions.label: go to the creditor yourself and negotiate a resolution

success.body:
Your presence changes the calculation. You are not the debtor — you have no obligation — and your willingness to appear on the debtor's behalf suggests this is not a man being abandoned by his connections. The creditor accepts a structured repayment.

The rogue looks at you when you report back with an expression that contains several things he is not going to say aloud.

failure.body:
The creditor has been burned before by negotiations that delayed without resolving, and he is not accepting them now. Your standing does not outweigh his experience.

Some debts have passed the point where negotiation is possible. Earlier would have been better.

entries.body:
He sits at the roadside with his bells removed and lying in the grass beside him. Not lost — placed there deliberately, with care. He is between courts, he says: the lord he served has dissolved the household for the winter, and the next court is three weeks' travel away, and three weeks is a long time to be a fool without an audience.

Without the bells he is an ordinary man of uncertain age with a face that is accustomed to doing many things and currently doing none of them. He looks exhausted in the way that professional performers look exhausted when the performance is finished.

responses.label: You may offer him company on the road to the next court.
You may ask who he is when no one is watching.

entries.body: Three weeks of road is enough time to understand a person, if you are paying attention, and this person repays attention. Off duty, he is quieter than expected, drier, and considerably more observant than he allows to appear when the bells are on.

resolutions.label: be good company for the three weeks without requiring the performance

success.body:
You manage it — the three weeks are companionable without being demanding, and he arrives at the next court rested in a way that he was not when you found him. He puts his bells back on at the court gate, and before he goes he says, without the voice he uses for the performance:

'You are better company than most. That is unusual for a knight.'

failure.body:
The three weeks have moments where you want more from him than he wants to give — the wit, the performance, the thing he is known for — and he knows it, and it produces a particular kind of tiredness that he had been trying to avoid.

A resting performer is not a performing performer. The difference is important.

entries.body: He looks at you with an expression that is different from the fool's expression — more cautious, more assessing. He is not used to being asked this question directly. The question implies that there is a who, which is the assumption his vocation regularly invites people to abandon.

resolutions.label: ask and truly receive the answer

success.body:
He tells you. The answer is not interesting in the dramatic sense — he is a man from a place, with a history, with things he regrets and things he does not — but it is interesting in the way that any honest account of a person is interesting. He seems surprised to have been asked, and more surprised to have answered.

'I had forgotten I knew that,' he says. 'Thank you for making me remember it.'

failure.body:
He begins to answer and then reverts to the performance — old habit, or self-protection, or genuine preference — and the answer becomes a joke about itself, which is a kind of answer but not the kind you asked for.

Some people have worn the mask so long that they cannot always find their face underneath it.

entries.body:
He is not funny today. He was funny, or so everyone says — the lord's fool for twelve years, known in three counties for a wit that was always kind even when it was sharp. This morning he cannot find a single jest. He has tried. He has sat here for two hours trying to be funny and produced nothing, and the trying has made it worse.

He is not laughing at himself about this. He cannot.

responses.label: You may try to make him laugh.
You may sit with him in the unfunniness and let it be what it is.

entries.body: Making a professional fool laugh is its own kind of challenge — the standard material bounces off, and earnest attempts at humour tend to produce the opposite of laughter in a man who is already aware of what laughter is supposed to be and where it is not.

resolutions.label: find the thing that makes him laugh without trying to

success.body:
You do not try to be funny. You simply tell him something that is true about the day, or the road, or an observation that you make for your own interest rather than his — and something in the accidental quality of it catches him. He laughs. Not a performance laugh. The real kind.

He looks immediately better. The weight was in the trying. The not-trying took the weight off.

failure.body:
Your attempts are earnest and he sees the earnestness and the earnestness is the problem — it asks him to receive the gift of laughter, and he is not in a receiving state. The attempts make things slightly worse.

Laughter that is offered as medicine is usually felt as medicine.

entries.body: The unfunniness has its own quality — it is not sadness, exactly, but the particular absence of something that was always there. Sitting with the absence, rather than trying to fill it, is a choice that requires a certain kind of regard for the person.

resolutions.label: let the morning be what it is without improvement

success.body:
You sit, and the morning passes, and nothing is fixed, and somewhere in the passing he begins to make small dry observations about the road that are not quite jokes but are the beginning of the territory where jokes live.

He puts his bells back on at the noon hour. You have not said anything useful. You were what was needed.

failure.body:
The sitting becomes uncomfortable for you before it resolves for him, and you fill the silence with something — advice, a question, anything — and the something undoes the quality you were building.

Silence kept is a harder skill than most.

entries.body:
He has been touched by something. Not enchantment — enchantment leaves a residue that you can feel. This is cleaner: he was on the road this morning and something happened, and now he is different without being able to say how. He is still making jokes, but the jokes have a different quality, as though they are made from a slightly different place than usual.

He would like to understand what happened to him. He would also like to find someone it would be useful to tell.

responses.label: You may ask him what happened and receive his account.
You may engage with the jokes and see where they lead.

entries.body: His account is precise in the way that accounts of such things are when they are genuine: he can describe everything up to the moment and everything after, and the moment itself is a gap filled with light that he cannot make language approach without the language becoming insufficient.

resolutions.label: receive his account with full reverence for the gap

success.body:
You listen to the edges of the moment without trying to fill the centre. When he finishes, you say something that acknowledges the reality of what he experienced without reducing it, and he looks at you with the expression of a man who has finally found the listener he was looking for.

'Everyone else thought I was performing,' he says. You say: you know the difference.

failure.body:
Your response — however well-intentioned — arrives as an interpretation rather than a reception, and interpretation closes the thing it tries to open. He nods politely and continues on his road, still carrying it unsaid.

The hardest thing to do with someone else's transcendent experience is nothing.

entries.body: The jokes are a kind of theology. Not openly — he is not making arguments — but they are shaped differently from ordinary jokes, and the shape points toward something. Following the jokes where they lead requires a willingness to be surprised by the destination.

resolutions.label: follow the jokes all the way to what they are reaching toward

success.body:
Three jokes, each one broader than the last — and at the end of the third you see what the third is a version of, and it is not nothing. He watches you arrive at the thing and his face opens.

'You got it,' he says. 'I have been telling that for four days and you are the first one who got it.' He seems genuinely delighted. So do you, actually.

failure.body:
The jokes are funny but their theology escapes you. You laugh at the right moments and miss the thread. He tells them to the end anyway.

Not all wisdom arrives through the door it uses. You watched the door and missed the wisdom.

entries.body:
He is performing to an empty road. Not rehearsing — performing, fully, with the timing and the physical commitment of a man before a packed hall, and the packed hall is not there. He has been doing this for some time, judging by the exhaustion underneath the performance.

He does not stop when you approach. He incorporates you into the performance, which means he knows you are there. The enchantment — if it is one — does not prevent him from seeing the world. It only prevents him from stopping.

responses.label: You may try to break the performance by becoming a genuine audience.
You may try to find the source of the compulsion and end it.

entries.body: The performance needs something to end against — a conclusion, a response, a thing that the performance has been building toward. If you can provide that, the show has somewhere to go.

resolutions.label: become the audience the performance needs to reach its conclusion

success.body:
You laugh — genuinely, at the right moment, in the right way — and the performance finds its ending. He takes a bow that is too deep, the bow of a man who has been waiting to make it for a very long time, and then he straightens and is simply a person again, somewhat baffled.

'How long?' he asks. You tell him. He sits down.

failure.body:
Your laugh does not have the right quality — you are performing the laugh rather than genuinely giving it, and a professional fool knows the difference before the sound has finished. The performance continues.

Some things cannot be faked, even in service of the real.

entries.body: The compulsion is woven into the performance itself — a working placed inside the art, not on the artist. It activates when he performs and feeds on the performing, which means it will continue as long as he does. To break it, you must address the working directly.

resolutions.label: unravel the working from the performance while he continues it

success.body:
You work around the edges of the performance, finding the seams where the enchantment is attached and loosening them one by one. When the last one releases, the performance ends mid-gesture, and he stands very still.

He says: 'That was uncomfortable.' You say: 'I imagine so.' He regards you with the genuine, unperformed gratitude that is his most honest expression.

failure.body:
The working is embedded more deeply than you can reach from outside the performance. You pull at one thread and the performance incorporates your pulling, and you stop before you make things worse.

Some workings can only be addressed from inside what they are working on.

entries.body:
He is composing a love song, but it is the worst love song you have ever heard, and you have heard many. The craft is entirely missing — every verse promises a direction and then forgets where it was going, and the chorus makes no grammatical sense in any language he might be using.

This is unusual, because he is a man whose entire livelihood is based on the careful construction of speech. He is aware the song is terrible. He cannot make it better. He is in love, and love has taken his craft.

responses.label: * You may help him fix the song.
You may tell him the song is terrible in a way that is useful rather than just true.

entries.body: The song needs complete reconstruction. But there is something in it — one phrase in the second verse — that is genuinely good, which is the one moment when he forgot to think about how good it was and just said the thing.

resolutions.label: build the song around the one genuine thing and help him finish it

success.body:
You find the phrase and you build from it, mostly by suggesting he say more of the same thing rather than different things. By the end there is a song — not a great one, but a true one, which is better.

He sings it to himself twice. Then he says: 'That is actually about her.' You say: 'Yes.' He looks alarmed in the best possible way.

failure.body:
Your contributions improve the craft and lose the truth, and he knows it before you do. He reads back what you have between you and shakes his head.

'It is better,' he says. 'It is not mine anymore.' He returns to the terrible original.

entries.body: He knows it is terrible. What he needs is not a confirmation but a diagnosis — which specific thing is wrong, and why, and whether the wrongness can be addressed while the feeling that made the song necessary is still present.

resolutions.label: diagnose the song's faults with enough precision to be useful

success.body:
You find it: the song is trying to be impressive rather than true, which is the wrong instinct for someone in his position. He needs to be true, and the impressive part will take care of itself, because truth from a person who knows how to speak it is always impressive.

He stares at you. Then he starts again from the top with entirely different words.

failure.body:
Your diagnosis is accurate but too blunt for where he is, and accuracy delivered badly is just bluntness. He thanks you and returns to the song, carrying the criticism alongside the love.

Truth is only useful when it can be heard.

entries.body:
He has stopped being a fool and become the thing the fool parodies. He arrived at this place gradually, over years — the performance becoming more real than the person, the distance between the joke and the truth narrowing until the joke and the truth are the same thing, which is not a place where fools live but a place they are not supposed to reach.

He is making perfect sense and it is alarming. The sense he makes is about the court he serves and the people in it and the distance between what they believe and what is true, and he is right about all of it, and none of it is funny.

responses.label: You may engage with the truth he is speaking, however uncomfortable.
You may try to help him find the joke in what he is saying before it consumes him.

entries.body: The truth he is speaking is real. It is not comfortable. It is about people and institutions that have power, and a man who knows what he knows and says it without the protection of comedy is a man in a particular kind of danger.

resolutions.label: hear what he is saying and engage with it as truth

success.body:
You hear him out fully, which few people have done, and you take it seriously, which nobody has done. The taking-seriously does something — he does not become less true, but he becomes less alone with the truth, and the aloneness was the thing making it dangerous.

You also give him practical advice about who to tell and how, which is more useful than any validation.

failure.body:
The truth is more than you are ready to engage with in its full weight, and you find yourself doing what everyone else has done — deflecting it into something smaller, or more manageable, or less true. He notices.

Some truths require a particular listener who has not yet arrived.

entries.body: The joke is in there somewhere. It always is. The fool's insight and the prophet's warning are made of the same material; the only difference is whether the jester finds the angle that lets people receive it without feeling attacked. He has lost that angle. You might be able to find it.

resolutions.label: find the joke in the truth and give it back to him

success.body:
It takes some doing, but you find it — the single phrase, the specific observation, the thing that contains all of what he has been saying but in a form that can be heard. You say it. He blinks. He says it back differently.

'That is what I have been trying to say,' he says, now with the expression of a fool rather than a prophet. 'Thank you.' He has his bells back, metaphorically speaking.

failure.body:
The joke that would do it is too specific — it requires knowledge of the exact people and the exact court — and from the outside you cannot find it. He continues being a prophet.

The right joke for the right moment is one of the most difficult creative feats. You were not equipped for this particular one.

entries.body:
He is going to the hall of the lord who dismissed him — dismissed him specifically and not the other fools, which means there was a reason, and the reason was that he told a truth the lord did not wish told, and the telling was the best work he ever did.

He is going back not to reclaim his position. He is going back to tell it again, in front of the lord's new guests, and then leave on his own terms rather than being thrown out. He would like a witness.

responses.label: You may ride with him and be the witness he asks for.
You may ask whether this is justice or just a more elaborate exit.

entries.body: The hall is a day's ride. The lord is in residence and entertaining, which means there is an audience, and an audience is both a protection and a stage. He needs you to be present and known, because your presence changes the lord's calculation about consequences.

resolutions.label: stand as his witness while he speaks his truth before the hall

success.body:
He performs. It is the best performance of his life — the truth sharper for being withheld two years, the delivery refined by two years of imagining this moment. The hall does not know whether to laugh or be alarmed. The lord knows.

You escort him out. He is smiling in the way that a person smiles when they have done the thing they were for.

failure.body:
The lord moves to eject him before the truth reaches its destination — your presence slows but does not stop the ejection. He leaves before the full performance.

Partial truth is still truth. The part that was said was said.

entries.body:
He considers the question with the seriousness of a man who has not asked it of himself. Then he says: both. Can it be both?

You say it can. The question is whether the justice part is genuine enough to be worth the cost, and whether the exit part undermines it.

resolutions.label: help him separate the justice from the performance and find what is genuinely worth doing

success.body:
The examination reveals: the truth he wants to tell is genuinely worth telling, and the exit would be genuinely satisfying, and these two things are separate rather than in conflict. He can do both.

But he could also just write the truth down and send it to the lord's guests without going himself, which achieves the first without the second. He looks at this option for a long time.

'No,' he says. 'I want to be there.' At least now he knows why.

failure.body:
The examination raises more questions than it resolves, and he departs less certain than he arrived, which may not be better for him.

Certainty is not always clarity. Sometimes the question is more useful than the answer.

entries.body:
He sits in the road as though he owns it and addresses you the moment you come into earshot: 'You are riding north when you should be riding south.' He has never seen you before and knows nothing about your journey.

He is a fool, clearly — the professional kind, with the marks of the vocation on him — and he delivers this information with the certainty of a man who knows things that he should not know and says them as though the knowing is entirely ordinary.

responses.label: You may ask him how he knows this.
You may consider whether he is right before you respond.

entries.body: He says: he does not know it, exactly. He sees it. There is a quality to the direction people travel that tells an observer what direction they are meant to be going, and the two often do not correspond. He has been watching travellers for thirty years and has become accurate about the gap.

resolutions.label: probe his method and see if it holds

success.body:
His method holds: he describes three other travellers he has seen this week and what he told each of them and why. The reasoning is internal and consistent and not at all foolish. He has built a model of human misdirection that is genuinely useful.

You ask what his model says about you. He repeats: you should be going south. He declines to say why. You sit with that.

failure.body:
His method, under examination, is too impressionistic to verify — he reads something that he cannot transmit, and the transmission loses it. Whether it is real or performance you cannot determine from the outside.

Some knowledge is in the knower and cannot be extracted. It is still knowledge.

entries.body: You sit with the possibility that he is right. The north road is where you were going. The south road is where something is that you have been avoiding. The observation has landed in a place it was not expected.

resolutions.label: examine honestly whether he has named something real

success.body:
He has. You do not say so aloud, because some truths are private, but the examination confirms it, and you sit for a while with the confirmed thing. Then you turn south.

He watches you go without comment. He does not look surprised.

failure.body:
You examine it and cannot quite arrive at the honest answer — the south road's weight is too familiar to be seen clearly, and the north road's purpose too recent to be doubted.

He does not say anything further. He watches you ride north.

entries.body:
He is performing being a fool, but he is not one. You know this because you have met fools, and the slightly-too-perfect quality of the jingle, the slightly-too-timed stumble, tells you that what you are watching is an imitation from the inside rather than the real thing.

He is, in fact, a skilled agent of some kind — you can see this in the way his eyes move when he thinks no one is watching — using the fool's immunity to gather information in a lord's hall. He has not yet noticed that you have noticed.

responses.label: You may let him know you have seen through him, in private.
You may play along and see what he is actually doing.

entries.body: He goes still when you take him aside and name what he is. The still has the quality of a professional, not a guilty man — he is calculating what your knowing means and what his response should be.

resolutions.label: establish a position of mutual understanding rather than exposure

success.body:
You are not going to expose him — you say so, plainly — and you are not going to help him unless what he is doing is something that should be done. He looks at you for a long time, and then he tells you what he is actually doing. It is legitimate. You both proceed.

A secret mutually held is the foundation of a useful alliance.

failure.body:
He decides you are a threat and deploys a series of responses that are simultaneously plausible and untrue. You leave the conversation knowing less than when you began.

A skilled professional, cornered, does not yield. They adapt.

entries.body: Playing along means behaving as though you have not noticed, which is an exercise in sustained performance on your own part. What you are watching for is what he is watching for, because that tells you who sent him and what they want.

resolutions.label: observe him observing and deduce the mission from the method

success.body:
He watches three people specifically, with the casual attentiveness of a professional. Two of them are who you would expect an agent to watch. The third is surprising, and the surprise tells you more than the expected two did.

You file this information, which is useful in a way you have not yet determined, and wait for the situation to clarify.

failure.body:
He catches you watching him watch, which means he was watching for that, which means he knew you had seen through him earlier than you thought. You are both professional now, each aware of the other, and neither willing to move first.

Being observed while observing is a particular kind of check.

entries.body:
He has forgotten his material. Not a single joke — all of it. He was walking to a performance and the material simply departed from him, as cleanly as a breath, and he is now a fool with no jokes, which is a particular kind of lost that the walking in circles represents.

He can remember what the jokes were about. He can remember the structure. He cannot remember the jokes themselves. They have gone somewhere, and the performance is in three hours.

responses.label: You may help him reconstruct the material before the performance.
You may help him build entirely new material from what the day offers.

entries.body: Reconstructing lost material is possible if you can find the entry points — the specific observations or images that the jokes grew from. The structure he remembers is the skeleton; the images are what put flesh on it.

resolutions.label: help him find the images that rebuild the material

success.body:
You ask him to describe the audience he was imagining when he wrote the material — who they were, what they cared about, what he wanted them to feel at the end. From the audience, the material follows. He finds it, not the same as before, but close enough.

He performs in two hours. You hear laughter from the hall.

failure.body:
The entry points do not unlock the material — it is gone too thoroughly for this approach. He goes to the performance with what he can improvise.

Improvisation is its own skill, and he has it. It will not be what it was. It may be better.

entries.body: The day offers what the day always offers — the road, the weather, the people on it, the quality of the light. A fool of experience can build a performance from any of these, if the panic can be set aside.

resolutions.label: help him set aside the panic and see what the day is offering

success.body:
You spend an hour on the road, and you point at things — an overconfident cart driver, a dog with pretensions, a cloud that is shaped like an argument — and he begins to make observations, and the observations become material, and the material becomes a performance about what is actually happening today.

He performs it. The audience, who had seen last year's version, says it was better.

failure.body:
The panic is stronger than the day's offerings, and what he sees he cannot transform — the connection between the world and the word is not available today. He performs anyway, and manages, and it is not what it could have been.

Managing is its own form of courage.

entries.body:
He has made a joke about the wrong person. Not out of malice — fools are not always malicious, and this one is not — but out of accuracy, which is sometimes worse. The person in question is a lord of considerable influence who does not have a sense of humour about himself, and the fool has twenty-four hours before the lord's men arrive to explain the alternative interpretation of the joke.

He needs to be somewhere else. The somewhere else is three days' travel, and he needs to cover it in one.

responses.label: You may help him reach safety before the lord's men catch up.
You may try to resolve the situation with the lord before it escalates.

entries.body: Three days in one day requires horses, the right roads, and no delays. You have a horse. You know the roads. The delays are the unknown.

resolutions.label: ride him to safety at the price of speed and endurance

success.body:
You make it — barely, and at the cost of a horse that will need two days' rest and a back that will not be grateful tomorrow. He arrives at the sanctuary he named, a house belonging to a lord who has his own reasons for sheltering awkward fools.

He looks at you from the gate with the expression of a man who has just been saved and is not sure what to do with that. He makes a small, genuine bow.

failure.body:
The road presents a delay — a ford too high to cross quickly — and you do not reach safety before the lord's men catch up. He is apprehended, not violently. You follow to see what happens next.

Some rescues require more speed than any single day can provide.

entries.body: The lord is a man of pride rather than cruelty — he was offended, not genuinely harmed, and there may be a version of this that resolves without the fool needing to flee. An apology, if framed correctly, can close what an insult opened.

resolutions.label: go to the lord and craft an apology that restores his dignity without requiring the fool's complete submission

success.body:
The lord receives you — a knight, not the fool, which changes the initial calculation — and you present a version of events that gives him something he can accept: the joke was misheard, the intention was complimentary in a form he did not recognise, and the fool will make an amendment that is itself clever enough to be worth hearing.

The lord accepts. He even laughs, slightly. The fool is safe.

failure.body:
The lord's pride is too committed to the offence to accept the reframing. He thanks you for coming and continues his plans.

The three-day journey will have to be made in one after all.

entries.body:
The road is blocked by a figure the size of a tree, carrying a tree — not metaphorically, but an actual uprooted oak that he has over one shoulder as a man carries a staff. He is going somewhere with it and sees no reason to move for a knight, which is the customary relationship between giants and the rest of Britain.

He is not hostile. He is simply large, purposeful, and occupying the road. He regards you with the mild curiosity of a being for whom your horse is roughly the size of a dog.

responses.label: You may ask where he is going with the tree.
You may challenge him to step aside in the name of Arthur.

entries.body: He is building. A hall, or a dam, or a bridge — something that requires a large piece of oak in a specific location two miles east. He explains this simply, without hostility, in a voice that carries the road's dust.

resolutions.label: offer to help him carry the tree to where it needs to go

success.body:
You cannot lift the tree, but you can guide it, clearing branches at the sides and calling the corners, which speeds things considerably. He accepts your help without commentary, which is the highest form of giant approval.

At the site, he sets the oak down and looks at you with an expression that is, in giant terms, warmth. He says one word: 'Good.'

failure.body:
You attempt to assist, but the scale of the thing defeats you — you become more obstacle than help, and he eventually sets you aside with infinite gentleness and continues alone.

There are problems of scale that are genuinely beyond individual solution.

entries.body: He looks at you the way mountains look at things: without malice, without urgency, with the patient certainty of something that has been here longer. The challenge requires him to take you seriously, which is a question of what you are prepared to do if he does not.

resolutions.label: hold the challenge until he decides to respect it

success.body:
You hold your ground with the complete conviction of a man who will be here when the situation resolves, in whatever direction it resolves. He looks at you for a long time. Then he steps aside, not to your right or your left, but backward — a full giant's step — and gestures at the road.

It is not the deference of the defeated. It is the gesture of one who has decided you are worth the step.

failure.body:
He does not step aside, and you cannot make him, and the challenge dissolves into the reality of the situation — that he is the size of a tree and you are the size of a man, and some challenges are not winnable in the terms they are posed.

Honour is not diminished by meeting a superior force. It is only tested.

entries.body:
He sits at the edge of a cliff with his feet hanging over it, which is both alarming and somehow very sad. He is not going to jump — giants do not usually jump, there being a scale problem with cliffs — but his posture is the posture of a being who has lost something and is not certain it can be replaced.

His name, when he tells you, is known in three counties as a name to avoid. His current aspect is entirely incompatible with that reputation. He has lost his brother, he says, and the loss has made the world feel too small rather than too large.

responses.label: You may sit with him and be present with his grief.
You may speak to him of his brother — what was known of him.

entries.body: Sitting with a grieving giant at the edge of a cliff is an experience with a particular quality of scale. His grief fills a larger space than a person's would, and the sitting with it requires an equivalent space of attention.

resolutions.label: remain present with the grief without flinching from its size

success.body:
You stay as long as the grief requires, which is longer than you expected. At some point he begins to speak — not of his brother, but of the place they grew up, which is a place that does not exist anymore, and the speaking is its own kind of burial.

He stands eventually and looks at the distance. Then he walks back from the cliff. You go with him.

failure.body:
The scale of the grief is more than you can hold for long enough, and you find yourself retreating from it rather than meeting it. He notices. He does not seem surprised.

'Small people have small patience,' he says, without malice. 'I do not blame you.'

entries.body: His brother had a reputation of his own — a giant of lesser size but considerable wilfulness. The stories were not always flattering. But the stories are from the outside, and what a brother knows is from the inside, and the inside is different.

resolutions.label: speak of the brother honestly and let him correct what the outside missed

success.body:
You tell him what was said of his brother — not the worst, and not the best, but the honest account you have. He listens. He corrects two things, which tells you more about the brother than the corrected version did.

You leave with a clearer picture of both the dead and the living, and with the giant's single-handed gesture of farewell — which feels, at that scale, more than a farewell.

failure.body:
The accounts you have of his brother are not the right kind — too formal, or too based on the reputation that the giant himself does not recognise. He receives your words without the recognition.

Some knowledge of a person can only come from those who knew them directly.

entries.body:
He is working. Not the usual work of a giant — not carrying or destroying or intimidating — but carefully, slowly, with enormous hands that are nonetheless managing the task: he is building a cairn for a smaller creature, a deer or a hare, that lies dead at his feet. He has arranged stones with considerable precision.

He does not look up when you approach, but he is aware of you. He is concentrating on a stone that will not sit level.

responses.label: You may help him get the stone level.
You may ask what the creature was to him.

entries.body: The stone needs a different angle. You can see it from where you stand. A small adjustment would fix it. The adjustment is within your ability and possibly beyond his — his hands are too large for the precision the stone requires.

resolutions.label: adjust the stone into the right position

success.body:
You find the right angle and settle the stone, and he regards it with the satisfaction of a craftsman seeing a joint close true. He adds the final stones himself.

When it is done, he looks at it for a long time. Then he looks at you. His expression is something that very large faces manage only awkwardly but genuinely: it is gratitude.

failure.body:
The stone's correct angle is subtler than you read it. It settles less well than you hoped, and he looks at it without correcting you, which suggests he does not wish to seem ungrateful.

You have done what you could. The cairn stands.

entries.body: He considers the question with the deliberateness of someone for whom considered speech is the normal mode. The deer, he says, had lived near him for three years. He had not owned it and it had not belonged to him. It had simply been present in the same place.

resolutions.label: receive his answer with the seriousness it carries

success.body:
You receive it simply, without comment or interpretation. The weight of the answer — three years of company without ownership, the small creature and the enormous one in the same space — is its own sufficient thing.

He adds a final stone and stands. 'It is finished,' he says. He walks away from the cairn without looking back. That, too, is finished.

failure.body:
You offer an interpretation — something about the preciousness of unexpected companionship — and he looks at you with a patience that suggests you have said something smaller than what was said.

Sometimes the right response is no response.

entries.body:
He walks in a ring in a high meadow, and the ring has been walked so many times that the grass is bare in a circle forty feet across. He has been walking this ring for — the local village says — three weeks, at the same pace, without stopping to eat or sleep, and he does not look particularly hungry or tired, which is the working's contribution to the problem.

His face shows awareness of his situation. He knows he is in the ring. He cannot stop.

responses.label: You may step into the ring and try to break the circuit.
You may look for the working's anchor and remove it.

entries.body: Stepping into the ring is the direct approach. The risk is that you join the circuit — circle-enchantments have a hungry quality — but you are not a giant, and the working was sized for him, and sized things sometimes cannot hold something differently sized.

resolutions.label: step into the ring and break the circuit with your presence

success.body:
You step into the ring and stand in his path and do not move, and the giant — who cannot stop walking — walks into you and through you, and the collision is what the circuit could not contain. He stumbles. He stops. He looks down at you with the bewildered clarity of someone who has just been woken by being stepped on.

The ring grass begins to grow back.

failure.body:
The working grabs you when you enter the ring, and you begin to walk — not as compulsively as the giant, but with a tendency that you must resist actively. You leave the ring before it takes further hold.

The circuit is stronger than your presence. A different approach is needed.

entries.body: Circle-enchantments are anchored in the earth. The anchor is usually an object — a stone, a buried thing — at the ring's centre, placed when the working was cast. If you find it and remove it, the circuit loses its source.

resolutions.label: find the anchor and pull it from the ground

success.body:
The anchor is a worked iron disc buried two inches down in the centre of the ring, inscribed with a binding that you recognise the form of. You pull it up and the working collapses immediately — the giant stops mid-step and stands for a long time.

He looks at you. He looks at the disc. He says: 'I want to sit down.' You say that seems reasonable.

failure.body:
The anchor is there — you can feel it — but it is deeper than you can reach, or differently anchored than the form suggests. The circle continues.

The working was placed by someone who knew what they were doing. A deeper understanding is required.

entries.body:
He is composing a gift. It is the size of a cart and made of woven branches and wildflowers, and it is for a giantess in the valley to the west, and he has been at it since before dawn. He regards his work with the anxious eye of a craftsman who does not trust his own judgment.

He is, he says, uncertain about the flowers. There are too many, or not enough, or the wrong kind — he cannot tell which. He asks you, with the earnestness of a being for whom this question is the most important one of the day, what you think.

responses.label: * You may advise him honestly on the flowers.
* You may ask him what she prefers before you advise.

entries.body: The gift is, by any measure, extraordinary — the craft of the weaving alone would take a person days. The flowers are, in fact, well-chosen; the problem is abundance rather than selection. He has included everything he could find, which has produced a gift of sincerity and overwhelming proportion.

resolutions.label: tell him honestly what serves the gift and what obscures it

success.body:
You tell him: the gift needs to show him in it, not everything that grew this morning. Three flowers, chosen specifically, say more than thirty chosen in a sweep. He listens to this with the concentrated attention of a very large person receiving genuinely new information.

He removes most of the flowers. What remains is specific. He looks at it and says: 'Yes.'

failure.body:
Your advice is sound but it reduces the gift in ways that he cannot yet appreciate — the abundance was part of his intention, even if it obscured the others. He adds the flowers back.

Not all advice, however true, serves the moment it is given in.

entries.body: He is surprised by the question. He knows what she prefers, obviously — she is his giantess — but no one has ever suggested that the preference was the relevant starting point for a gift. He has been starting from what he could give.

resolutions.label: help him build the gift from what she would want rather than what he can offer

success.body:
He tells you what she prefers, and it is specific: not flowers at all, but the sound of a particular stream, brought to her in a vessel. He has never thought of this because it seemed impossible. You suggest it may not be.

He is still working on the problem when you leave, looking considerably more cheerful than he did with the flowers.

failure.body:
He tells you what she prefers, and it is something you cannot help him provide, and the conversation ends at the point where the advice would have to go beyond your knowledge.

The honest limit of your advice is better than advice beyond it.

entries.body:
He is coming down the hillside at a pace that is neither running nor walking but something in between that covers ground with alarming speed, and his face has the quality of a storm: not random, but gathering toward something specific. Three villages have sent runners ahead of him.

Something has been done to him — or to his, which is the same thing. The fury has an object, and the object is somewhere at the bottom of the hill where you are standing.

responses.label: You may stand in the road and attempt to stop him with words before he reaches the village.
You may ride to find out what was done before he arrives.

entries.body: Standing in the road when a furious giant is coming at a hillside-covering pace is a statement about either your courage or your judgment, and the coming moments will determine which. You have until he reaches you to find the thing that can interrupt a momentum of this size.

resolutions.label: hold the road and find the words that stop him before violence

success.body:
You hold completely still and you speak his name — which you learned from a runner — and the name at full voice in the path of something that size does something that a drawn sword would not. He stops. The momentum breaks not against you but against the humanity of the thing: the name, the stillness, the refusal to flee.

He breathes. He looks at you. You have three minutes before the fury rebuilds. You use them.

failure.body:
You hold as long as holding is possible, and then you move — not from cowardice, but from the rational recognition that you cannot stop something of this size with a body of this size. He passes you. You ride after him.

Some things cannot be stopped at the front. You will have to intervene differently.

entries.body: The thing that was done is in the village below. Riding there before he arrives gives you the shape of the situation and possibly a path to resolution before the resolution becomes rubble.

resolutions.label: find out what was done and find a way to address it before the giant arrives

success.body:
A villager took something from the giant's dwelling — a small thing, but his. You recover it and ride back up the hill and hold it out as the giant descends. He stops. He takes it. He looks at the village below and then at the thing in his hand and makes a decision.

He goes back up the hill. You follow to make sure.

failure.body:
What was done is complex enough — or the villager has fled — that you cannot recover it in the time available. The giant arrives at the village boundary.

You stand between them. That is all you can do. Sometimes that is enough.

entries.body:
He is looking for a man who wronged him. The man is small — ordinary-sized — which means he could be anywhere, and the giant has been searching for two years with the patient determination of a being for whom time operates differently. He has a description and a name and no face.

He asks you, with the directness of one who has been asking everyone for two years, whether you know a man of this name on this road.

responses.label: You may tell him what you know honestly.
You may ask what the man did before you say anything.

entries.body: The name is known to you, or not. Either way, the honest answer is the safer one — a giant of this disposition who discovers he has been misled is likely to produce consequences.

resolutions.label: tell him truly what you know of the man and his whereabouts

success.body:
What you know is either what he needed or it is not, but in either case you have given him the truth of it, and he receives the truth with the deliberateness of a being who weighs everything he is given.

He nods. He goes in the direction your information pointed. Whether the end of that road is good or bad depends on what was done, which he has not yet told you.

failure.body:
Your information is incomplete or outdated, and he knows it — the half-knowledge is worse than no knowledge, because it points in a direction that may be wrong, and a giant of this size going in the wrong direction is a problem for people who had nothing to do with any of this.

Know the edge of your knowledge before you offer it.

entries.body: He tells you what was done: the man took something belonging to the giant's family — not something that can be replaced, not something with a market value, but something given by someone who is now dead and therefore irreplaceable. The telling is simple and the weight of it is not.

resolutions.label: help him think through what justice would actually look like for this loss

success.body:
The question of what justice looks like for an irreplaceable thing is a question that reduces the two years of searching to its real form: he does not want the thing back, because it cannot come back. He wants the man to know what was taken.

This is different from what he has been seeking. He is quiet for a long time. 'I will still find him,' he says. 'But I know now what I will say.'

failure.body:
The question of justice for an irreplaceable loss does not resolve neatly, and your attempt at the question produces only the repetition of the loss rather than its reframing. He goes on searching.

Some griefs are not available to be reasoned with.

entries.body:
He is old. Not old in the way that people are old — old in the way that some of Britain's older things are old, with the quality of having seen more cycles than can easily be counted. He sits on a hillside with a view of three counties and watches the world with the interest of someone who has been watching long enough that interest has not worn off.

He speaks when spoken to, and he speaks in the old language, though he can use the new one when he chooses. He has, he says, seen this before.

responses.label: You may ask what he has seen before.
You may sit with him and simply observe what he observes.

entries.body: What he has seen before, he says, is the shape of what is coming. Not the specific events — he is not a prophet — but the pattern that the specific events are following. The pattern is old enough that he has seen it several times, and he knows where it goes.

resolutions.label: receive what he knows of the pattern

success.body:
He tells you: this age of Britain has reached the point in the pattern where the great work and its destruction begin to happen simultaneously. The building and the undoing are the same action, and the question is not how to stop the undoing but what to preserve within it.

You sit with this for a long time. It is not comforting. It is true.

failure.body:
The pattern he describes is too large for you to receive clearly — it requires a span of experience you do not yet have, and what you take from it is only a part of the whole.

Some wisdom requires years before it can be heard, however clearly it is spoken.

entries.body: He observes with a quality of attention that is itself instructive: the gaze moves across the landscape neither quickly nor slowly, and it pauses where it pauses for reasons that become clear only if you wait for them.

resolutions.label: observe alongside him and learn to see what he sees

success.body:
After an hour of watching, you begin to understand what he is watching for: not events, but states. Not what is happening, but what is becoming. The smoke on the southern horizon is not one fire but three converging, which tells a story that is different from one fire.

He does not say anything. He does not need to. You have seen it.

failure.body:
The quality of attention required to see what he sees is beyond your current range — the span of what he watches is too large, the timescale too different from your own. You see the landscape. He sees the history of the landscape.

Long sight requires long living, or the willingness to borrow it from someone who has.

entries.body:
He is playing stupid, which is immediately obvious because stupid and large do not go together the way people assume they do, and he has made three tactical errors of exactly the kind a genuinely stupid being would make if they were trying to play stupid and were actually not.

He has set up a toll on the road — a boulder in the way and himself beside it — and he is extorting passage, and he is doing it at the most boring possible level of extortion. He is capable of considerably more, and the question is why he is not using it.

responses.label: You may call his bluff and see what he actually wants.
You may pay the toll and pass without engaging further.

entries.body: If you call the bluff, he has three options: escalate, which is what he has been avoiding; capitulate, which costs him something he does not want to lose; or tell the truth, which is what he has been working toward and needed an excuse to do.

resolutions.label: name the performance and invite the truth beneath it

success.body:
He drops the performance with the relief of someone who has been waiting for permission to stop. The truth is this: he is protecting a particular section of road from a knight who is due to pass it, and the toll is a deterrent aimed at that specific person.

He tells you who the knight is. This is information of some value. You discuss the terms of further cooperation.

failure.body:
He does not drop the performance — he escalates it instead, committing to the stupid act more fully than you expected. The bluff was more deeply laid than you read.

Not every performance is a bluff. Some are the only position available.

entries.body: Paying the toll is an option that he will accept and that will end the immediate situation without revealing what the situation actually is. Whether you want to know what the situation is depends on what you are riding toward.

resolutions.label: pay and pass, watching what you can on the way through

success.body:
You pay and pass, and on the way through you notice things: the angle of his gaze down the road, the direction in which he has positioned himself to see, the quality of his attention toward the southern horizon. These details say something about the thing he is watching for.

You carry the picture forward, in case it becomes useful.

failure.body:
You pay and pass without reading the situation, which means you have spent coin and gained nothing but passage.

Paying for something you did not need to pay for is its own kind of lesson, if you let it be.

entries.body:
He does not know which way is north. This is not a metaphor. He has become separated from the geography he knows — walked three days in a condition he does not explain, and emerged somewhere that none of his landmarks appear in. Britain, but which Britain.

He is sitting in the middle of a crossroads regarding all four directions with equal distrust. He looks, for a being of his size, very small in the way that people look small when the world has become incomprehensible.

responses.label: You may orient him and set him on the right road.
You may ask him about the three days he walked before you help him with north.

entries.body: Orienting a giant is the same as orienting anyone: the sun, the streams, the prevailing wind, the lie of the land. The difference is the scale at which the information needs to operate — he has been walking long enough that the relevant landmarks may be a day's travel in any direction.

resolutions.label: orient him using the land itself

success.body:
The high ground gives you the sight line you need, and from that sight line the ridges that run north-south are readable. You show him the ridge, tell him to keep it to his right, and describe the landmark he will know when he reaches his own country.

He looks at the ridge and something in him settles. He says: 'I know that.' He goes.

failure.body:
The land around here is not reading clearly — too much forest, too little high ground — and your orientation is guesswork disguised as knowledge. He goes in the direction you indicated and you hope the guesswork was accurate.

A good guess and a bad guess look the same until they arrive at their destination.

entries.body: The three days he walked before arriving here are the interesting part — the geographic disorientation is a symptom, and whatever produced the symptom may be worth understanding before you send him home.

resolutions.label: ask about the three days and understand what happened

success.body:
He tells you: he walked through a place that should not have been where it was, a valley that was not on any route he knows, with a quality that made him walk further than he meant to. It was not a trap. It was a place of significance that he stumbled into.

The description matches a specific location you know from the old accounts. That location being accessible suggests something is changing in the landscape. You file this and orient him north.

failure.body:
His account of the three days is too fragmented for you to make sense of — pieces of experience that do not connect into a coherent place. Whatever happened to him is beyond your current knowledge to interpret.

Some experiences require a different kind of ear.

entries.body:
He is sick. Not ill in the way that people are ill — not feverish or wounded — but diminished, the light gone out of him in the way that lights go out when the thing that fed them is removed. He has sat in this valley for three weeks and the local people have brought him food, unsure what else to offer.

A working has been done against him: not to kill him, but to diminish. His strength, which was once considerable, is now the strength of an ordinary man in a giant's body, which is a worse condition than either. He cannot protect himself. He cannot protect his land. And something is taking advantage of this.

responses.label: You may attempt to find and break the diminishing working.
You may ride to find what is taking advantage of his condition and deal with it first.

entries.body: The working is of the draining kind — it does not take his strength directly but prevents it from being replenished, which amounts to the same thing over time. The drain has a source: something that is drawing the strength somewhere, for someone else's use.

resolutions.label: find the source of the drain and cut it

success.body:
The source is a long way away — someone has woven this from a distance — but you find the thread and follow it and at the end of the thread is a knot of working that you can cut. When you cut it, the drain stops.

The giant does not recover instantly — the strength returns slowly, like water refilling a vessel — but the quality of his eyes changes immediately. He says: 'I can feel the ground again.'

failure.body:
The working is too well-made for what you can bring against it alone. You can see the thread but not cut it.

This requires more than one person, or a different kind of skill. Note the form of the working. It will be breakable.

entries.body: Whatever is taking advantage of his condition is in the valley to the east — a lord who has been edging his holdings into the giant's lands for three weeks, knowing the giant cannot stop him. The lord is not evil, exactly. He is opportunistic, which in this instance is close enough.

resolutions.label: confront the lord and force him back from the giant's lands

success.body:
The lord receives you with the wariness of a man who knows the opportunity he has taken was not quite legitimate. You present the situation clearly, with the implication that the giant's condition is temporary and his memory is not.

The lord withdraws. The encroachment stops. The giant's lands are his again, for now.

failure.body:
The lord has already committed his resources to the extension and is not easily persuadable without the giant's presence to underscore the argument. He thanks you for your concern and continues.

Some arguments require the principal, not just the advocate.

entries.body:
He stands in the middle of the road with the manner of one who considers standing in the middle of roads to be his right, which for a creature of his kind it arguably is. He is carrying a sealed message and looking for a knight of certain qualities — the qualities are not specified in advance, but he is watching you arrive with the air of one conducting an assessment.

He is a messenger of one of the fey courts, which means the message he carries is either an invitation, a challenge, or a trap, and distinguishing between the three is not something he is prepared to help with.

responses.label: You may accept the message and see what it contains.
You may ask who sends it before you touch it.

entries.body: The message is in an old hand on old vellum and the seal bears a device that is not quite any device you have seen: almost a fleur-de-lis, but different at the base. The message itself is in three languages, one of which you can read, and the one you can read describes a task.

resolutions.label: accept the task as described and ask the dwarf to witness the acceptance

success.body:
He witnesses with the formality of his kind — a bow so precise it is almost a threat — and then he is gone, which is the way of fey messengers when their function is complete. The task is real and the message is now yours, and the doing of it will take you somewhere you had not intended to go.

The something at the end of that road may be worth the going.

failure.body:
You accept the letter but not the task — you lack the specific commitment it requires — and the dwarf takes the letter back with the expression of a professional who has seen this before and finds it unsurprising.

Fey business requires full acceptance or none at all. There is no partial engagement.

entries.body: He considers the question with the air of one for whom the question is reasonable but irritating. The sender, he says, is one who does not give their name to those who have not agreed to receive the message. This is circular in a way that is typical of the kind of communication he serves.

resolutions.label: press him for a description without a name

success.body:
You find the formulation that works — not the name but the nature, not the identity but the quality of the intention — and he answers it in the same oblique register: the sender is one who tests rather than harms, whose messages have all been completed by those who accepted them.

This is enough. You take the message.

failure.body:
He will not give you what you need to make an informed decision, and you will not take the message without it, and the standoff ends with him departing with the message and a comment about the declining quality of knights.

Some questions are unanswerable by design. The design is itself an answer.

entries.body:
He moves through the road with the quality of one whose errand has failed. Not in the physical sense — he is whole and uninjured — but the task he was sent to accomplish has not been accomplished, and in the economy of his kind this is a significant state. He carries an object that was meant to be delivered and was not received.

The object — he does not show it, but its shape is visible through the wrapping — is important. Returning it is not an option. Finding its correct recipient is now his problem.

responses.label: You may offer to help him find the right recipient.
You may ask him what the object is before you offer help.

entries.body: The correct recipient is not a specific person but a specific quality — someone capable of receiving what the object is. He cannot describe the object's nature, but he can describe the quality he is looking for: clarity of purpose without ambition of power.

resolutions.label: demonstrate that you have the quality he describes

success.body:
He looks at you for a long time with the assessment of someone whose professional judgment is being tested alongside yours. Then he gives you the object. It is smaller than it appeared — a ring, or a stone, or a small carved thing — and it fits in your hand with a naturalness that suggests it was made for a hand like yours.

He bows and departs. Whatever you have received, it was meant for you.

failure.body:
The assessment does not go in your direction — he sees something in you, or does not see something, that is the determining factor. He continues his search.

Not every gift has your name on it. Some things are meant for someone else.

entries.body: He will not describe the object but he will describe its effect on those who have held it: they have all become, in some way, more precisely themselves. Not better, not worse — more concentrated in what they already were.

resolutions.label: consider whether you want to be more precisely yourself and decide accordingly

success.body:
The consideration is genuine and takes longer than the question suggests. What you are is acceptable; what you are more completely might be harder to live with, or might be better. You decide to accept the examination.

He conducts it. The result, and whether you pass it, is its own answer.

failure.body:
The consideration is not quite complete — you arrive at a position that is convenient rather than honest — and he knows the difference.

The accurate understanding of yourself is a precondition for some offers. You are not yet there.

entries.body:
He carries a small lantern that is not made of fire. The light in it is steady and does not change with the wind, and it illuminates things with a clarity that ordinary light does not produce — including, you notice, your own face, which you cannot usually see from the outside.

He is taking the lantern to a chapel three days east as a gift from a court that does not wish to be named. He is going peacefully. The road is not at peace.

responses.label: You may escort him and his lantern safely to the chapel.
You may ask to look at the lantern and speak with him about its nature.

entries.body: Three days with a lantern that shows the truth of things is three days in which you discover things about the road and the people on it that the ordinary light obscures. The experience is not comfortable, but it is not terrible either.

resolutions.label: escort the lantern to the chapel and see what you see

success.body:
You arrive at the chapel. The priest receives the lantern with the matter-of-fact gratitude of someone who was expecting it. The dwarf bows and is gone.

You ride away carrying the memory of what the lantern showed you on the road — three things you had not seen clearly before, each with its own weight and its own implication.

failure.body:
The three days produce difficulties — not dangerous ones, but persistent enough that the lantern arrives at the chapel late, and the lateness has some consequence that the priest mentions briefly.

You have delivered something precious. That it arrived late does not erase the delivering.

entries.body: He allows you to look, which means he permits you to be illuminated — the light from the lantern shows what it shows, and he has been looking at people all week and has developed a particular expression about what he has seen.

resolutions.label: look into the lantern and receive what it shows

success.body:
What it shows about you is not dramatic — it is simply more precise than the usual. You see, for a moment, what you are rather than what you perform, and the two are closer together than you might have feared, and further apart in one particular than you hoped.

The dwarf watches. 'Good,' he says, meaning the looking, not necessarily the finding.

failure.body:
You look, but the looking requires a willingness to see that you do not quite have today — you pull back from the clarity before it is complete. The lantern shows you the beginning of something.

The beginning of an honest self-examination is still a beginning.

entries.body:
He has been transformed. Not grotesquely — the working did not change his form — but he is moving in two directions simultaneously: his feet go forward while his inclination goes back, and the result is a particular awkward progress that has taken him in a circle for three days.

Something disagreed with his errand. The working has set him in opposition to his purpose, which means every step toward his destination takes him away from it and every step away brings him closer. He is exhausted and furious.

responses.label: You may try to break the contradiction-working.
You may offer to carry his errand in his place, which the working cannot redirect.

entries.body: The working is of the opposition type — it is relatively simple but requires addressing the correct axis. The axis of opposition is not direction but intention. If the intention can be dissolved, the working has nothing to oppose.

resolutions.label: dissolve the intention and reform it beyond the working's reach

success.body:
You dissolve his intention temporarily — he becomes a dwarf without an errand, briefly, which is uncomfortable for him — and then reform it in a register that the working cannot find. He starts forward and does not stop. The working chases him for a moment and then gives up.

He looks back once. He does not say thank you. He says: 'Do not tell anyone that happened.' You agree.

failure.body:
The working is more precisely calibrated than you thought — it does not simply oppose direction but opposes the specific form of his intention, and your reformulation uses the same form. He continues circling.

Precision in craft requires precision in counter-craft.

entries.body: The working was placed on him, not on the errand. If you carry the errand, you carry it as yourself — without the contradiction — and it reaches its destination. He does not like this solution but he cannot argue with its logic.

resolutions.label: take the errand and carry it to its destination

success.body:
The errand is simpler than whatever was important enough to warrant a contradiction-working against it: a word spoken to a specific person at a specific crossroads. You speak it. The person receives it with a nod that tells you it was the right word.

When you return to the dwarf, the working has dissolved — its purpose, apparently, was only to prevent the word being spoken. Now it has been spoken.

failure.body:
The person at the crossroads is not there, or will not receive the word, and the errand is still undelivered. The contradiction-working continues.

Some errands are blocked from multiple directions.

entries.body:
He is in love with a woman of ordinary size, which in the economy of such things is not impossible but is complicated. She is at a lord's hall two days east and does not know he is coming, and he is bringing a gift that required three months to procure and is wrapped in a cloth that has writing on it in a language he declines to identify.

He does not ask your opinion on any of this. He does ask whether you are going east.

responses.label: * You may ride east with him and learn about the woman.
* You may arrive at the hall ahead of him and prepare the ground.

entries.body: He speaks of her with the particular precision that love grants, and the precision reveals a woman worth the precision: specific in her interests, clear in her judgments, and entirely unaware that a dwarf of the fey courts has spent three months procuring a gift on her behalf.

resolutions.label: listen to him speak of her and arrive at the hall as his companion

success.body:
She receives him — not as she would receive a suitor, but not as she would receive a stranger either. She receives the gift with careful attention, reads the writing on the cloth with an expression that suggests she can read it, and looks at him with something that is not quite surprise.

You have been a useful presence. What happens between them next is its own matter.

failure.body:
The arrival does not go as planned — some quality of the hall's reception is wrong, or the moment is wrong, and the meeting is awkward in ways that the three months of preparation did not anticipate.

Love's best-laid approaches still require the right moment.

entries.body: Arriving ahead to prepare the ground means speaking to her before he does, which requires care — you are not her friend, and what you say will shape how she receives him. The preparation must not become advocacy, which would tell her what to feel before she has the chance to feel it.

resolutions.label: tell her something true about him that lets her form her own view

success.body:
You tell her one thing: that he has spent three months on something specifically for her. Not why, not what — only the fact. She receives this with the attention of someone who was not expecting attention.

When he arrives, her face has a quality it would not otherwise have had. The three months were not wasted.

failure.body:
You tell her too much, or frame it wrong, and what arrives in the room ahead of him is an expectation rather than an openness. He walks into a situation that has already been decided.

Preparing the ground works best when you leave some ground unprepared.

entries.body:
He has exceeded his mandate. He was sent to observe and report; he has decided to intervene, and the intervention has gone sideways in a way that has produced consequences he was not authorised to produce. He is not apologetic. He is defiant in the way of someone who believes they were right even while facing the consequences of being right at the wrong moment.

He asks you to help him contain the consequences. He declines to specify what the consequences are until you agree.

responses.label: You may agree to help first and learn what you're helping with after.
You may decline to agree without knowing and make the case for knowing first.

entries.body: The consequences, when revealed, are: a lord's prized object has been moved, two guards have been confused about an order they received, and a prisoner who was being illegally held is now free. The containing is required to prevent the lord from understanding what happened.

resolutions.label: help contain the consequences of a justified intervention

success.body:
You manage the containing — the lord's investigation goes in a different direction, the guards remember their confusion differently, and the freed prisoner is helped to a place where they cannot easily be re-found.

The dwarf regards you with an expression that, for his kind, constitutes gratitude. He says: 'You are more useful than you appeared.'

failure.body:
The containing is not quite complete — the lord's investigation finds something, not the central thing, but enough to cause ongoing difficulties. The situation is not resolved.

Containing a justified consequence is different from hiding a crime, but it is not always easier.

entries.body: He considers your refusal to agree without knowledge with the expression of one unaccustomed to this response. His kind expects compliance. Your kind is allowed to ask questions. The tension between these expectations has its own productive charge.

resolutions.label: hold the position: no agreement without knowledge

success.body:
