A patrol disturbed something in the swamp — they do not know what, they heard it in the reeds and ran — and now two of the patrol are missing. The others are at the swamp's edge, afraid to go back in and afraid to leave without their companions.

The missing men went in at the north end. Something in the reeds is very quiet.

responses.label: You may enter the swamp and find the missing soldiers.
You may get the remaining patrol to describe exactly what they heard before going in.

entries.body: The north end of the swamp has the kind of quiet that follows a recent disturbance — the birds have not come back, the frogs are silent. Whatever is in here knows you have arrived.

resolutions.label: move through the swamp carefully and find the soldiers

success.body:
You find them twenty minutes in — they had gone to ground in the reeds when the patrol ran, hiding from something that is now also hiding. Whatever disturbed the patrol has decided you are not worth the conflict.

You bring the soldiers out. The swamp returns to its ordinary silence.

failure.body:
The swamp's silence is not cooperative — every step sounds loud, and what is in here moves faster than you can track. You find one soldier and retreat with him, the other still in there.

One is better than none. Go back for the other.

entries.body: The patrol's account is detailed in the way accounts are when people have been frightened: very specific about the sound, less certain about the direction. They heard something large moving through water and then heard it stop when the patrol stopped. A thing that stops when you stop is a thing that is tracking.

resolutions.label: go in understanding it is tracking you and use that against it

success.body:
A thing that tracks by sound can be deceived by sound. You make noise at the north end while you move silently at the south, and whatever is in the reeds moves toward the noise. You find the soldiers in the space it vacates.

All three of you are out before it returns to the south end.

failure.body:
The thing is cannier than the tracking-by-sound pattern suggested — it does not take the bait. You go in directly, which is what you would have done without the information.

The information was good. The thing was better.

entries.body:
The sea creature — a whale, the fishermen say, though larger than any they have seen — has been in the bay for a week, and its presence has fouled the nets, upended two boats, and prevented the coastal settlement from fishing. The settlement is already short of food; a week without fish is beginning to matter.

The creature is not aggressive. It is simply present, and large, and in the way.

responses.label: You may try to drive the whale from the bay.
You may find why the creature is in the bay and address that reason.

entries.body: Driving a whale from a bay requires more noise than a single person can produce — but boats, drums, and the settlement's collective effort might achieve it. Coordinating that effort is the challenge.

resolutions.label: organise the settlement to drive the creature from the bay

success.body:
Twelve boats in a line, drums beating, making more noise than the bay has heard in a decade. The whale sounds and does not resurface in the bay. It is not harmed — just inconvenienced, which is the most you could ask of the operation.

The nets go out the next morning.

failure.body:
The noise is insufficient, or the creature is more tolerant of it than anticipated. The whale remains.

The settlement's boats are at the bay's edge anyway. Something else must be tried.

entries.body: A whale in an enclosed bay for a week is behaving unusually. Something has drawn it here or is holding it — a calf separated from it, perhaps, or a food source the bay currently contains that it does not normally contain. Finding the reason might remove the reason.

resolutions.label: investigate the bay and find what is holding the whale

success.body:
A shoal of fish has been pushed into the bay by unusual currents — more food in this bay than anywhere nearby. When you arrange for a net to be drawn across the bay's mouth, the shoal begins to disperse. The whale follows the dispersing shoal and is gone by evening.

The net, once removed, allows the fishermen in and the whale is not seen again.

failure.body:
The reason is not visible from above the water, and the bay offers no obvious clue. The whale remains inscrutable.

The driving approach may be the only option.

entries.body:
The storm hit the city an hour ago and has not relented. Hail like thrown stones, lightning that has started two fires in the western quarter, and streets turned to rivers by rain that will not stop. The city's administration is overwhelmed — there are more crises than hands to manage them.

You can see three distinct problems from where you stand: the fires, a collapsed wall trapping people in a yard, and the flooding isolating the infirmary from its supplies.

responses.label: You may organise a response to all three problems simultaneously.
You may address the most urgent problem first and work through them in order.

entries.body: Three problems, limited hands. The organisation required is less about doing each task yourself and more about putting the right people to each task and keeping them coordinated.

resolutions.label: coordinate the city's response to the threefold emergency

success.body:
The fires are managed by the bucket-chains already forming when you direct the guards to them. The collapsed wall yields to twelve people pulling in the right direction. The infirmary route is kept open by two people with ropes across the flood.

All three problems are addressed. Two people are hurt but none killed.

failure.body:
The simultaneous approach loses coherence when the collapse site needs more people than you estimated. One fire gets further than it should. People are hurt who might not have been.

Sequential would have been better. Noted.

entries.body: The most urgent is the collapsed wall — people are trapped, and the fires can be slowed by the rain itself. The wall site is three streets east.

resolutions.label: address the collapse first, then move to the other problems in turn

success.body:
The wall yields to methodical effort — you work until everyone is out, then move to the fires, then to the infirmary route. Each problem is solved before the next is begun. The night is long but clear in its structure.

Sequential is slower but complete.

failure.body:
The collapse is more complex than it appeared and takes three times as long as expected. By the time the wall is clear, the fires have spread and the infirmary's situation is acute.

The sequence was right. The first problem was larger than anticipated.

entries.body:
The storm in the forest has brought trees down across the road in three places, and a family — a merchant, his wife, and two young children — is trapped between the second and third fall, their cart unable to go forward or back. The storm continues.

You are on the road ahead of them, the first fall behind you, and you can hear the cart's horse.

responses.label: You may work to clear a path through the second fall to reach them.
You may guide them to a path through the forest that bypasses the road.

entries.body: The second fall is large — three old oaks across the road — but the trees are dry-rotted in their centres, which means they are lighter than they look. The tools are limited to what you carry.

resolutions.label: clear a passage through the second fall

success.body:
Two hours in the rain, cutting and rolling with the family's help once they can reach you through the gap you make. The cart gets through. The third fall you assess together and find a way around it through the verge.

The family reaches shelter before dark.

failure.body:
The central trunk is too heavy to move without more hands or better tools. You clear enough for people to pass on foot but not the cart.

Abandon the cart and lead the family through on foot.

entries.body: A forest path wide enough for a cart exists — the woodcutters use it in the dry season. In the storm it will be muddy, but mud is passable where fallen trees are not.

resolutions.label: find the woodcutters' path and lead the family through it

success.body:
The path is there and the mud is manageable. The family's cart makes it through in an hour, coming out ahead of the third fall.

The merchant has been in this forest before but not in a storm, and not in a hurry. He had not known the path existed.

failure.body:
The woodcutters' path is there, but a stream crossing on it has flooded beyond the cart's ability to cross. The forest route is not available today.

Back to clearing the road.

entries.body:
The mountain path has disappeared under snow that came faster than anyone expected, and you are a mile above the last shelter with a party of six pilgrims who did not turn back when the signs said to. The storm is not letting up and the cold is becoming serious.

The shelter is a mile below. The way down is partially visible but partially guessed.

responses.label: You may lead the descent immediately before conditions worsen.
You may find local shelter — a rock overhang, a shepherd's bothy — and wait out the worst.

entries.body: Descending in a whiteout requires reading the ground through the snow — the angle of the slope, the feel of the path underfoot, the direction of the wind relative to the mountain's shape. You have done mountain work before.

resolutions.label: lead the pilgrims down through the storm

success.body:
You read the mountain correctly — the slope, the wind, the one landmark visible through the white — and bring the party down in two hours. They are cold and shaken and intact.

The mountain shelter receives them.

failure.body:
The path goes wrong at the first broad section where the snow covers the trail marks. You lose twenty minutes finding the correct line again, and one pilgrim falls — not badly, but the delay has cost warmth.

You reach shelter, eventually.

entries.body: A shepherd's bothy fifty yards from the path — you saw it on the way up, three hours past. It was locked then. In an emergency, locked is a detail rather than an obstacle.

resolutions.label: find the bothy and shelter the pilgrims until the storm breaks

success.body:
The bothy yields to a simple application of force, and inside it has straw, a flint, and a broken ladder that burns. The party is warmed by the time the storm breaks at dusk.

You leave silver for the shepherd when you go.

failure.body:
The bothy is there but harder to find in the whiteout than remembered — you search for twenty minutes before locating it, and the party is very cold by the time you do.

Inside is survival. Outside was the alternative.

entries.body:
The plains offer no shelter and the storm arrived without warning. You are two miles from the nearest cover and the lightning is close enough that staying in the open on horseback is genuinely dangerous. A small group of merchants without horses are in the same predicament.

The merchants are frightened and the nearest cover is a copse of trees to the south — which is, itself, a lightning target.

responses.label: You may ride hard for the copse and accept the risk of shelter near trees.
You may find lower ground — a ditch, a hollow — and shelter there without cover.

entries.body: The copse is southern trees — oak and ash — and lightning does hit trees, but the trees also break the wind and the lightning is moving north-east. The probability calculation is not comfortable but it is not hopeless.

resolutions.label: ride for the copse and shelter all under the trees

success.body:
You reach the copse and the lightning moves north as you hoped. The trees take two strikes before the storm passes — neither near enough to harm — and the wind-break is significant.

Everyone is wet and shaken and alive.

failure.body:
A strike hits the oak thirty feet to your left when you are halfway there. No one is hurt but two merchants refuse to continue toward the trees. You shelter where you are, in the open, which is worse than the copse would have been.

The storm passes. The waiting is miserable.

entries.body: Plains drainage runs in a predictable direction, and low ground means ditches, and ditches mean getting out of the lightning zone — on the plains, height is the risk and you can reduce height by going to ground.

resolutions.label: find low ground and shelter the party there

success.body:
The drainage ditch is where the land's slope said it would be — three feet below the plain's surface, enough to make the difference. Twelve people crammed into a muddy ditch while lightning hammers the open ground above them.

Nobody is struck. Nobody is comfortable. Both of these things are true simultaneously.

failure.body:
The ditch you find is the drainage channel rather than the drainage ditch — it fills with water as the rain intensifies. You must move everyone out and find the ditch again.

Wet and in the open. Try again faster.

entries.body:
The swamp in a storm becomes something else: the water-channels rise and the firm ground becomes islands, and the paths between islands disappear under new water. You entered the swamp before the storm and now you are on an island of dry ground with rising water around it.

The water is not violent — it is rising slowly, steadily, with the patience of physics.

responses.label: You may move now, before the water rises further.
You may find or build higher ground and wait for the storm to end.

entries.body: Moving through a flooded swamp requires reading the water's depth and current at each step. What was firm ground an hour ago may be knee-deep now, and what is knee-deep may be fordable.

resolutions.label: move through the rising water toward higher ground

success.body:
You read each crossing correctly — probing before stepping, taking the long route when the short is too deep. An hour of difficult wading and you reach the swamp's edge.

You are wet to the waist. The island you left is no longer visible.

failure.body:
A channel you misjudge puts you deeper than expected — you lose your footing for a moment in the current and must scramble back to your last solid patch.

The water is still rising. A different route.

entries.body: A willow at the island's centre has a branch-spread that would keep you above the water if the flooding stays below two feet. A rough platform in the branches is not comfortable but it is dry.

resolutions.label: build a high refuge and wait out the storm

success.body:
The storm passes in three hours. The water reaches its highest point — two inches below the platform you built — and then begins to drop. By morning the paths are visible again and you walk out.

Patience, once committed to, has its own reward.

failure.body:
The flooding goes higher than expected, and the branches become inadequate. You must move anyway, in worse conditions than before.

The waiting cost you better conditions.

entries.body:
The storm came from nowhere and came fast — the sea went from grey to white in an hour, and the fishing fleet is out. Six boats, thirty people, and the storm has the harbour mouth closed by waves that would swamp a boat trying to enter.

From the cliff you can see two of the boats. They are running before the wind. Their course, if they hold it, will take them onto the rocks.

responses.label: You may signal the boats to change course.
You may go to the headland and light a beacon that marks the safe passage.

entries.body: Signalling from the cliff requires a signal they can see through spray and darkness — large enough, specific enough, and in a language the fishermen know.

resolutions.label: signal the boats to the safe course

success.body:
The fishermen know cliff signals — they have learned them for exactly this reason. Your signals are clear enough and the crews are watching the shore.

Both boats change course. The rocks are missed. The other four boats find their way to the alternative harbour.

failure.body:
The spray is too thick and your signal is not seen clearly enough to be read. The boats continue on their course.

The beacon is the remaining option.

entries.body: The headland beacon — a fire-basket on a post — is there, but it has not been lit in a decade and the basket needs clearing before the fire will draw properly. You have flint and oil and fifteen minutes before the boats reach the decision point.

resolutions.label: clear and light the headland beacon in time

success.body:
The beacon catches and the light is distinctive against the white of the storm. The boats see it and steer south of the rocks, toward the cove the headland marks.

All six boats make cove by midnight. None are lost.

failure.body:
The basket is too damp to light quickly, and the fire is barely going when the boats pass the decision point. The light is not bright enough in the spray.

But one boat sees it and turns, and that one boat's captain has the sense to shout the change across the water.

entries.body:
The storm that struck the city has a quality the questors recognise: it is not entirely natural. The Grail quest has brought several knights here, and the storm arrived with them, and it has the character of a test — it targets specifically, damages symbolically, and has left the old cathedral untouched while destroying the newer administrative buildings around it.

The cathedral is open and its lanterns are lit.

responses.label: You may go to the cathedral and receive what the storm is directing you toward.
You may address the storm's practical damage before its symbolic message.

entries.body: The cathedral in the storm is a different place than the cathedral in calm — the light is different, the acoustics are different, and the sense of being in a place that is working is very strong. Whatever the storm is directing the questors toward, it is here.

resolutions.label: enter the cathedral and receive what is offered

success.body:
The reception is wordless — a quality of understanding that arrives complete rather than piece by piece. The storm's point, received in the cathedral's stillness, is about what the questors are building and what they are destroying in the building of it.

The storm ends when the last questor has been inside and come out.

failure.body:
You enter but the reception does not fully form — you are not quite in the right state for receiving, and what arrives is partial. Enough to be a beginning, not enough to be the thing itself.

The storm does not end for another day.

entries.body: People are hurt and buildings are down regardless of the storm's spiritual character, and there is an argument that the storm tests what you do with what it gives you rather than whether you recognise what it is.

resolutions.label: work through the storm's damage practically and without seeking its meaning

success.body:
You work through the storm helping its victims, and the storm responds — not dramatically, but genuinely — by reducing in intensity as the work continues. As though the practical service was itself the reception.

The cathedral is visited after the work is done. What is received there is different from what was offered during the storm, but it is real.

failure.body:
The practical work is good but the storm is indifferent to it — this particular storm requires engagement with its meaning before it will relent.

The work was right. The sequence was wrong.

entries.body:
The forest hermit said: when the storm comes in the forest, it is not to be sheltered from. It is to be walked into. The questors who shelter from it complete their task eventually; the questors who walk into it sometimes find what they were looking for.

The storm is here. You are at the forest's edge. The hermit is watching from his doorway.

responses.label: You may walk into the storm as the hermit advised.
You may shelter and accept the longer but safer path.

entries.body: Walking into a forest storm is walking into noise and water and the chaos of branches moving. The direction to walk is not obvious because the storm has no direction — it is simply everywhere. The walking is its own kind of prayer.

resolutions.label: walk into the storm without destination

success.body:
The walking takes you to a clearing you did not know was here, where the storm is strangely calm — not absent, but contained — and in the calm there is something. Not a vision. A quality of presence.

You receive it. You return to the hermit, drenched. He nods.

failure.body:
The walking finds no clearing — only the storm, continuing in all directions. You walk for an hour and emerge at the forest's edge without having found anything except wet.

The hermit says: sometimes that is also the answer.

entries.body: Shelter is the hermit's own cell, which he offers with the equanimity of a man who has sheltered questors before. He does not say the sheltering is wrong. He says only that the walking is available.

resolutions.label: shelter and use the time in the hermit's company

success.body:
The shelter produces its own gift: the hermit speaks for three hours during the storm, and what he says is worth every moment of the storm's duration. The walking, he says, is for those who cannot sit. Sitting has its own storms.

The rain ends. The road continues.

failure.body:
The shelter is physical but not receptive — you sit in the hermit's cell thinking about the storm outside rather than the person beside you. The three hours pass without either gift.

Presence in a shelter requires the same attention as presence in a storm.

entries.body:
The mountain storm on the Grail road is the one the accounts describe: a storm that comes specifically to questors on this pass, that tests something. What it tests varies by account — endurance, direction, willingness to be lost — but its presence here is not accident.

The storm is here. The pass is ahead. The descent behind is closing with cloud.

responses.label: You may push through the storm to the other side of the pass.
You may wait in a lee of rock and treat the storm as prayer.

entries.body: Pushing through requires reading the mountain in whiteout conditions — knowing where the path is by feel and memory and the angle of the ground. The storm does not prevent passage; it makes passage difficult.

resolutions.label: press through the storm to the pass's far side

success.body:
The far side is clear — the storm is contained on the western face of the pass, and once through, the way is open. The clearing happens suddenly, the kind of sudden that makes you understand the storm was indeed specific.

What you find on the far side is what the pass guards.

failure.body:
The pass in the storm is not passable today — the wind is too strong and the path too icy. You retreat to the lee and wait.

The storm must be the waiting kind today.

entries.body: The lee of rock is a specific kind of shelter — partial, cold, but buffered from the wind. The storm becomes a presence to be sat with rather than a problem to be solved. The accounts that recommend this approach describe it as the storm teaching rather than testing.

resolutions.label: sit with the storm as a form of prayer until it passes

success.body:
Three hours in the lee. The storm speaks in the way storms speak to those who sit still enough to hear — not in words but in the specific quality of the cold, the particular pattern of the lightning, the long silence after. You understand something you had not before.

The storm passes. The pass is clear.

failure.body:
The sitting becomes endurance rather than prayer — the cold is too insistent and the mind too occupied with the cold to receive anything else. You sit out the storm and cross when it passes, unchanged by the experience.

Endurance without reception is still endurance.

entries.body:
The plains storm on the Grail road has a tradition — the questors speak of it as the storm that shows you where you are going rather than where you think you are going. It is violent enough to prevent travel but clear enough that the lightning illuminates the landscape in long flashes.

In the lightning, you can see the country around you very clearly.

responses.label: You may use the lightning's illumination to read the road ahead.
You may find shelter and use the forced rest for prayer.

entries.body: The lightning comes at intervals — three seconds of brilliant clarity, then dark. In each flash the landscape resolves completely: roads, buildings, hills, and the specific quality of what is ahead.

resolutions.label: read the country in the lightning and understand the road

success.body:
Flash by flash you build the map: the road ahead divides in two miles, the left branch passes through a ford that is flooding in this rain, and the right branch stays high. You would not have known this in daylight — the left branch looks better.

The storm gave you what a clear sky would have hidden.

failure.body:
The lightning is too intermittent to build a coherent picture — each flash shows something different and the pieces do not compose. You emerge from the storm with fragments.

Fragments are better than nothing, barely.

entries.body: A shepherd's shelter two hundred yards from the road offers walls and a roof and the forced quiet that storms create. The questors' tradition says the storm's gift is what you do with the time it gives you.

resolutions.label: use the storm's pause for prayer and honest examination

success.body:
The prayer comes more easily with the storm as background — the noise dissolves the usual obstacles and something opens that the ordinary quiet does not always open. An hour of genuinely honest prayer.

When the storm clears, the road is different. You are different.

failure.body:
The storm's noise is an obstacle rather than an aid — the mind settles on the thunder rather than the prayer. The hour passes in discomfort rather than opening.

Not every storm is the right context for every person.

entries.body:
The swamp in storm becomes the swamp fully expressed: the water level rises, the boundaries between firm and unfirm dissolve, and the few trees visible above the flood become the only landmarks. You are in the swamp seeking something the questors' accounts said is here in all conditions.

The storm is the condition. The something is here. You are wet.

responses.label: You may move through the flooded swamp toward the accounts' location.
You may wait on high ground and observe where the water draws things together.

entries.body: The accounts say: on the small island where three waterways meet. In the storm the three waterways are indistinguishable from the general flood, but the island is still there — elevated ground is elevated ground in any weather.

resolutions.label: wade to the three-water island

success.body:
The island is there, and on it something that the rain has not touched — a dry patch surrounding a stone, and on the stone a question carved that you did not expect to find in any swamp.

You read the question. You carry it forward. That is all that is required.

failure.body:
The flooding is too deep where the three waterways join — you cannot reach the island without a boat, and there is no boat here. You mark the location and retreat.

The storm has prevented the finding today.

entries.body: Watching where the flood draws things together reveals the swamp's structure from above — higher ground, drainage, the invisible architecture of the place. The three-waterway meeting point is visible from the correct vantage as a slight elevation surrounded by moving water.

resolutions.label: read the swamp from height and find the approach to the island

success.body:
The approach requires a longer route than the direct one — going south to shallower ground and then east along a ridge of higher land that the flood has not yet covered. An hour's work but a dry arrival.

The island and its question are found.

failure.body:
The view is insufficient — the storm keeps reducing visibility at the critical moments. The swamp's structure remains opaque.

Direct wading is the only remaining option.

entries.body:
The sea storm on the Grail questors' route is the one the accounts call the Mirror Storm: the sea in storm reflects something back to those who face it, and what is reflected is the questor's own state without the flattering adjustments that ordinary self-perception makes.

You are on the shore. The storm is in front of you. The accounts say: look.

responses.label: You may look into the storm and receive the reflection.
You may turn from the storm and face the land, seeing what you carry forward rather than what you are.

entries.body: The storm is not comfortable to look at directly — the spray and the lightning and the noise are all designed to make looking away easier. What is reflected back comes through the discomfort.

resolutions.label: look into the storm without flinching

success.body:
What is reflected is not flattering, but it is accurate, and accuracy is what the questors' accounts say is the gift. You see yourself clearly for perhaps three minutes — your actual state, your actual progress, the gap between where you are and where the Grail requires you to be.

The three minutes are worth the storm.

failure.body:
You look, but the self-reflection meets its own resistance — the very thing the storm is trying to show you is the thing you are most practiced at not seeing. You receive the edge of it.

The edge is still information.

entries.body: Turning from the storm and facing the land is a choice some accounts endorse — the land behind you holds what you have already been, and the storm in front holds what you are now. The tension between them is its own form of truth.

resolutions.label: face the land and understand what you are carrying forward

success.body:
With the storm at your back and the land before you, you see more clearly what you are bringing from your past into this quest — the useful things and the things that are weight rather than resource.

You set one thing down. Metaphorically. It is lighter after.

failure.body:
The land offers its own distractions — the road home, the things left behind — and the examination becomes nostalgia rather than clarity. The storm passes without the thing set down.

Nostalgia is the storm of the past.

entries.body:
The storm arrived with Mordred's army two days past and has not left — the soldiers say the weather is on his side, and the people of the city say the same thing in a different tone. Whatever the truth, the storm has complicated the city's situation: the wells are flooding, the cellars are taking water, and the city's stores are threatened.

You are here and you have an hour before the cellars flood completely.

responses.label: You may organise the city's water management under the storm.
You may salvage what can be salvaged from the threatened stores.

entries.body: Water management in a flooding city requires redirecting where the water goes rather than stopping it — ditches dug quickly in the right places can route the flood away from the cellars toward the drainage channels.

resolutions.label: direct the city's water away from the threatened stores

success.body:
Twenty people with spades and thirty minutes of coordinated work. The water finds the channels you gave it and the cellars stay dry enough. The stores are intact.

The work was unglamorous and essential, which is most of what needs doing in most emergencies.

failure.body:
The water is faster than the digging. The cellars take water before the channels are complete.

Salvage is the remaining option.

entries.body: Salvaging from a flooding cellar requires moving quickly and choosing well — not everything can be saved, and the time available is fixed by the water's rate of rise.

resolutions.label: salvage what matters most from the flooding stores

success.body:
You know immediately what to prioritise: the grain over the wine, the preserved meat over the root vegetables, the medicine over everything except the grain. In forty minutes you have moved what matters.

The cellars flood. The stores are diminished but not lost.

failure.body:
The choices in the moment are harder than they seemed from outside — each barrel looks equally important when you are carrying it. You save some and lose more than you should have.

Prioritisation under pressure is a skill that requires practice.

entries.body:
The storm in the forest has been used by Mordred's forces as cover for a flanking movement — the noise and rain concealing the sound of a column moving through the trees. You have spotted the movement, but the storm makes communication with Arthur's forces difficult.

You have the information. The question is how to use it.

responses.label: You may ride back through the storm to warn Arthur's forces directly.
You may find a way to slow the flanking column while others are warned.

entries.body: The ride back through the storm is two miles of forest in poor visibility at speed. Your horse knows this kind of riding; the question is whether you can hold the direction.

resolutions.label: ride back through the storm with the warning

success.body:
You arrive with the warning and you arrive in time. Arthur's flank is reinforced before the column emerges from the treeline. The storm that hid Mordred's movement has hidden your ride as well.

The flanking move fails.

failure.body:
You arrive, but barely too late — the column has already begun to emerge. Your warning still has value but the advantage is gone.

Warnings still matter even when they arrive late.

entries.body: Slowing a column means using the terrain — fallen trees, mud, wrong directions given to scouts, anything that buys the quarter-hour that a warning rider needs.

resolutions.label: delay the column through the forest while the warning rides

success.body:
You give the scouts the wrong direction at a fork with convincing confidence, and the column takes twenty minutes to backtrack. Twenty minutes is more than enough.

The warning arrives. The flank holds.

failure.body:
The scouts are better than you expected — they correct the misdirection in minutes. The delay is insufficient.

The ride was the right choice all along.

entries.body:
The mountain storm has closed the pass for two days, and on both sides of the pass there are soldiers waiting — not enemy soldiers to each other, but soldiers from both sides of the war, separated by the snow and waiting for the pass to clear before they become enemies again.

They are sharing a mountain shelter. They have been in it for two days.

responses.label: You may enter the shelter and work with both groups through the storm.
You may remain at the pass and wait for it to open, watching from outside.

entries.body: Two dozen soldiers from opposing sides, shared food running low, and a pass that will open eventually. The situation requires that the opening not begin with a resumption of violence.

resolutions.label: keep the peace in the shelter through the storm

success.body:
The storm creates a temporary human logic that the war has suspended. You facilitate it — not peace, exactly, but the recognition that the men in the shelter are men in a shelter, and the war can wait until the pass opens. It does wait.

Three of the soldiers do not continue after the pass opens. They go home instead.

failure.body:
A fight starts over something small — food, a word, a look — and you must intervene physically before it becomes worse. You stop it, but the shelter is now a fragile thing.

Peace without foundation requires constant maintenance.

entries.body: Watching from outside — cold, but outside — lets you see the shelter from a perspective its occupants cannot have. You can see when the pass will open and can time the groups' departures so they don't encounter each other immediately.

resolutions.label: time the pass-opening to separate the two groups

success.body:
You read the weather and enter the shelter with an hour's notice. You send one group early — giving them a reason — and the other group leaves when that group is out of sight.

The pass opens. Neither group sees the other. The violence does not resume today.

failure.body:
The pass opens faster than expected and both groups begin to move simultaneously. You manage the meeting, but it is tense and the knife-edge of it is not comfortable.

Management under pressure is a skill. You have it, barely.

entries.body:
The storm on the plains has caught a messenger riding urgent dispatches between Arthur's remaining forces. She has taken shelter in a farmhouse, but her horse has a foreleg cut from the debris that took them both down, and she has three hours before the dispatches are critical and no horse to ride.

The farms around here have horses. She has no time to negotiate.

responses.label: You may lend her your horse and walk to find another.
You may negotiate with the nearest farmer for a horse on her behalf.

entries.body: Your horse is sound and the messenger is a skilled rider. The mathematics of this situation are not complicated: she needs a horse, you have one, the dispatches matter.

resolutions.label: give her your horse and see yourself right afterward

success.body:
She rides. You walk to the nearest farm and explain the situation, and the farmer — who has a regard for Arthur's remaining cause — lends you a horse for the return.

The dispatches reach their destination. The farmer's horse is returned in good condition.

failure.body:
The farmer has no horse to spare — his were taken by Mordred's requisition officers a week past. You manage, but the walk in the storm is long.

The dispatches still get through. You still get wet.

entries.body: The farmer two fields over has horses — you can see them in the storm, miserable in their field. The farmer will be reluctant to lend to a stranger in an emergency, even a well-stated one.

resolutions.label: negotiate the loan of a horse against all reasonable objection

success.body:
The combination of your name, the messenger's credentials, and a pledge of return in good condition carries the day. The farmer yields. The messenger rides within the hour.

The storm continues. The dispatches do not.

failure.body:
The farmer's hesitation is well-founded — his horses have been borrowed before and not returned — and your assurances are not sufficient. The loan is refused.

Your own horse must be the answer.

entries.body:
The swamp storm has sealed off a monastery on an island within the swamp — the bridge flooded two days ago and has not emerged. The monks have food for a week but the abbot is ill, and the medicine he needs is not in the monastery.

You are at the swamp's edge with the medicine. The bridge is under three feet of water.

responses.label: You may wade or swim across to the island.
You may build or find a means of crossing that does not require swimming.

entries.body: Three feet of water over a bridge is manageable if the bridge is still there — and it is, you can feel it underfoot if you go carefully. The current above the bridge is the problem.

resolutions.label: wade the flooded bridge to the island

success.body:
The current is strong but the bridge holds and your footing holds and you reach the island with the medicine sealed in oilcloth and dry enough to use.

The abbot is treated. The storm seems inconsequential from inside the monastery's walls.

failure.body:
The current takes your footing at the bridge's midpoint and you go in. You reach the far bank but the medicine is wet — the oilcloth failed. Most of it is still usable.

Most is better than none.

entries.body: The monastery has a bell-rope that a monk could throw from the island's edge — and if a rope can be attached to either side, a sling can carry the medicine across above the water.

resolutions.label: arrange a rope crossing for the medicine

success.body:
You call across to the monks, who understand the plan and throw a line. Fifteen minutes of arrangement and the medicine crosses dry, in a sling above the current.

The monks receive it with the ceremony appropriate to the receiving.

failure.body:
The throw falls short and the current carries the line downstream before it can be retrieved. The rope solution fails.

The wading approach remains.

entries.body:
The sea storm has pushed a supply ship off course and onto a shoal within sight of shore — the ship is not sinking but it cannot move, and its cargo of provisions was intended for Arthur's remaining coastal garrison. The storm is too strong for the fishing boats to go out.

The ship's crew are visible from the shore, waving.

responses.label: You may find a way to reach the ship through the storm.
You may organise the shore to receive the cargo if the crew can get it off.

entries.body: Reaching a stranded ship in storm requires a boat that can handle the conditions, and the fishing boats are not that boat. But the harbour has one vessel — the harbour master's own — that is heavier and might manage the distance.

resolutions.label: take the harbour master's boat through the storm to the ship

success.body:
The boat handles the storm badly but survivably — you reach the ship, take on as much cargo as the boat can carry, and return. Three trips before the storm worsens enough to stop.

A third of the cargo is ashore. Two-thirds remains. That third feeds the garrison for another week.

failure.body:
The storm is too strong for the harbour master's boat and you must turn back before reaching the ship. The cargo remains on the shoal.

The storm must weaken before the crossing is possible.

entries.body: The crew can use the ship's own boat to get cargo to the shore — small loads, multiple trips, in the lee of the shoal where the water is calmer. The shore must be organised to receive quickly so the boat can return for more.

resolutions.label: organise the shore reception to maximise each trip

success.body:
Twelve people in a chain, a cart waiting, everything cleared between trips. The crew makes seven trips before they judge the weather too bad for another. Enough cargo is ashore to matter.

The garrison is fed. The crew sleeps on the ship and comes in when the storm breaks.

failure.body:
The shore organisation is slower than the crew's trips require, and cargo sits waiting to be moved when the next load arrives. The inefficiency costs two trips' worth of cargo.

Coordination is a skill that looks easy and is not.

entries.body:
Four armed men have blocked the road through the city's outer district — not openly, but effectively: they occupy the narrow stretch between two warehouses and they are charging passage. The merchants waiting behind you have been here an hour.

The men are armed and organised in the way of people who do this regularly. They have not threatened violence, only implied it through the nature of the situation.

responses.label: You may challenge them directly and clear the road.
You may find the lord whose district this is and report the toll before acting.

entries.body: Four men with the confidence of habitual success against unarmed merchants. Against a knight they will calculate differently — the question is whether they calculate fast enough.

resolutions.label: challenge the brigands and clear the road by force or its threat

success.body:
The challenge changes the calculation and two of them accept the new arithmetic immediately, departing into the warehouses. The remaining two make a show of resistance before following. The road is open.

The merchants thank you and move through quickly before a second calculation is made.

failure.body:
They have friends inside the warehouses and the confrontation becomes more complicated than you anticipated. You hold the road but sustain a cut doing it.

The road is open. They are gone but not caught.

entries.body: The lord's district office is three streets away, and the toll operation is illegal under his jurisdiction. A lord informed of illegal toll-taking in his district generally responds with enthusiasm, because the enforcement is his right and the brigands are cutting into his own revenue.

resolutions.label: report to the lord and let his authority clear the road

success.body:
The lord's men arrive within twenty minutes and the toll-men disperse before the question of arrest becomes relevant. The road is clear and the lord has dealt with a problem at no cost to himself, which suits everyone.

The merchants are still an hour behind schedule. They do not complain.

failure.body:
The lord's men are otherwise occupied, and the response takes longer than the situation can wait. The merchants begin paying the toll individually — it is cheaper than another hour lost.

The legal approach was right. The timing was wrong.

entries.body:
The forest road has been watched for three days, and today the watchers have collected their toll: a travelling family, their cart, and their horse are being held at the side of the road while the brigands discuss their options. The family is frightened.

There are six brigands. They have not yet done harm. The family has nothing worth the effort if it becomes an effort.

responses.label: You may approach openly and insert yourself between the brigands and the family.
You may circle through the forest and approach from the brigands' rear.

entries.body: An open approach changes the situation immediately — a mounted knight is a new variable that changes the brigands' cost-benefit calculation. The family is watching you come with expressions ranging from hope to terror.

resolutions.label: ride between the brigands and the family and hold the position

success.body:
The position is held. The brigands reassess. Your presence as witness — a knight who has seen them, knows their faces, and has not yet decided what to do about it — is worth more than the family's cart.

They go. The family goes. You go.

failure.body:
Six men have a different calculation than four. Three of them circle around you while three address your front. The family gets away in the confusion, which is the thing that mattered.

You extract yourself. It is not comfortable.

entries.body: Coming from the rear requires getting through the forest quietly and quickly, which is possible. Six men watching a road watch the road — the trees behind them are not their concern.

resolutions.label: circle through the forest and take the brigands from their unguarded side

success.body:
You emerge from the trees behind them before they know you have been in the trees, and at that moment the calculation is entirely different: flanked, surprised, facing a knight who knows they have not seen him coming. They go into the forest before the situation worsens.

The family watches this with the expression of people who cannot explain what just happened.

failure.body:
The forest is denser than you estimated and the circuit takes too long. When you emerge, the situation has changed — the brigands have moved the family and the road is empty.

You follow the tracks.

entries.body:
The mountain pass has been claimed by a band that numbers perhaps a dozen and is well-organised enough to have maintained the claim for six months. They know the passes, they know the lookout points, and they know the difference between travellers who can be held and travellers who cannot.

They have stopped you at the upper gatehouse, politely, with their hand extended.

responses.label: You may pay and pass without incident.
You may refuse and hold your ground.

entries.body: Paying is a choice that resolves the immediate situation without resolving the larger one — the pass remains theirs after you have passed, and the next traveller faces the same situation. But you will be through, and through is what you need to be.

resolutions.label: pay the toll and pass, noting what you have seen for later report

success.body:
You pay and you note: twelve men, two lookout posts, one main gatehouse, a rough camp on the southern approach. The lord of the next valley will want this information, and you carry it cleanly.

The pass is behind you. The information is ahead of you.

failure.body:
They charge more than you have, or the negotiation of what constitutes 'payment' takes longer and uglier turns than you wanted. You pass eventually, but not cleanly.

The information is still good.

entries.body: Refusing changes the situation to one of force or of standoff. They have twelve men and a defensible position. You have a horse and the moral authority of being on the right side of the law.

resolutions.label: hold your ground until they yield or you find another way

success.body:
Their leader is a man who respects force and respects resolution with equal regard. You are resolute without being aggressive, and after a long standoff he makes a calculation: a knight who holds his ground without attacking is a knight who will also report accurately. He steps aside.

'Next time,' he says. You say: there will not be a next time.

failure.body:
Twelve men and a gatehouse is a more defensible position than your resolution can overcome alone. You reach an impasse that resolves with you going back down the mountain.

The other pass is two days further.

entries.body:
The plains are wide enough that robbery is difficult — there is nowhere to set an ambush, no chokepoint to hold. These brigands have solved the problem by using horses: they ride alongside travellers and make it clear that stopping is optional but the consequences of not stopping are also clear.

There are eight of them and they are riding alongside you now.

responses.label: You may outride them — your horse against theirs.
You may turn and face them, making stopping your own choice rather than theirs.

entries.body: Eight riders who have been doing this for some time will have horses selected for exactly this kind of pursuit. Your horse is a knight's horse — built for power, not distance. The mathematics may or may not work.

resolutions.label: ride hard and outpace the brigands

success.body:
Your horse is better than they expected and you push it harder than they anticipated. The gap opens over a mile and by the second mile they have given up — there are easier targets than a knight who knows how to ride.

You arrive at the next settlement lathered and intact.

failure.body:
Their horses are better on the flat than yours. The gap narrows instead of opening. You stop — stopping on your own terms is better than being stopped.

Face them now from a position of choice.

entries.body: Turning to face them changes the situation from pursuit to confrontation, and confrontation gives you control that running does not. Eight men against one knight still favours the eight, but a knight who chooses to face you is a different proposition than one who runs.

resolutions.label: turn and face them on your own terms

success.body:
The leader reads the situation correctly: a knight who faces eight without flinching has either calculated the odds in his own favour or is not calculating at all, and either is troublesome. He wheels his riders off and they go west.

The plains are yours.

failure.body:
Their leader calculates differently than you hoped and they do not wheel off. The encounter that follows is a fight you survive but do not win cleanly.

Survival, in this case, is the whole of what was possible.

entries.body:
The swamp has a reputation that serves the brigands well — most travellers who disappear in the swamp are assumed to have drowned, not robbed. The three men who have been working this stretch of path are efficient and largely invisible.

You have seen them before they have seen you. You have a choice in this.

responses.label: You may use your advantage and disarm the situation before it begins.
You may walk into their ambush and face them on the other side of surprise.

entries.body: You have seen them and they have not seen you. What you do with this advantage determines how the encounter goes. A demonstration that they have been observed may be sufficient.

resolutions.label: use your prior knowledge to defuse the ambush before it springs

success.body:
You call their names — invented, but specific-sounding — from behind a screen of reed before you walk the path. The confidence of your knowing changes their calculation: someone who knows you are there is not someone to rob.

They let you through without emerging. You never see their faces.

failure.body:
They know you do not know their names, which means the false confidence reads as exactly what it is. The ambush springs as planned.

The walking-in approach was always the real option.

entries.body: Walking into a known ambush is a choice — one that gives you the freedom to respond rather than react when it springs. Three men in the swamp are not insurmountable, if you are ready.

resolutions.label: walk into the ambush ready to meet it

success.body:
When they emerge from the reeds you are already positioned, and the readiness shows in you in a way that changes the encounter from robbery to negotiation. You resolve it without injury on either side.

The swamp path is clear.

failure.body:
The swamp is not ideal ground for this kind of preparedness — your footing is uncertain and their knowledge of the terrain is better. You manage the encounter but not cleanly.

Knowing is not the same as having the ground.

entries.body:
The sea cliffs provide cover that the road does not — you can approach the road from above, unseen, and what you can see from the cliff is six men at a coastal waystation, relieving a ship's messenger of a sealed packet that was intended for Camelot.

The messenger is outnumbered and on the ground. The packet is in a brigand's hand.

responses.label: You may descend the cliff and intervene directly.
You may call down to them from the cliff, using your height as authority.

entries.body: The cliff descent is not gradual — it is a scramble, and you will arrive at the waystation already committed to the encounter before you have had time to assess their full number and disposition.

resolutions.label: descend and take the packet back

success.body:
The surprise of the descent counts for something. You are among them before they know where you came from, and the packet is back in the messenger's hands before the situation fully resolves.

The messenger rides for Camelot. You see him to the road.

failure.body:
Six men absorb the surprise better than expected. The encounter is harder than the descent suggested it would be.

You hold the waystation but the packet has gone with the leader while you dealt with the others.

entries.body: The height of the cliff gives your voice an authority that the flat ground would not — you are above them, you can see everything, and they cannot tell how many you are. This is an opportunity if played correctly.

resolutions.label: use the height to command them without descending

success.body:
You identify yourself clearly, state what you have observed, and give them a specific choice: the packet returned and they leave, or you note their faces and descriptions for the lord of this coast.

The packet is placed back beside the messenger. They go. You descend.

failure.body:
They call your bluff — they do not believe you are alone, and then they conclude you are, and the cliff's authority collapses. The descent is now required in worse circumstances.

The bluff was worth attempting.

entries.body:
The city's beggars have become organised — not brigands in the traditional sense, but a network of the dispossessed who take what they need with enough coordination to be effective and enough desperation to be dangerous. One of the Grail questors was stripped of her provisions this morning.

The leader of this network knows why the questors are here, and he has a position on the matter.

responses.label: You may find the leader and negotiate the return of the provisions.
You may give your own provisions to the questor and speak to the leader about the quest's nature.

entries.body: The leader is found by asking in the right places with the right manner — he is known in the district, and his knowledge of the questors is genuine. He took the provisions because he believes the quest is for the lords and not the people.

resolutions.label: negotiate honestly with his position rather than dismissing it

success.body:
You do not dismiss his position. You engage with it — the Grail's healing is not for lords only, and the questors who are truly worthy of the quest understand this. The negotiation produces both the return of the provisions and a more nuanced understanding on both sides.

The provisions are returned. The conversation is not over.

failure.body:
His position is held with a conviction that your negotiation cannot shift. The provisions are not returned through diplomacy.

A different approach, or a different day.

entries.body: Giving your own provisions is an action rather than an argument. The leader, who respects action over argument, will receive this differently than he received the questor's request for return.

resolutions.label: give what you have and speak of the quest with honesty

success.body:
The action precedes the speech, which is the correct order. He receives both — the provisions and the account of what the quest is truly for — with the attention of a man who has been given something real rather than an argument.

The questor's provisions are returned. The network is not changed, but he has been given something to think about.

failure.body:
The giving is received as a bribe rather than a gift, and the speech that follows is heard through that interpretation. His position does not shift.

Generosity requires context to be read as generosity.

entries.body:
The forest road has a reputation among the Grail questors as a place of testing through encounter — the brigands here have, it is said, a specific mandate: to take from those who are seeking for wrong reasons and leave untouched those seeking truly. They do not know they have this role.

They have stopped you at the forest's deep point.

responses.label: You may explain your purpose and let it be judged.
You may treat this as an ordinary brigand encounter and respond accordingly.

entries.body: Your purpose, stated plainly, is either sufficient or not. The brigands' leader is a man who has been living in this forest long enough to have developed a quality of judgment that the quest has, unknown to him, cultivated.

resolutions.label: state your purpose clearly and honestly

success.body:
He hears your purpose and stands aside. He does not explain why — his judgment is not a thing he could account for — but you pass through without incident, and the quality of the passing has something to it that an ordinary passage would not.

The road is different on the other side of them.

failure.body:
Your purpose, as stated, does not satisfy whatever criterion he is applying. He does not attack — he simply does not step aside, and the standoff continues until you find a different way to express what you are doing and why.

The truth, in its first form, was not the whole truth.

entries.body: An ordinary brigand encounter, treated as such, produces ordinary results — conflict, resolution through force or cunning, passage. The testing dimension is lost, but the road is gained.

resolutions.label: resolve the encounter on practical terms

success.body:
You resolve it — quickly, without unnecessary drama — and pass through. The brigands' leader watches you go with an expression that suggests he knows you chose the practical over the meaningful.

You have the road. You do not have what the road held.

failure.body:
The practical approach fails — they are better at practical encounters than you gave them credit for. You must fall back on something more honest.

The honest approach was available all along.

entries.body:
The mountain pass brigands are testing the questors specifically — they have been told to by a hermit who watches them from a ridge and whose theology involves the idea that the Grail requires tested recipients. His instruction to them is: take what the questors have that they do not need.

They have taken your extra provisions. Your horse they did not touch.

responses.label: You may speak with the hermit and question his theology.
You may continue without the provisions and see what the testing produces.

entries.body: The hermit is on the ridge, visible, watching. His theology has a certain coherence — the idea that stripping away the non-essential is preparation for the essential — but it is being applied through agents who do not understand the purpose.

resolutions.label: engage the hermit's theology and test its logic

success.body:
The theological debate produces, after an hour, a modified position: the hermit acknowledges that what the questors carry is not reliably distinguished into essential and non-essential by men who do not understand the quest. He instructs the brigands to return what they took.

The provisions come back. The hermit keeps watching.

failure.body:
His theology is more robust than you gave it credit for, and your arguments do not find the gap you needed. The provisions remain taken.

The continuing-without approach may have been the right one.

entries.body: Continuing without the provisions — accepting the test as the test it is and responding not by recovering what was taken but by proceeding without it — may be the answer the hermit's theology requires.

resolutions.label: accept the loss and proceed on whatever remains

success.body:
The pass, once through it, provides: a shepherd's family who share their meal without being asked, and the knowledge that what was taken was indeed not essential. The hermit's methodology was crude, his instinct was not wrong.

You arrive at the far side better than you left the near side.

failure.body:
The pass without the provisions is harder than you estimated, and you arrive at the far side more depleted than tested. The loss was a loss, not a lesson.

Sometimes a robbery is just a robbery.

entries.body:
The plains brigands in the Age of the Grail Quest have a particular character — several of them were questors who abandoned the quest and cannot go back, and their resentment of the quest and its pursurers has made them specifically dangerous to those still on it.

They have recognised you as a questor and their disposition reflects this.

responses.label: You may speak to the leader of what drove him from the quest.
You may simply defend yourself and pass.

entries.body: A man who abandoned something and is angry about the abandoning is a man still in a relationship with the abandoned thing. His anger at the quest is the anger of something unresolved.

resolutions.label: speak with him about the quest and what he left

success.body:
The conversation goes somewhere neither of you expected. His abandonment was not failure of will but an encounter with something he was not equipped for — not a disqualification from the quest but a signal that a different preparation was required. He has been here while that preparation happened.

He steps aside. He says he will return to the road when he is ready.

failure.body:
The wound is too fresh for conversation — he hears engagement as condescension from someone still on the road he left. The conversation closes before it can open.

The defence is the remaining option.

entries.body: A man who abandoned the quest and is now defending his abandonment through action will fight harder than an ordinary brigand — there is a pride wound in it. The fight will be harder than the numbers suggest.

resolutions.label: fight through them and continue on the road

success.body:
The fight is harder than it should be, and cleaner than it could have been — you do not kill what does not need killing, and the leader respects this. He lets you through when the fight reaches its natural conclusion.

The road continues. He watches you go.

failure.body:
The pride wound makes them fight past wisdom, and the encounter goes further than you intended. You pass through, but the cost is higher than a practical brigand encounter would have been.

Emotional stakes change the arithmetic.

entries.body:
The swamp brigands are a holy test, according to the questors' current belief — a tradition has developed that the swamp has its own guards who purify those who pass through. The men in the reeds have heard of this and are exploiting it: they take provisions from questors who hand them over willingly, believing it is the test.

You are not sure whether to believe it or not.

responses.label: You may give them what they ask and accept the uncertainty.
You may refuse and see what the refusal produces.

entries.body: Giving in uncertainty is a form of faith — the possibility that this is a genuine test is real enough to act on, even if the possibility that it is exploitation is equally real. What you give is given as a gift rather than a payment.

resolutions.label: give what is asked as an offering, without knowing if it is required

success.body:
The giving is received — by something, whether it is the men in the reeds or the swamp itself is not clear — and the passage through is strangely easy. The things that should slow you do not.

You arrive at the far side with less than you started with and more than enough.

failure.body:
The giving satisfies neither possibility — the genuine test, if it is one, requires something different, and the exploitation takes what it takes without consequence. You pass through diminished.

Uncertainty is not resolved by giving into it.

entries.body: Refusing is itself a test if this is a genuine testing situation, and it reveals exploitation if it is exploitation. Either way, refusal produces information.

resolutions.label: refuse and see what the refusal reveals

success.body:
The refusal produces: ordinary men asking for ordinary things, and when refused, departing. There was no holy test — only men in a swamp who heard a useful story. You pass through with your provisions.

The questors who gave theirs away will be told.

failure.body:
The refusal produces something else: the swamp itself becomes harder, as though the refusing was the wrong answer. You pass through but everything about the passage is difficult.

The genuine and the false are hard to distinguish in a swamp.

entries.body:
The sea brigands — pirates, in the honest word — have taken a questor's boat and left her on a coastal rock with the tide coming in. The questors' community is assembling a rescue, but the pirates are still in the bay.

The questor on the rock has perhaps two hours before the tide covers it. The pirates have the boat she needs.

responses.label: You may take a fisherman's boat and board the pirates directly.
You may negotiate with the pirates for the questor's boat from shore.

entries.body: The pirates in the bay are not expecting to be boarded — they are anchored, celebrating, and their watch is casual. A small fast boat and the element of surprise might reach them before they can respond.

resolutions.label: board the pirates and take back the questor's boat

success.body:
The surprise works. You are aboard their vessel before they have finished being surprised, and the questor's boat is alongside before they have finished being alarmed. You take both boats back to shore.

The questor is off the rock with forty minutes to spare.

failure.body:
Their watch is better than it appeared, and you are met boarding. The fight on the water goes badly, and you must retreat to the fisherman's boat.

Negotiation from shore is the remaining option.

entries.body: Pirates in a bay with a prize can be negotiated with — they took the boat for value, and value can be met with counter-value. The questor's safety has to move faster than the tide.

resolutions.label: negotiate the boat's return with whatever you can offer

success.body:
The pirates' leader wants something specific — passage out of the bay without challenge, which you can arrange by speaking to the harbour master ahead of their departure. The exchange is made. The boat is returned. The questor is off the rock.

The pirates leave. The harbour master is irritated but cooperative.

failure.body:
The negotiation takes longer than the tide allows, and while you are still speaking the questor is in water to her waist. She swims. She makes it. The negotiation becomes irrelevant.

She loses her boat. She does not lose her life. The relative values are clear.

entries.body:
The city brigands in this age are not criminals — they are people the war has made desperate, and the desperation is visible in how they operate: efficiently, without cruelty, taking only what they need and nothing more. A merchant was robbed this morning of grain that will feed the brigands' families for a week.

The merchant wants the grain back. The brigands' families will go hungry if it is returned.

responses.label: You may find the brigands and negotiate a solution that serves both parties.
You may speak with the lord of this city about the situation these people are in.

entries.body: A solution that serves both parties requires understanding what the merchant actually needs versus what he wants. He wants the grain returned; he needs compensation for the loss. These are potentially separable.

resolutions.label: negotiate a solution that addresses the merchant's real need and the brigands' desperation

success.body:
The merchant's real need is financial compensation rather than the grain itself. You find a lord's restitution fund — small, but sufficient — and arrange a payment that satisfies the merchant without requiring the grain to be returned. The brigands keep the grain. The merchant is compensated.

Neither party is happy. Both parties can continue.

failure.body:
The merchant's position is inflexible — he wants the grain and only the grain — and the brigands cannot return it. The standoff is unresolvable through negotiation.

The lord's intervention is the remaining option.

entries.body: The lord of this city has a responsibility to those who are going hungry within his walls. The war does not remove that responsibility; it makes it more urgent. A lord informed of a specific, solvable problem is sometimes a lord who solves it.

resolutions.label: bring the situation to the lord's attention with specific solutions proposed

success.body:
The lord is a man who has been looking for something specific to do in a situation that has defeated everything general. You give him a specific problem and two specific solutions. He chooses the second one.

The brigands are given temporary work in the city's relief efforts. The grain situation resolves itself in a week.

failure.body:
The lord has too many such situations and too few resources. He acknowledges the problem and cannot address it today. The situation remains.

The problem was not wrong. The timing was not right.

entries.body:
The forest outlaws are soldiers from the war who have been declared traitors — but the declaration was made by a lord who was himself betraying Arthur, and the soldiers' act of desertion was an act of loyalty. The distinction matters, but not to the men currently hunting them.

You have found the outlaws before the hunters have. They ask for help.

responses.label: You may help them evade the hunters.
You may speak with the hunters and argue the soldiers' case.

entries.body: Helping them evade requires knowing the forest well enough to create a false trail, or knowing the hunters well enough to predict their search pattern.

resolutions.label: help the soldiers disappear into the forest

success.body:
The false trail takes the hunters an hour north while the soldiers go south, and south leads to a ford that the hunters' horses cannot cross quickly. By the time they correct, the soldiers are three hours into country that will hide them.

You watch the hunters from a tree and feel no guilt.

failure.body:
The hunters are more experienced at this than you expected, and the false trail is read correctly within twenty minutes. The chase continues.

The soldiers know this forest better than the hunters. Let them lead themselves.

entries.body: The hunters' leader is a lord's man doing what lords' men do. The argument that the lord's declaration was itself traitorous is a complex one — the kind that takes longer to win than a forest chase allows, but that might produce something useful.

resolutions.label: make the case for the soldiers' loyalty and slow the hunt

success.body:
The hunters' leader is a man of enough conscience to hear an argument that involves the concept of higher loyalty. He does not stop the hunt — he cannot unilaterally cancel his orders — but he slows it by an hour while he considers.

An hour is enough.

failure.body:
The argument is sound but the hunter is not in a position to respond to it — he has orders and his job is the orders, not the orders' legitimacy. The hunt continues at full pace.

The evading approach must carry them now.

entries.body:
The mountain pass is held by men who were a legitimate garrison until their lord died at Camlann and their pay stopped. They are not brigands by nature — they are soldiers by training who are now taking from the road to survive. They know the difference.

Their leader receives you with the embarrassment of a man who understands exactly what he has become.

responses.label: You may offer them legitimate employment or a way back from the road.
You may pay the toll and leave them with the knowledge of what you know about their lord.

entries.body: Legitimate employment exists — Arthur's remaining forces are always short of trained men, and these men are trained. The journey south to where Arthur's people can receive them is possible.

resolutions.label: offer them the road back to legitimate service

success.body:
The leader receives the offer with the relief of a man who has been waiting for exactly this. He knows the road he is on. The men vote. Not all go — two have families in the mountain villages now — but seven ride south with a letter bearing your name.

The pass is open. The toll is ended.

failure.body:
The offer is genuine but the men do not trust it — they have been disappointed before by authorities who said one thing and did another. The offer is declined.

Trust, once broken, requires more than good intentions to rebuild.

entries.body: Their lord's lands were taken after Camlann by the winning faction — but there may be compensation available through the surviving steward's court, if the right petition is filed within the right timeframe. This is information. Information has value.

resolutions.label: give them this information and the means to act on it

success.body:
The information changes their situation in their own minds — from a group with no options to a group with one option, which is already better. The leader writes down the steward's name and the petition form you describe.

He lets you through the pass without payment. He says: that was worth more.

failure.body:
The information is correct but the window for the petition has already closed. You have given them hope and then taken it away with the timing. That is worse than nothing.

Accuracy requires timeliness.

entries.body:
The plains brigands in the Final Wars are former men-at-arms from both sides, united by shared loss and no longer distinguished by which side they served. They take from everyone equally and their leader has developed a philosophy of impartiality that he finds satisfying.

He has stopped you and wishes to explain his philosophy before taking your horse.

responses.label: You may hear his philosophy and engage with it seriously.
You may decline the philosophy and address the horse-taking directly.

entries.body: His philosophy is genuinely coherent — a post-loyalty pragmatism that argues, not without evidence, that all sides failed their people and therefore the obligation to sides has expired. It is the philosophy of a man who has been badly used by several ideals.

resolutions.label: engage his philosophy honestly and find what it is avoiding

success.body:
The philosophy is avoiding: the possibility that the failure of particular ideals does not invalidate the idea of obligation itself. You make this argument. He argues back. After twenty minutes he says he will take your silver instead of your horse.

You give him the silver. The philosophy is not settled. It has moved.

failure.body:
His philosophy is more entrenched than a single conversation can shift, and the engagement produces annoyance rather than movement. He takes your horse anyway, more quickly than he would have without the debate.

Arguing with conviction sometimes hardens it.

entries.body: The direct address: you are not giving up your horse, and here is why, and here is what the alternative is. His men are experienced fighters, but so are you, and you are asking him to assess the cost.

resolutions.label: refuse the horse-taking and make the cost of taking it clear

success.body:
He does the assessment — a knight prepared to fight for a horse is a knight who will make the taking expensive. The cost is not worth it when there are easier prospects.

He lets you go. His philosophy is unchanged. Your horse is yours.

failure.body:
He has done this calculation before with knights and his assessment is that the cost is acceptable. The fight is harder than it should be in its first minutes before the new calculation settles.

You keep the horse. The philosophy wins on the day's terms.

entries.body:
The swamp brigands have taken something they do not know they have: a wounded messenger who carries the last confirmed location of Arthur before Camlann, sealed in a case at his belt. The brigands are two hours north of you, deep in the swamp.

The messenger is alive. The information is irreplaceable.

responses.label: You may track the brigands through the swamp and recover both.
You may find the brigands' camp and negotiate from there.

entries.body: Tracking through a swamp requires reading water disturbance, broken reed, and the specific quality of mud pressed by boots. Two hours is enough time for the trail to fade, but not completely.

resolutions.label: track the brigands through the swamp

success.body:
The trail holds well enough in the wet ground, and you find their camp two hours later. The messenger is being held for ransom they haven't yet named. You recover both the man and the case before the ransom is demanded.

The location of Arthur is on its way north.

failure.body:
The trail goes cold at a wide water crossing — they crossed by boat, which leaves no surface track. The camp is out of reach by tracking alone.

Their camp must be found another way.

entries.body: The brigands' camp can be found by asking the swamp's other inhabitants — the fishing families who live in the swamp's margins know where everyone is. They will tell you if the asking is right.

resolutions.label: find the camp through the swamp people and recover the messenger

success.body:
A fishing family knows the brigands and their camp — they have had dealings before that were mutually satisfactory. For the information, you trade provisions and a promise of safe passage that you can make good on. The camp's location is given.

You reach the camp and negotiate the messenger's release. The case is undisturbed.

failure.body:
The fishing family knows but will not say — the brigands are their neighbours and the relationship is too important to trade for a stranger's promise. You leave without the location.

The tracking remains the only approach.

entries.body:
